<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define Operations Research. List any four characteristics of OR.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Operations Research (OR) is the application of scientific, mathematical, and analytical methods to study and optimize the behavior of complex systems, with the goal of providing a quantitative basis for managerial decision-making.

**Four Characteristics of OR:**

1. **Inter-disciplinary Approach:** OR teams consist of experts from mathematics, statistics, economics, engineering, and management sciences.
2. **Quantitative Orientation:** OR relies on mathematical models and numerical data rather than qualitative judgment.
3. **Optimization Focus:** The primary aim is to find the *best* (optimal) solution — either maximum profit or minimum cost.
4. **Scientific Methodology:** Uses hypothesis testing, validation, and iterative refinement rather than trial-and-error.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. Explain the difference between deterministic and probabilistic models in OR with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Deterministic Model | Probabilistic (Stochastic) Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameter Nature** | All input parameters are *known and fixed* | Input parameters are *random variables* |
| **Output Nature** | A single definite optimal solution | Distribution of possible outcomes |
| **Examples** | Linear Programming (LP), Assignment Model, Transportation | Queuing Theory, Game Theory, Inventory under uncertain demand |
| **Engineering Use** | Factory production planning | Hospital bed availability forecasting |

**Examples:**
- **Deterministic:** Solving an LP to decide weekly production of two products (all profits, costs, and resources are fixed numbers).
- **Probabilistic:** Analyzing the average waiting time in a bank where customer arrival rate follows a Poisson distribution.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Question Paper Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **six phases of the OR methodology** with a neat flowchart. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company manufactures two products X and Y. Product X gives a profit of ₹30 per unit and Y gives ₹40 per unit. Both products require processing on two machines M1 and M2. Product X needs 1 hour on M1 and 2 hours on M2. Product Y needs 2 hours on M1 and 1 hour on M2. Available machine hours are 80 on M1 and 70 on M2. **Formulate this as a Linear Programming Problem.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The six phases of OR are:

1. **Formulating the Problem:** This is the most critical step. The decision-maker defines the problem scope, identifies the objectives (e.g., maximize profit), recognizes decision variables, and lists all relevant constraints. A poorly formulated problem leads to a useless model.

2. **Constructing the Mathematical Model:** The real-world situation is converted into a mathematical structure using variables, an objective function, and constraints. For example, an LP model expresses the problem as linear equations and inequalities.

3. **Deriving a Solution:** Mathematical techniques such as the Simplex Method, Hungarian Method, or Calculus are applied to obtain the optimal solution from the model.

4. **Testing the Model and Solution:** The model is validated by feeding it historical/known data and checking if the outputs reasonably match observed reality. If not, the model is revised.

5. **Establishing Controls over the Solution:** Sensitivity analysis is performed to check how the optimal solution changes when parameters (e.g., costs, demands) change. This ensures robustness.

6. **Implementing the Solution:** The validated optimal solution is converted into actionable operating policies and put into practice. Continuous monitoring ensures the solution remains valid as conditions evolve.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Decision Variables:** Let $x_1$ = units of product X produced per week, and $x_2$ = units of product Y produced per week.

**Step 2 — Objective Function:** Total profit is to be maximized.

$$
Z_{\max} = 30 x_1 + 40 x_2
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

Machine M1 constraint (1 hour per X, 2 hours per Y, 80 available):

$$
x_1 + 2 x_2 \le 80
$$

Machine M2 constraint (2 hours per X, 1 hour per Y, 70 available):

$$
2 x_1 + x_2 \le 70
$$

**Step 4 — Non-Negativity:**

$$
x_1 \ge 0, \quad x_2 \ge 0
$$

**Complete LPP Formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } Z &= 30 x_1 + 40 x_2 \\
\text{Subject to } & x_1 + 2 x_2 \le 80 \\
& 2 x_1 + x_2 \le 70 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Discuss the **scope and limitations of Operations Research** in modern engineering decision-making. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A firm produces three products P1, P2, P3 using two raw materials R1 and R2. The data is given below. Formulate an LP to maximize profit.
>
> | Product | R1 (kg/unit) | R2 (kg/unit) | Profit (₹/unit) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | P1 | 2 | 3 | 40 |
> | P2 | 4 | 1 | 50 |
> | P3 | 1 | 2 | 30 |
> | **Total Available** | **60** | **40** | — |
>
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Scope of OR:**
- **Manufacturing:** Production planning, inventory control, quality control, and machine scheduling.
- **Finance:** Portfolio optimization, risk analysis, capital budgeting, and project valuation.
- **Marketing:** Advertising budget allocation, media mix, and sales territory design.
- **Defense:** Radar deployment, missile trajectory, and logistics planning.
- **Healthcare:** Hospital staffing, ambulance location, and waiting line management.
- **Transportation:** Vehicle routing, supply chain network design, and traffic flow.

**Limitations of OR:**
- **Magnitude of Computation:** Real problems involve thousands of variables; computational cost can be prohibitive.
- **Data Dependence:** OR requires accurate data; garbage-in, garbage-out applies brutally.
- **Human Factors:** OR cannot fully account for human emotions, political considerations, and intuition.
- **Implementation Gap:** Mathematical optima may be infeasible in real life due to non-quantifiable constraints.
- **Communication Barrier:** OR professionals must translate technical jargon to managers — failure leads to poor adoption.

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Decision Variables:**
Let $x_1, x_2, x_3$ = number of units of products P1, P2, P3 produced respectively.

**Step 2 — Objective Function:**

$$
Z_{\max} = 40 x_1 + 50 x_2 + 30 x_3
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

For R1 (raw material 1):

$$
2 x_1 + 4 x_2 + x_3 \le 60
$$

For R2 (raw material 2):

$$
3 x_1 + x_2 + 2 x_3 \le 40
$$

**Step 4 — Non-Negativity:**

$$
x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0
$$

**Final Formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } Z &= 40 x_1 + 50 x_2 + 30 x_3 \\
\text{Subject to } & 2 x_1 + 4 x_2 + x_3 \le 60 \\
& 3 x_1 + x_2 + 2 x_3 \le 40 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> - **Do NOT skip non-negativity constraints.** Marks are deducted heavily for missing $x_j \ge 0$.
> - **Units matter.** Always double-check that "per unit" coefficients match constraint totals. ₹/unit must be multiplied by "units produced."
> - **Objective function direction is critical.** A "maximize" problem solved as "minimize" (or vice versa) loses **all 7 marks** in part (b) of the LPP formulation question.
> - **Sign convention:** For a $\le$ constraint, the available resource is on the RHS. For a $\ge$ constraint, the requirement is on the RHS. Mixing them is the most common 1-mark deduction.
> - **Phase ordering matters:** In Q.A(a), phases must be listed in **correct sequence** — the examiner's answer key awards no partial credit for shuffled order.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Page Rapid Revision for KTU Exam Day**
>
> ✅ **OR** = scientific method for optimal decision-making; coined in 1937 by A.P. Rowe in the UK.
>
> ✅ **Other names:** Management Science, Decision Science, Industrial Engineering.
>
> ✅ **Six Phases of OR:** Formulate → Model → Solve → Test → Control → Implement (and loop back).
>
> ✅ **Mathematical model has 3 parts:** Decision variables + Objective function + Constraints (+ non-negativity).
>
> ✅ **Model Types:** Deterministic vs Probabilistic; Linear vs Non-Linear; Descriptive vs Prescriptive.
>
> ✅ **George B. Dantzig (1947)** invented the Simplex Method — a guaranteed KTU question.
>
> ✅ **Strong Duality Theorem:** $Z_{\text{Primal Max}} = W_{\text{Dual Min}}$ at optimality.
>
> ✅ **LP Formulation Template:** Define $x_j$, write $Z = \sum c_j x_j$, then constraints $\sum a_{ij} x_j \le b_i$, then $x_j \ge 0$.
>
> ✅ **Applications span** Production, Inventory, Transportation, Assignment, Queuing, Game Theory, CPM/PERT, Replacement.
>
> ✅ **Limitations:** Computation cost, data quality, human factors, implementation gap.
>
> ✅ **Magic number for the question paper:** Expect **one 14-mark formulation question** and **one 3-mark theory question** from this topic in every KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models and Modelling in OR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Models and Modelling in OR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Operations Research (OR): The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operations Research (OR)** is defined as the scientific method of providing executive departments with a **quantitative basis for decisions** regarding the operations under their control. It is an interdisciplinary, mathematical, and analytical approach used to find optimal or near-optimal solutions to complex decision-making problems.

The term **Operations Research** originated during **World War II (1940)** when British military scientists, including **A.P. Rowe**, developed mathematical models to optimize radar deployment, convoy routing, and anti-submarine warfare strategies. After the war, the techniques migrated into business, industry, and engineering.

**KTU Board-Standard Definition (Examination Verbatim):**
> *"Operations Research is the application of scientific methods, techniques, and tools to complex problems arising in the direction and management of large systems of men, machines, materials, and money in industry, business, government, and defense."*

## 1.2 Models and Modelling: The Heart of OR

> [!NOTE]
> A **Model** is a simplified, idealized representation of a real system, process, or problem, expressed in mathematical, logical, schematic, or physical form, used to analyze, predict, or optimize the behavior of that system under varying conditions.
>
> **Modelling** is the art and science of constructing such representations — converting a real-world decision problem into a structured mathematical or logical framework that can be analyzed systematically.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Architectural Blueprint of a Skyscraper** 🏢
>
> Imagine an architect designing a 50-storey building. Before pouring a single drop of concrete, the architect builds a **scale model**. Why?
> - To **test** structural integrity under earthquake loads
> - To **predict** wind resistance and shadow patterns
> - To **optimize** material costs and space utilization
> - To **communicate** the design to engineers and contractors
> - To **avoid costly mistakes** before they become disasters
>
> Similarly, in **Operations Research**, the **mathematical model** is our "blueprint" for a real-world problem (factory, supply chain, hospital, transport network). Instead of testing in the real world (which is expensive, slow, and risky), we build a **mathematical model** and experiment on it.

**Key takeaway:** Models are the **bridge** between the messy, complex real world and the clean, analyzable world of mathematics. Without models, OR would have no foundation.

## 1.4 Why is Modelling Critical for KTU Engineers?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU OR problem — whether it is a Linear Programming problem, a Transportation problem, a Queuing model, or a Game Theory payoff matrix — **starts with a model**. Your grade depends as much on **how you build the model** as on how you solve it.

**Standard OR Metrics Every Student Must Memorize:**

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Decision Variables | $x_j$ | Controllable quantities we choose |
| Objective Function | $Z$ (or $f$) | The goal to maximize/minimize |
| Constraints | $g_i(x) \leq b_i$ | Real-world restrictions |
| Parameters | $c_j, a_{ij}, b_i$ | Known/estimated constants |
| Optimum Value | $Z^*$ | Best achievable objective |

---

## 1.5 Characteristics of a Good OR Model

A model that earns full marks in KTU valuation has the following properties:

1. **Simplicity** — Captures the essence without unnecessary complexity.
2. **Validity** — Accurately represents the real system.
3. **Robustness** — Yields meaningful results even with approximate data.
4. **Sensitivity-Ready** — Allows testing of "what-if" scenarios.
5. **Computability** — Solvable with available mathematical/computational tools.
6. **Completeness** — Considers all relevant factors without distortion.

---

## 1.6 KTU 2024 Syllabus Learning Outcomes Mapped to This Topic

> [!NOTE]
> After studying this topic, the student must be able to:
> - **CO1:** Define and explain the scope, methodology, and phases of Operations Research.
> - **CO1:** Classify the different types of models used in OR with examples.
> - **CO1:** Discuss the principles, advantages, and limitations of mathematical modelling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Phase Methodology of OR (Board-Favourite Diagram Question)

Every OR study, regardless of complexity, passes through **six standard phases**. KTU examiners frequently test this as a 7-mark descriptive question.

### Phase 1: Formulating the Problem
- Observe the real system.
- Identify the **decision-maker**, **objectives**, **decision variables**, **constraints**, and **interrelationships**.
- Define the **scope** of the problem explicitly.

### Phase 2: Constructing the Model
- Convert the verbal/written problem into a **mathematical model**:
$$\text{Optimize } Z = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$$
$$\text{Subject to: } g_i(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \leq b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$
$$x_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

### Phase 3: Deriving a Solution
- Solve the model using classical mathematics, algorithmic methods (Simplex, Hungarian, etc.), or simulation.

### Phase 4: Testing the Model (Validation)
- Run the solution through historical data or test cases.
- Check if the model reproduces past behavior accurately.

### Phase 5: Establishing Controls (Sensitivity Analysis)
- Determine how the solution changes when parameters change.
- Identify the **shadow prices** and **allowable ranges**.

### Phase 6: Implementation
- Translate the mathematical solution into **operational policy**.
- This is often the **hardest phase** — human and organizational resistance is common.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *"Form Model Derive Test Control Implement"* → **F-M-D-T-C-I** → think of it as a circular flow: real world → model → real world.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Models in Operations Research (High-Yield Topic)

Models are broadly classified into **three primary categories** based on their representation:

### A. Iconic (Physical) Models
- **Definition:** Physical replicas of real objects, scaled in size.
- **Examples:** Aircraft wind-tunnel models, architectural models, globe.
- **Use:** When physical appearance matters (engineering design).
- **KTU Buzzword:** *"Replica in scaled form."*

### B. Analog Models
- **Definition:** Replace one property of the system with an analogous property from a different domain.
- **Examples:** Flowchart of a production process, organizational chart, signal-flow graph, temperature map.
- **Use:** Best for representing **dynamic** or **structural** relationships.
- **KTU Buzzword:** *"One property substituted for another."*

### C. Symbolic (Mathematical) Models — The OR Workhorse
- **Definition:** Uses mathematical symbols, equations, and logic to represent the system.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Deterministic Models** — All parameters known with certainty.
  - **Probabilistic (Stochastic) Models** — Involves randomness; uses probability theory.
  - **Static Models** — Single time period.
  - **Dynamic Models** — Time-dependent (e.g., dynamic programming).
- **Examples:** Linear Programming, Queuing Theory, Game Theory matrices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "which model is most commonly used in OR?", the answer is always **Symbolic/Mathematical Model**, specifically **Linear Programming** for deterministic and **Queuing Theory** for probabilistic cases.

---

## 2.3 Principles of Mathematical Modelling (KTU 7-Mark Question Bank)

| # | Principle | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identify the Problem** | Clearly state the decision to be made. |
| 2 | **Define Decision Variables** | Use $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ for controllable quantities. |
| 3 | **Build the Objective Function** | Linear in most KTU problems: $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \dots + c_n x_n$. |
| 4 | **State Constraints** | Real-world limits expressed as inequalities. |
| 5 | **Apply Non-Negativity** | $x_j \geq 0$ for physical quantities. |
| 6 | **Solve Using Appropriate Method** | Graphical, Simplex, etc. |
| 7 | **Validate & Interpret** | Does the solution make business sense? |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exam Day)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this table mentally. It is the spine of every Module 1 numerical.

| Concept | Mathematical Form | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| General Linear Model | $\max Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | Objective Function |
| Subject To (Constraints) | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i, \forall i$ | Resource Limits |
| Non-Negativity | $x_j \geq 0, \forall j$ | Physical Realizability |
| Slack Variable | $s_i = b_i - \sum a_{ij} x_j$ | Convert $\leq$ to $=$ |
| Surplus Variable | $e_i = \sum a_{ij} x_j - b_i$ | Convert $\geq$ to $=$ |
| Artificial Variable | $R_i$ (non-physical) | Start the Simplex process |
| Feasible Region | $\{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : Ax \leq b, x \geq 0\}$ | All valid solutions |
| Optimal Solution | $x^* = \arg\max f(x)$ | Best decision |
| Duality (LP) | Primal $\leftrightarrow$ Dual | Shadow prices, economic interpretation |

> [!TIP]
> **Watch Out:** Never use the vertical pipe $\vert$ inside any KTU table. Always use $\mid$ or $\vert$ for absolute values to prevent LaTeX/markdown table breaking.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where do OR models live in real engineering?**
> - **Manufacturing:** Production planning, inventory control (EOQ models).
> - **Logistics:** Vehicle routing, warehouse location (Transportation Models).
> - **Healthcare:** Hospital staffing, surgery scheduling (Queuing Models).
> - **Defense:** Radar placement, missile trajectories (Game Theory).
> - **Finance:** Portfolio optimization (LP, Markov chains).
> - **Telecommunications:** Call routing, network design (Graph theory, LP).
> - **Civil Engineering:** Project scheduling (CPM/PERT).

The **modelling approach** — the focus of Module 1 — is the universal entry point to all of these.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From a Real-World Verbal Problem to a Mathematical Model

Let us walk through the **modelling derivation** with a canonical KTU textbook problem so you can see the analytical matrix in action.

### Worked Verbal Problem

> *"A small furniture company manufactures two products: **Chairs (C)** and **Tables (T)**. Each Chair requires **2 hours of carpentry** and **1 hour of painting**. Each Table requires **1 hour of carpentry** and **3 hours of painting**. The company has at most **40 hours of carpentry** and **60 hours of painting** available per week. The profit is **₹50 per Chair** and **₹80 per Table**. How many of each should be produced to maximize profit?"*

---

### Step 1 — Identify and Tabulate the Data

| Resource | Chair (C) | Table (T) | Available |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carpentry (hr) | 2 | 1 | 40 |
| Painting (hr) | 1 | 3 | 60 |
| Profit (₹/unit) | 50 | 80 | — |

**Logic Row:** *Organize the raw data into a structured matrix. This is the foundational step before any algebra.*

---

### Step 2 — Define the Decision Variables

Let us define:
- $x_1$ = number of Chairs produced per week
- $x_2$ = number of Tables produced per week

**Logic Row:** *Decision variables are the controllable quantities whose values we must determine. The variable subscripts must be distinct.*

---

### Step 3 — Formulate the Objective Function

The total profit $Z$ is the sum of profits from chairs and tables:

$$Z = 50 x_1 + 80 x_2$$

Since the company wants to **maximize** profit, the objective becomes:

$$\max Z = 50 x_1 + 80 x_2$$

**Logic Row:** *The objective function is a linear combination of decision variables with coefficients given by per-unit profit (in this case ₹50 and ₹80). The "max" operator is set because the verbal problem says "maximize profit."*

---

### Step 4 — Formulate the Constraints

**Carpentry constraint:** Each Chair uses 2 hours, each Table uses 1 hour, total available is 40 hours:

$$2 x_1 + 1 x_2 \leq 40$$

**Painting constraint:** Each Chair uses 1 hour, each Table uses 3 hours, total available is 60 hours:

$$1 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 60$$

**Logic Row:** *Each real-world limitation becomes a linear inequality. The coefficients come directly from the resource-use column of the data table; the right-hand side comes from the "Available" column.*

---

### Step 5 — Apply Non-Negativity Constraints

Physical production cannot be negative:

$$x_1 \geq 0, \quad x_2 \geq 0$$

**Logic Row:** *This step is non-negotiable in KTU papers. Forgetting $x_j \geq 0$ loses 1–2 marks.*

---

### Step 6 — Assemble the Complete Mathematical Model

Putting it all together, the **Linear Programming (LP) model** is:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\max Z &= 50 x_1 + 80 x_2 \\
\text{subject to: } \quad & 2 x_1 + x_2 \leq 40 \\
& x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 60 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}}$$

---

### Step 7 — Solve (Graphical Method — Preview for Module 2)

For completeness, find the corner points of the feasible region:

**Corner point A (origin):** $(0, 0) \Rightarrow Z = 0$

**Corner point B (Carpentry axis intercept):** $2 x_1 + x_2 = 40$ with $x_2 = 0$ gives $x_1 = 20$, so $B = (20, 0)$.
$$Z_B = 50(20) + 80(0) = 1000$$

**Corner point C (Painting axis intercept):** $x_1 + 3 x_2 = 60$ with $x_1 = 0$ gives $x_2 = 20$, so $C = (0, 20)$.
$$Z_C = 50(0) + 80(20) = 1600$$

**Corner point D (Intersection of constraints):** Solve:
$$\begin{aligned}
2 x_1 + x_2 &= 40 \\
x_1 + 3 x_2 &= 60
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply the second equation by 2:
$$\begin{aligned}
2 x_1 + 6 x_2 &= 120 \\
2 x_1 + x_2 &= 40
\end{aligned}$$

Subtracting:
$$5 x_2 = 80 \Rightarrow x_2 = 16$$

Substituting back:
$$2 x_1 + 16 = 40 \Rightarrow x_1 = 12$$

So $D = (12, 16)$.
$$Z_D = 50(12) + 80(16) = 600 + 1280 = 1880$$

**Optimum:** Compare all $Z$ values: $Z_A = 0, \ Z_B = 1000, \ Z_C = 1600, \ Z_D = 1880$.

Maximum is at $D$. Therefore:

$$x_1^* = 12 \text{ chairs}, \quad x_2^* = 16 \text{ tables}, \quad Z^* = \text{₹}1880$$

**Logic Row:** *The optimal solution always lies at a corner (extreme) point of the convex feasible polygon — this is the Fundamental Theorem of LP, and it is the basis of the Simplex Method in Module 2.*

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Types of OR Models at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is your **single-page revision tool** for the entire classification question.

| Model Type | Sub-Type | Example OR Technique | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Iconic** | Scale replica | Wind tunnel model | Physical appearance |
| **Analog** | Block diagram | Flowchart, signal-flow graph | One property ↔ another |
| **Symbolic – Deterministic** | Linear | Linear Programming | All parameters known |
| **Symbolic – Deterministic** | Non-Linear | Quadratic Programming | Curved relationships |
| **Symbolic – Probabilistic** | Stochastic | Queuing Theory | Involves randomness |
| **Symbolic – Probabilistic** | Stochastic | Inventory with uncertain demand | Lead-time variability |
| **Symbolic – Static** | Single period | Assignment, Transportation | No time element |
| **Symbolic – Dynamic** | Multi-period | Dynamic Programming | Sequential decisions |
| **Simulation** | Monte Carlo | Risk analysis | Imitates reality |
| **Heuristic** | Rule-based | Genetic Algorithms | Good enough, not optimal |
| **Game Theoretic** | Competitive | Two-player matrix games | Conflict / strategy |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Why Slack Variables Are Linear

For each $\leq$ constraint, define a slack variable $s_i \geq 0$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j + s_i = b_i, \quad s_i \geq 0$$

**Why introduce $s_i$?**
- It converts an inequality into an equation — required by the Simplex Method (Module 2).
- $s_i$ represents the **unused resource** in the original problem.
- For our furniture example: $s_1$ = unused carpentry hours; $s_2$ = unused painting hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students sometimes write $s_i \leq 0$ instead of $s_i \geq 0$. Slack variables **must** be non-negative. This loses 1 mark if wrong.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: The Dual of a Primal LP Model (Module-1 Theory Bonus)

For every **Primal LP**, there exists a **Dual LP** with a beautiful symmetry.

**Primal (Standard Form):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\max Z &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \\
\text{s.t.: } \quad & \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i, \quad i = 1, \dots, m \\
& x_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, n
\end{aligned}$$

**Dual:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\min W &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} b_i y_i \\
\text{s.t.: } \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_{ij} y_i \geq c_j, \quad j = 1, \dots, n \\
& y_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, m
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic Row:** *The dual variables $y_i$ are the shadow prices — they tell us the marginal worth of relaxing the $i$-th resource constraint by one unit. This is fundamental to economic interpretation and sensitivity analysis.*

**Duality Symmetry Rules (KTU High-Yield):**

| Primal | Dual |
|---|---|
| $\max$ | $\min$ |
| $\leq$ constraint | $y_i \geq 0$ |
| $\geq$ constraint | $y_i \leq 0$ |
| $=$ constraint | $y_i$ unrestricted |
| $n$ variables | $n$ constraints |
| $m$ constraints | $m$ variables |
| $c_j$ (objective coefs) | RHS of dual |
| $b_i$ (RHS) | Objective coefs of dual |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Phase OR Methodology (Block Flow)

This diagram is the **most-asked OR diagram** in KTU exams. Memorize its structure and arrows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Formulate the Problem] --> B[Phase 2: Construct the Model]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Derive a Solution]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Test the Model]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Establish Controls]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Implementation]
    F --> G[Real World Decision]
    G -.Feedback Loop.-> A
    D -.Validation Failure.-> B
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **forward arrows** (solid) represent the **normal progression**.
- The **feedback arrows** (dashed) represent **iteration**: if validation fails, return to modelling; if implementation reveals new issues, return to formulation.

---

## 4.2 Classification of OR Models (Hierarchical Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    O[OR Models] --> I[Iconic Physical Models]
    O --> AN[Analog Models]
    O --> SY[Symbolic Mathematical Models]
    
    I --> I1[Aircraft Models]
    I --> I2[Architectural Replicas]
    I --> I3[Engineering Prototypes]
    
    AN --> AN1[Flowcharts]
    AN --> AN2[Organizational Charts]
    AN --> AN3[Signal Flow Graphs]
    
    SY --> SY1[Deterministic]
    SY --> SY2[Probabilistic Stochastic]
    
    SY1 --> SY1A[Linear LP]
    SY1 --> SY1B[Non Linear NLP]
    SY1 --> SY1C[Integer Programming]
    
    SY2 --> SY2A[Queuing Theory]
    SY2 --> SY2B[Inventory with Random Demand]
    SY2 --> SY2C[Markov Chains]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Real World to LP Solution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real World Verbal Problem] --> B[Data Extraction Tabulation]
    B --> C[Identify Decision Variables]
    C --> D[Define Objective Function]
    D --> E[Formulate Constraints]
    E --> F[Apply Non Negativity]
    F --> G[Assemble LP Model]
    G --> H[Solve via Simplex or Graphical Method]
    H --> I[Validate Solution]
    I --> J[Implementation and Sensitivity Analysis]
    J --> K[Optimal Decision in Real World]
```

**Explanation:**
- Each block is a **distinct modelling step**.
- The output of one block becomes the **input** of the next — this is the canonical KTU modelling pipeline.
- KTU examiners test **two-step**: "What comes after Constraint Formulation?" → Answer: "Non-negativity, then model assembly."

---

## 4.4 Primal–Dual Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Primal LP Max Problem] -- Transpose A -- C[Constraints of Dual]
    C --> D[Dual LP Min Problem]
    D -- Optimality Theorem -- E[Same Optimum Z star equals W star]
    P -- Shadow Prices -- F[y i as marginal value of constraint i]
    F --> D
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The matrix of constraint coefficients $A$ is **transposed** to form the dual.
- At optimality, $Z^* = W^*$ (**Strong Duality Theorem**).
- Each $y_i$ is the **shadow price** of the $i$-th primal resource.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Operations Research. List any four characteristics of OR.**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

> **Definition:** Operations Research (OR) is the scientific method of providing executive departments with a quantitative basis for decisions regarding the operations under their control.
>
> **Characteristics of OR:**
> 1. **Interdisciplinary** — draws from mathematics, statistics, economics, and engineering.
> 2. **Scientific Approach** — uses mathematical models and logical analysis, not intuition.
> 3. **Optimization-oriented** — seeks the best (optimal) solution under constraints.
> 4. **Quantitative** — relies on numerical data and measurable variables.
> 5. **Systems Approach** — views the problem holistically rather than in isolation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Iconic and Symbolic models with examples.**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

| Feature | Iconic Model | Symbolic Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Physical replica | Mathematical/logical |
| **Representation** | Scaled-down object | Equations and symbols |
| **Ease of analysis** | Limited | High — solved analytically |
| **Example** | Aircraft wind-tunnel model, globe | Linear Programming equations, Queuing formulas |
| **Use in OR** | Rare | Universal |

> **Conclusion:** OR primarily relies on **symbolic models** because they are abstract, scalable, and computationally tractable.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**A manufacturing firm produces two products $P_1$ and $P_2$. Each unit of $P_1$ requires 2 hours of machine time and 1 hour of labour. Each unit of $P_2$ requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 hours of labour. The available machine hours are 100 and labour hours are 90 per week. The profit per unit is ₹30 for $P_1$ and ₹20 for $P_2$.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Formulate the problem as a Linear Programming model.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Solve the model graphically and find the optimal production mix and maximum profit.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Formulation

**Step 1: Decision Variables**
- $x_1$ = number of units of $P_1$ produced per week
- $x_2$ = number of units of $P_2$ produced per week

**Step 2: Objective Function**
$$\max Z = 30 x_1 + 20 x_2$$

**Step 3: Constraints**

Machine hours: $2 x_1 + x_2 \leq 100$

Labour hours: $x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 90$

Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Complete LP Model:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\max Z &= 30 x_1 + 20 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.: } \quad & 2 x_1 + x_2 \leq 100 \\
& x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 90 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Graphical Solution

**Corner Points of the Feasible Region:**

**Point A (origin):** $(0, 0) \Rightarrow Z_A = 0$

**Point B (Machine-axis intercept):** $2 x_1 = 100, x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow B = (50, 0)$
$$Z_B = 30(50) + 20(0) = 1500$$

**Point C (Labour-axis intercept):** $3 x_2 = 90, x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow C = (0, 30)$
$$Z_C = 30(0) + 20(30) = 600$$

**Point D (Intersection):** Solve:
$$\begin{aligned}
2 x_1 + x_2 &= 100 \\
x_1 + 3 x_2 &= 90
\end{aligned}$$

From the first equation: $x_2 = 100 - 2 x_1$. Substitute:
$$x_1 + 3(100 - 2 x_1) = 90 \Rightarrow x_1 + 300 - 6 x_1 = 90 \Rightarrow -5 x_1 = -210 \Rightarrow x_1 = 42$$

Then $x_2 = 100 - 2(42) = 16$. So $D = (42, 16)$.
$$Z_D = 30(42) + 20(16) = 1260 + 320 = 1580$$

**Optimum Comparison:** $Z_A = 0, \ Z_B = 1500, \ Z_C = 600, \ Z_D = 1580$.

$$\boxed{x_1^* = 42 \text{ units}, \quad x_2^* = 16 \text{ units}, \quad Z^* = \text{₹}1580}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the six phases of the OR methodology with a neat flowchart. State any three limitations of mathematical modelling.
**(b) [7 Marks]** A diet problem requires at least 8 units of vitamin A, 10 units of vitamin B, and 6 units of vitamin C per day. Two food types are available: Food $F_1$ (cost ₹5/unit, provides 2 units of A, 2 units of B, 1 unit of C) and Food $F_2$ (cost ₹4/unit, provides 1 unit of A, 3 units of B, 2 units of C). Formulate the LP model to minimize cost.

---

#### Part (a) — Six Phases + Limitations of Mathematical Modelling

**The Six Phases:**

1. **Formulating the Problem** — Identify objectives, decision variables, constraints.
2. **Constructing the Model** — Translate to mathematical equations.
3. **Deriving a Solution** — Solve using appropriate technique.
4. **Testing the Model** — Validate using historical/test data.
5. **Establishing Controls** — Sensitivity and what-if analysis.
6. **Implementation** — Apply the solution to the real system.

*(Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 for the schematic.)*

**Three Limitations of Mathematical Modelling:**

> [!WARNING]
> These three points are KTU examiner favourites — write them verbatim in the exam.

1. **Oversimplification** — Real-world problems involve subjective, qualitative, and human factors that mathematics cannot capture fully.
2. **Data Dependency** — Bad or inaccurate input data leads to misleading outputs (the classic *"Garbage In, Garbage Out"* problem).
3. **Time and Cost of Development** — Building and validating a robust mathematical model is expensive, time-consuming, and requires expertise that may not always be available.

*(Other valid points: difficulty in formulating non-quantifiable variables, the assumption of linearity, and inability to handle dynamic change.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Diet Problem Formulation

**Step 1: Decision Variables**
- $x_1$ = units of Food $F_1$ consumed daily
- $x_2$ = units of Food $F_2$ consumed daily

**Step 2: Objective — Minimize Cost**
$$\min Z = 5 x_1 + 4 x_2$$

**Step 3: Nutritional Constraints (at-least requirements → $\geq$)**

Vitamin A: $2 x_1 + x_2 \geq 8$

Vitamin B: $2 x_1 + 3 x_2 \geq 10$

Vitamin C: $x_1 + 2 x_2 \geq 6$

Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Complete LP Model:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\min Z &= 5 x_1 + 4 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.: } \quad & 2 x_1 + x_2 \geq 8 \\
& 2 x_1 + 3 x_2 \geq 10 \\
& x_1 + 2 x_2 \geq 6 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Non-Negativity (–1 mark):** Always explicitly write $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \geq 0$. Even if the question seems trivial, the examiner will deduct marks if omitted.
>
> 2. **Wrong Inequality Direction (–2 marks):** If the verbal problem says *"at most 40 hours"*, the constraint is $\leq$, not $\geq$. Read the wording carefully.
>
> 3. **Confusing Maximization with Minimization (–2 marks):** Words like *"maximize profit"* → $\max$. Words like *"minimize cost"* → $\min$.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up Primal–Dual Transformations (–3 marks):** The transpose of the constraint matrix is the dual coefficient matrix. Mark $\geq$ constraints map to dual variables $\leq 0$, not $\geq 0$.
>
> 5. **Not Enumerating All Corner Points (–2 marks):** For graphical LP, every corner point must have its $Z$ value computed. Missing one = missing the optimal.
>
> 6. **Listing Without Explanation (–1 mark per item):** If a question asks for "four characteristics of OR", a bare bullet list gets partial credit — always add a brief sentence explaining each.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for "Models and Modelling in OR":**

- **Definition of OR:** Scientific, quantitative, optimization-driven decision-making methodology.
- **Origin:** World War II (1940), British military; pioneers include A.P. Rowe, Patrick Blackett.
- **Six Phases (F-M-D-T-C-I):** Formulate → Model → Derive → Test → Control → Implement.
- **Three Model Types:** Iconic (physical), Analog (substitution), Symbolic (mathematical).
- **Symbolic models** are the most-used in OR and include Linear Programming, Non-Linear Programming, Dynamic Programming, Queuing Theory, etc.
- **Deterministic vs Stochastic:** Deterministic = all parameters known; Stochastic = randomness present (uses probability).
- **Five Characteristics of a Good Model:** Simplicity, Validity, Robustness, Sensitivity-ready, Computability.
- **Three Limitations of Modelling:** Oversimplification, Data dependency, High cost of development.
- **General LP Form:** $\max/\min Z = c^T x$, subject to $Ax \leq / \geq / = b$, $x \geq 0$.
- **Slack Variable $s_i \geq 0$** converts $\leq$ constraint to equality.
- **Surplus Variable $e_i \geq 0$** converts $\geq$ constraint to equality.
- **Artificial Variable $R_i$** is a non-physical starting point for Simplex.
- **Dual LP:** $\max$ ↔ $\min$, $A \to A^T$, $b \leftrightarrow c$, $\leq \leftrightarrow \geq$, $n \leftrightarrow m$.
- **Strong Duality Theorem:** At optimum, $Z^* = W^*$.
- **Graphical LP Optimum:** Always at a corner (extreme) point of the convex feasible region.
- **Worked Example Insight:** Furniture problem — $x_1^* = 12, x_2^* = 16, Z^* = ₹1880$.
- **Practice Drill:** Re-read the primal–dual symmetry rules table before every exam.

> **End of Topic Notes — Models and Modelling in OR**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Methods for Solving OR Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Methods for Solving OR Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Operations Research (OR)** is a scientific, analytical, and mathematical methodology that aids in the decision-making process by formulating real-world problems into well-structured mathematical models, deriving optimal (or near-optimal) solutions, and validating them under practical constraints.

The phrase **"General Methods for Solving OR Models"** refers to the **universal, model-agnostic procedural framework** — the systematic sequence of phases through which any Operations Research problem is approached, formulated, solved, and implemented. These methods provide the **intellectual scaffolding** that is independent of the specific OR technique (Linear Programming, Queuing Theory, Game Theory, etc.) being applied.

The six canonical phases of the OR methodology (as adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus) are:

1. **Phase I – Problem Definition and Data Collection**
2. **Phase II – Model Formulation (Mathematical Construction)**
3. **Phase III – Model Solution (Derivation of Solution)**
4. **Phase IV – Model Validation and Testing**
5. **Phase V – Sensitivity Analysis and Control**
6. **Phase VI – Implementation of the Solution**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT417 – Module 1):**
> Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, Module 1 establishes the *philosophical and procedural foundation* of OR. Unlike later modules that focus on specific algorithms (Simplex, Hungarian, etc.), this topic teaches the **meta-methodology** — *how to think about an OR problem before choosing any technique*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The GPS Analogy:**
> Imagine you want to drive from **Kochi to Delhi**. You don't just jump in the car and start driving. You:
> 1. **Define the destination** (Where exactly am I going?).
> 2. **Consult a map / set up a route** (Build a model of roads, distances, traffic).
> 3. **Compute the optimal path** (Algorithm finds shortest/fastest route).
> 4. **Simulate the trip mentally** (Does the route make sense? Toll roads? Night driving?).
> 5. **Adjust for weather, roadblocks** (Sensitivity analysis).
> 6. **Actually drive** (Implement).
>
> The **General Methods for Solving OR Models** are precisely this *roadmap-to-roadmap* philosophy. They do not tell you **which road to take** (that's specific techniques like Simplex), but they tell you **how to plan any journey systematically**.

**Real-world linkage:** Whether optimizing airline crew scheduling, hospital patient flow, or a Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) load distribution problem, the **six-phase methodology** is invariant.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Mandatory Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Decision Variables** | Quantities the decision-maker can control (e.g., $x_1$, $x_2$). |
| **Objective Function** | The mathematical expression representing the goal (maximize profit, minimize cost). |
| **Constraints** | Restrictions on the decision variables (resources, demand, capacity). |
| **Parameters** | Fixed constants in the model (e.g., profit per unit, resource availability). |
| **Feasible Region** | The set of all values of decision variables that satisfy all constraints. |
| **Optimal Solution** | The feasible solution that yields the best value of the objective function. |
| **Model Validation** | Verification that the model's outputs realistically represent the real system. |
| **Sensitivity Analysis** | Studying how the solution changes when parameters vary. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Phase General Methodology (Detailed)

### Phase I – Problem Definition and Data Collection
- Clearly identify the **decision-maker**, the **objectives**, and the **scope**.
- Collect **quantitative and qualitative data**: costs, demands, capacities, resource limits.
- Establish **performance measures** (KPIs) — what constitutes "good" or "bad" performance.
- Identify **assumptions** under which the problem is being studied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Many students skip directly to "maximize Z = ..." without explicitly defining variables. In Part A questions (3 marks), simply stating the **correct identification of decision variables** often earns full credit.

### Phase II – Model Formulation
This phase converts the verbal problem into a **mathematical programming structure**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } & Z = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \\
\text{Subject to } & g_i(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \leq b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& x_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

The model components:
- **Decision variables** ($x_j$)
- **Objective function** $Z = f(\cdot)$
- **Constraints** $g_i(\cdot)$
- **Non-negativity restrictions** $x_j \geq 0$

### Phase III – Model Solution
The model is solved using a suitable OR technique:
- **Analytical methods** (Calculus-based, closed-form)
- **Iterative methods** (Simplex, Hungarian, VAM)
- **Numerical/Heuristic methods** (Dynamic Programming, Simulation)
- **Graphical methods** (for ≤ 2 variables)

### Phase IV – Model Validation
Compare model outputs with **historical or observed data**. If deviations are large, refine the model. This is also called **verification and validation (V&V)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests:** "What is the difference between model validation and model verification?" Memorize this distinction — it is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

| Validation | Verification |
|---|---|
| *"Are we building the right model?"* | *"Are we building the model right?"* |
| Compares model to **real-world** behavior. | Checks **internal mathematical consistency**. |

### Phase V – Sensitivity Analysis & Establishing Controls
Determine how robust the solution is to changes in parameters (coefficients, RHS values). The **shadow prices** and **reduced costs** of linear programming are classic sensitivity outputs.

### Phase VI – Implementation
Translate the optimal mathematical solution into **operational decisions**. This involves:
- Communication with stakeholders
- Training operating personnel
- Establishing **monitoring mechanisms** to ensure the solution remains optimal as conditions change.

---

## 2.2 Classification of OR Models (For KTU Context)

| Model Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic Models** | All parameters known with certainty. | Linear Programming, Assignment |
| **Probabilistic / Stochastic Models** | Parameters are random variables. | Queuing Theory, Inventory with uncertain demand |
| **Static Models** | Decisions are made at a single point in time. | Single-period production |
| **Dynamic Models** | Decisions span multiple time periods. | Dynamic Programming |
| **Linear Models** | All relationships are linear. | LP, Transportation |
| **Non-Linear Models** | Non-linear objective or constraints. | Quadratic Programming |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| General LP Form | $\text{Optimize } Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | Maximize or Minimize |
| Functional Constraints | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i$ | For all $i = 1, \dots, m$ |
| Non-negativity | $x_j \geq 0$ | For all $j = 1, \dots, n$ |
| Feasible Region Definition | $\mathcal{F} = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \vert \mathbf{Ax} \leq \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{x} \geq 0 \}$ | Set-builder notation |
| Sensitivity Range (RHS) | $b_i^{L} \leq b_i \leq b_i^{U}$ | Right-hand side allowable range |
| Sensitivity Range (Objective) | $c_j^{L} \leq c_j \leq c_j^{U}$ | Objective coefficient range |
| Shadow Price | $\Delta Z / \Delta b_i$ | Marginal value per extra unit of resource $i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Never write $ \vert x \vert $ inside a markdown table. Always use **$\lvert x \rvert$** or **$\mid x \mid$** with the LaTeX escape to prevent table-rendering breakage.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Optimizing project scheduling (CPM/PERT) using the OR methodology.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Production planning, inventory control, and quality optimization.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Power dispatch optimization, network reliability.
- **Computer Science:** Algorithm design, network flow, AI search heuristics.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Workforce scheduling, supply chain, logistics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Demonstrative Model Formulation — A Complete Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern):**
> **Problem Statement (Kerala Coir Co-operative Scenario):**
> A small-scale coir unit produces two products, **Coir Yarn (Y)** and **Coir Mat (M)**. The unit has:
> - **3,000 hours** of skilled labour available per month.
> - **1,800 kg** of raw coconut husk available per month.
> - Each unit of **Y requires 2 hours** of labour and **1 kg** of husk; profit = **₹40**.
> - Each unit of **M requires 3 hours** of labour and **3 kg** of husk; profit = **₹80**.
> - Maximum market demand for Y is **1,000 units**; for M, it is **600 units**.
>
> Formulate the mathematical model.

---

### Step 1: Define Decision Variables

Let:
- $x_1$ = number of units of Coir Yarn (Y) produced per month.
- $x_2$ = number of units of Coir Mat (M) produced per month.

**Reasoning:** Decision variables are the levers controlled by the decision-maker. Here, the manager decides *how much* of each product to make.

---

### Step 2: Identify the Objective Function

The goal is to **maximize total profit**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= 40 x_1 + 80 x_2 \\
\text{where } Z &= \text{Total monthly profit in ₹.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Each Y gives ₹40, each M gives ₹80, so the total profit is the sum.

---

### Step 3: Identify and Express the Constraints

**(a) Labour constraint** (≤ 3,000 hours):

$$
2 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 3{,}000
$$

**(b) Raw husk constraint** (≤ 1,800 kg):

$$
1 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 1{,}800
$$

**(c) Market demand — Y** (≤ 1,000 units):

$$
x_1 \leq 1{,}000
$$

**(d) Market demand — M** (≤ 600 units):

$$
x_2 \leq 600
$$

**(e) Non-negativity restrictions:**

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Reasoning:** Constraints represent real-world limits. Each ≤ inequality encodes one physical or market restriction.

---

### Step 4: Assemble the Complete Mathematical Model

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } & Z = 40 x_1 + 80 x_2 \\
\text{Subject to: } & 2 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 3{,}000 \\
& x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 1{,}800 \\
& x_1 \leq 1{,}000 \\
& x_2 \leq 600 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Model Validation Logic

Once a candidate solution (e.g., from Simplex or a software solver) is obtained as $(x_1^*, x_2^*) = (900, 200)$, the validation phase proceeds as follows:

| Step | Check | Mathematical Verification |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Feasibility | Are all constraints satisfied? | Substitute $(900, 200)$ into each ≤. |
| 2. Optimality | Does no other feasible point yield a higher $Z$? | Compare $Z$ with adjacent corner points. |
| 3. Realism | Is the solution practically achievable? | $x_1 = 900 \leq 1000$ ✓; $x_2 = 200 \leq 600$ ✓. |
| 4. Plausibility | Do outputs match past data trends? | Compare with last month's production pattern. |

**Numerical Verification for $(x_1, x_2) = (900, 200)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= 40(900) + 80(200) = 36{,}000 + 16{,}000 = 52{,}000 \\
2(900) + 3(200) &= 1{,}800 + 600 = 2{,}400 \leq 3{,}000 \quad \checkmark \\
1(900) + 3(200) &= 900 + 600 = 1{,}500 \leq 1{,}800 \quad \checkmark \\
900 &\leq 1{,}000 \quad \checkmark \\
200 &\leq 600 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All constraints hold → the solution is **feasible**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — General Methods of Solving OR Models

| # | General Method | When Used | Example Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Analytical (Closed-form)** | Smooth, differentiable objectives; small number of variables. | Calculus, Lagrange Multipliers. |
| 2 | **Iterative (Algebraic)** | Linear models with multiple variables/constraints. | Simplex Method, Big-M, Two-Phase. |
| 3 | **Graphical (Geometric)** | 2 decision variables at most. | Corner-point enumeration, isoprofit lines. |
| 4 | **Transportation / Network** | Flow and assignment problems. | MODI, Hungarian, VAM. |
| 5 | **Dynamic / Recursive** | Sequential decisions, multistage. | Dynamic Programming, Bellman. |
| 6 | **Heuristic / Metaheuristic** | Complex, non-convex, large-scale. | Genetic Algorithms, Simulated Annealing. |
| 7 | **Simulation** | Systems with randomness/uncertainty. | Monte Carlo, Discrete-Event Simulation. |
| 8 | **Branch and Bound / Enumeration** | Integer/binary decisions. | Implicit enumeration, B&B. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Module 1:** The emphasis is on the **methodology** — the student must know *which class of method to invoke* for a given problem type, even though the deep mechanics of each technique are taught in later modules.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Phase OR Methodology — Master Flow Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the **canonical six-phase OR methodology** as a top-down process flow. Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `phaseA`), and labels contain no markdown formatting characters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phaseA["PHASE 1 PROBLEM DEFINITION AND DATA COLLECTION"]
    phaseB["PHASE 2 MATHEMATICAL MODEL FORMULATION"]
    phaseC["PHASE 3 MODEL SOLUTION DERIVATION"]
    phaseD["PHASE 4 MODEL VALIDATION AND TESTING"]
    phaseE["PHASE 5 SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS AND CONTROLS"]
    phaseF["PHASE 6 IMPLEMENTATION AND MONITORING"]

    phaseA -- Verbal problem becomes structured problem statement --> phaseB
    phaseB -- Decision variables, objective, constraints derived --> phaseC
    phaseC -- Optimal solution obtained --> phaseD
    phaseD -- Solution tested against real world --> phaseE
    phaseE -- Robustness and parameter variations studied --> phaseF
    phaseF -- Feedback loop to refine model --> phaseA
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Which Solution Method to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START WITH OR PROBLEM"]
    q1A{"IS THE MODEL LINEAR"}
    q1B{"NUMBER OF DECISION VARIABLES"}
    q1C{"ARE PARAMETERS KNOWN WITH CERTAINTY"}
    q1D{"ARE DECISIONS INTEGER BINARY"}
    rAnalytic["USE CALCULUS OR LAGRANGE METHOD"]
    rSimplex["USE SIMPLEX LP METHOD"]
    rGraphical["USE GRAPHICAL LP METHOD"]
    rTransp["USE TRANSPORTATION OR ASSIGNMENT"]
    rDP["USE DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING"]
    rQueue["USE QUEUING THEORY"]
    rInt["USE BRANCH AND BOUND OR CUTTING PLANE"]
    rSim["USE MONTE CARLO SIMULATION"]
    rHeur["USE GENETIC OR EVOLUTIONARY HEURISTIC"]

    startA --> q1A
    q1A -- YES --> q1B
    q1A -- NO --> rAnalytic
    q1B -- TWO OR LESS --> rGraphical
    q1B -- MORE THAN TWO --> q1C
    q1C -- DETERMINISTIC --> rSimplex
    q1C -- STOCHASTIC --> rQueue
    q1D -- YES --> rInt
    q1D -- NO --> rTransp
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Interaction of Model Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
    inpB["RESOURCE AVAILABILITIES"]
    varA["DECISION VARIABLES X1 X2 XN"]
    objA["OBJECTIVE FUNCTION Z"]
    conA["CONSTRAINT MATRIX A B"]
    feasA["FEASIBLE REGION F"]
    solA["OPTIMAL SOLUTION X STAR"]
    sensA["SENSITIVITY REPORT"]

    inpA --> objA
    inpB --> conA
    varA --> objA
    varA --> conA
    conA --> feasA
    objA --> feasA
    feasA --> solA
    solA --> sensA
    sensA -- Feeds back to --> inpA
```
