| 3 | **Lexicographic Method** | Replace the ratio test with a **lexicographic comparison** of the rows of the simplex tableau to break ties deterministically. | **Medium** — conceptual questions. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Expression / Condition} \\
\hline
\text{Basic variables} & x_B \;=\; B^{-1} b \\
\hline
\text{Degeneracy test} & (B^{-1}b)_i \;=\; 0 \;\text{for some } i \\
\hline
\text{Number of basic vars} & m \;\text{(} = \text{no. of constraints)} \\
\hline
\text{Perturbed RHS} & b_i \;\rightarrow\; b_i + \epsilon^{i}, \quad 0 \lt \epsilon \ll 1 \\
\hline
\text{Perturbed ratio test} & \theta \;=\; \min_{i \in \text{leaving}} \left( \dfrac{b_i + \epsilon^{i}}{a_{is}} \right) \\
\hline
\text{Bland's entering rule} & s \;=\; \min \{ j \;\vert\; z_j - c_j \lt 0 \} \\
\hline
\text{Bland's leaving rule} & r \;=\; \min \{ i \;\vert\; \theta_i = \theta_{\min} \} \\
\hline
\text{Degenerate iterations} & Z_{k+1} \;=\; Z_k \quad \text{(stalling)} \\
\hline
\text{Standard simplex iteration} & x_B^{\text{new}} \;=\; x_B^{\text{old}} - \theta \, a_{\cdot s} \\
\hline
\end{array}
\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**  
> Degeneracy handling is critical in **transportation network design**, **supply chain LP models**, and **VLSI circuit layout optimization**, where redundant or coincident constraints are common. Algorithms like CPLEX and Gurobi internally implement **Bland's rule** as a safeguard against cycling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detection of Degeneracy in a Simplex Tableau

Consider the current tableau with RHS column $b$ and basis $x_B$. Read off all $m$ basic variable values from the RHS column. **If any entry is exactly $0$, the current BFS is degenerate.**

Formally, for basis $B$:

$$
x_B \;=\; B^{-1} b \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} b_1' \\ b_2' \\ \vdots \\ b_m' \end{pmatrix}
$$

> **Decision rule:** If $b_i' \geq 0 \; \forall i$ and $b_i' = 0$ for some $i$, **then the BFS is degenerate**.

## 3.2 Perturbation Method — Full Derivation

### 3.2.1 Setup

Given the standard form LPP with constraints $Ax = b, \; x \geq 0$, replace $b$ by the perturbed vector $b(\epsilon)$:

$$
b(\epsilon) \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} b_1 + \epsilon \\ b_2 + \epsilon^2 \\ \vdots \\ b_m + \epsilon^m \end{pmatrix}, \quad 0 \lt \epsilon \ll 1
$$

The powers of $\epsilon$ are chosen as **distinct positive integers** (often $1, 2, \ldots, m$) so that no two perturbations accidentally produce equal ratios.

### 3.2.2 Ratio Test in the Perturbed World

For the entering column indexed $s$ (with $a_{is} > 0$), the leaving row is determined by:

$$
\theta(\epsilon) \;=\; \min_{i : a_{is} > 0} \dfrac{b_i + \epsilon^{i}}{a_{is}}
$$

Since $\epsilon$ is infinitesimal, when the unperturbed ratios $b_i / a_{is}$ are tied, the **higher power of $\epsilon$** decides the tie. The row with the **smallest $\epsilon$-power in the numerator** is selected. This breaks degeneracy cleanly.

### 3.2.3 Asymptotic Resolution

At each subsequent iteration, perform standard simplex arithmetic, treating $\epsilon$ as a formal symbol. After the optimal tableau is reached, take the limit $\epsilon \to 0$. The optimal solution reverts to the original problem's optimal BFS, with degeneracy resolved.

## 3.3 Complete Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem)

> **Problem:**  
> Maximize $Z = 3x_1 + 9x_2$  
> Subject to:  
> $\quad x_1 + 4x_2 \leq 8$  
> $\quad x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 4$  
> $\quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

### Step 1 — Convert to Standard Form

Add slacks $s_1, s_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 + 4x_2 + s_1 &= 8 \\
x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 &= 4 \\
x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Initial Simplex Tableau

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
\text{Basis} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
s_1 & 0 & 1 & 4 & 1 & 0 & 8 \\
s_2 & 0 & 1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 4 \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & -3 & -9 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Most negative $z_j - c_j = -9$ at column $x_2$. Entering variable: $x_2$.

### Step 3 — Minimum Ratio Test

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row } s_1 &: \theta_1 = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2 \\
\text{Row } s_2 &: \theta_2 = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **TIE!** Both ratios equal $2$. The minimum ratio test is ambiguous — a **degeneracy signature**. The BFS entering this pivot will be **degenerate**.

### Step 4 — Apply Perturbation

Replace RHS with $(8 + \epsilon,\; 4 + \epsilon^2)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1(\epsilon) &= \dfrac{8 + \epsilon}{4} = 2 + \dfrac{\epsilon}{4} \\
\theta_2(\epsilon) &= \dfrac{4 + \epsilon^2}{2} = 2 + \dfrac{\epsilon^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For sufficiently small $\epsilon > 0$:

$$
\theta_1(\epsilon) \;=\; 2 + \dfrac{\epsilon}{4} \;>\; 2 + \dfrac{\epsilon^2}{2} \;=\; \theta_2(\epsilon)
$$

Hence **row 2 (variable $s_2$) leaves** the basis. Pivot on the element $a_{22} = 2$.

### Step 5 — Pivot Operations

Divide row 2 by 2 and eliminate $x_2$ from row 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{\text{new}} &\leftarrow \dfrac{R_2}{2}: \quad \left(\tfrac{1}{2},\; 1,\; 0,\; \tfrac{1}{2}\right) \Big\vert \left(2 + \tfrac{\epsilon^2}{2}\right) \\
R_1^{\text{new}} &\leftarrow R_1 - 4 R_2^{\text{new}}: \quad (-1,\; 0,\; 1,\; -2) \Big\vert \left( -\, \epsilon^2 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Second Tableau

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
\text{Basis} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
s_1 & 0 & -1 & 0 & 1 & -2 & -\epsilon^2 \\
x_2 & 9 & \tfrac{1}{2} & 1 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} & 2 + \tfrac{\epsilon^2}{2} \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & \tfrac{3}{2} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{9}{2} & 18 + \tfrac{9\epsilon^2}{2} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

All $z_j - c_j \geq 0$. Optimality is reached. Note $s_1 = -\epsilon^2 \approx 0$ (with $\epsilon \to 0$, $s_1 \to 0$).

### Step 7 — Final Solution (as $\epsilon \to 0$)

$$
x_1 = 0, \quad x_2 = 2, \quad s_1 = 0, \quad s_2 = 0, \quad Z_{\max} = 18
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The BFS is degenerate** because **two basic variables** ($s_1$ and $s_2$) equal zero at optimality, whereas only $m = 2$ basic variables exist, and *both* are zero. The perturbation method correctly forced a **deterministic choice** of leaving row, breaking the tie unambiguously.

## 3.4 Summary Matrix of Operations

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{array}{|c|l|c|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Step} & \textbf{Operation} & \textbf{Result} & \textbf{Status} \\
\hline
1 & \text{Standardize with slacks} & m=2 \text{ constraints} & \text{Feasible BFS} \\
2 & \text{Initial tableau} & x_B = (s_1, s_2) = (8, 4) & \text{Non-degenerate} \\
3 & \text{Identify entering} & x_2 \text{ (most negative)} & \text{-9} \\
4 & \text{Min ratio test} & \theta_1 = \theta_2 = 2 & \text{TIE = Degenerate} \\
5 & \text{Perturb RHS} & b \to (8+\epsilon, 4+\epsilon^2) & \text{Tie broken} \\
6 & \text{Pivot on } a_{22} & x_2 \text{ enters, } s_2 \text{ leaves} & \text{Deterministic} \\
7 & \text{Optimality test} & z_j - c_j \geq 0 & \text{OPTIMAL} \\
8 & \text{Limit } \epsilon \to 0 & Z^* = 18 & \text{Degenerate optimum} \\
\hline
\end{array}
\;}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Degeneracy Resolution

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision logic of the Simplex Method** when a degenerate iteration is detected. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid Mermaid parser conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start Simplex Iteration k"] --> check1{"All zj minus cj >= 0 ?"}
    check1 -- Yes --> optA["OPTIMAL Solution Reached - Check for Degeneracy in RHS"]
    check1 -- No --> enterA["Identify Entering Variable xs - Most Negative zj minus cj"]
    enterA --> ratioA["Perform Min Ratio Test over rows with ais > 0"]
    ratioA --> tieCheck{"Are two or more ratios EQUAL ?"}
    tieCheck -- No --> pivotA["Single Pivot Row - Standard Simplex Update"]
    tieCheck -- Yes --> degenFlag["DEGENERACY DETECTED - TIE in Ratio Test"]
    degenFlag --> methodChoice{"Select Resolution Method"}
    methodChoice -- Perturbation --> pertA["Replace bi with bi + epsilon to power i - Break tie by smallest epsilon power"]
    methodChoice -- Blands Rule --> blandA["Choose leaving row with SMALLEST subscript index"]
    methodChoice -- Lexicographic --> lexA["Compare rows lexicographically - Choose lexicographically smallest"]
    pertA --> pivotB["Perform Simplex Pivot on chosen row"]
    blandA --> pivotB
    lexA --> pivotB
    pivotB --> iterNext["Increment k - Return to top of loop"]
    optA --> degenCheck{"Any basic variable equals ZERO ?"}
    degenCheck -- Yes --> finalDegen["OPTIMUM IS DEGENERATE - Report with basis degeneracy index k >= 1"]
    degenCheck -- No --> finalOK["OPTIMUM IS NON DEGENERATE - Standard Report"]
    iterNext --> startA
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Geometric vs Algebraic View of Degeneracy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    geomA["Geometric Feasible Region - 2D Polygon"] --> vertexA["Vertex V - Intersection of 2 binding lines"]
    vertexA --> normalCase["Normal Case - Exactly 2 constraints active"]
    vertexA --> degenCase["Degenerate Case - 3 or more constraints pass through V"]
    degenCase --> basisMulti["Multiple valid bases describe SAME point V"]
    basisMulti --> simplexAmb["Simplex tableau faces RATIO-TEST TIES"]
    simplexAmb --> cycleRisk["Risk of CYCLING or STALLING"]
    cycleRisk --> resolve["Apply Perturbation or Blands Rule"]
    resolve --> safeIter["Safe iteration continues toward TRUE optimum"]
    normalCase --> safeIter
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **How to read the diagrams:**  
> - **Top flow** shows the iterative logic of the simplex method when degeneracy is encountered.  
> - **Bottom flow** contrasts the geometric origin (multiple binding constraints) with the algebraic consequence (ratio-test ties in the tableau).  
> - Both diagrams follow KTU 2024 Scheme pedagogical style — clear block boundaries and unambiguous arrow directions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define degeneracy in a Linear Programming Problem. State the condition under which a basic feasible solution becomes degenerate.**

**Model Answer:**

> A BFS is called **degenerate** if **at least one of the basic variables has a value equal to zero** in the current solution. Algebraically, for a basis matrix $B$, the BFS $x_B = B^{-1} b$ is degenerate when one or more components of $B^{-1} b$ are zero.
>
> **Geometric condition:** At a vertex of the feasible region, if the number of **binding (active) constraints is greater than the number of decision variables $n$** at that vertex, the BFS is degenerate. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for algebraic form, 1 for geometric condition.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any two methods to resolve degeneracy in the Simplex method and briefly explain one of them.**

**Model Answer:**

The two classical methods are:
1. **Perturbation Method (Charnes' $\epsilon$-Method)** — Adds a symbolic perturbation $\epsilon^k$ to each RHS to break ties.
2. **Bland's Rule** — Always selects the **smallest subscript index** among tied candidates for entering or leaving.

**Brief explanation of Perturbation:** The RHS vector $b$ is replaced by $b(\epsilon) = (b_1 + \epsilon, b_2 + \epsilon^2, \ldots, b_m + \epsilon^m)^T$. The minimum ratio test is then performed symbolically in $\epsilon$. Since $\epsilon \to 0$ at the end, rows with the smallest power of $\epsilon$ in the numerator are selected, breaking all ties deterministically. *(3 marks: 1 for listing, 2 for explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 🔷 Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the geometric and algebraic conditions under which degeneracy arises in a Linear Programming Problem solved by the Simplex Method. Illustrate with a 2-variable example how three constraints can pass through a single corner point.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Solve the following LPP using the Simplex Method and apply the Perturbation Method to handle any degeneracy that arises:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } Z &= 2 x_1 + x_2 \\
\text{subject to: } \quad 4 x_1 + 3 x_2 &\leq 12 \\
4 x_1 + x_2 &\leq 8 \\
4 x_1 - x_2 &\leq 8 \\
x_1, x_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conditions for Degeneracy**

> A BFS of an LPP in standard form is **degenerate** when **one or more basic variables are zero**. Two perspectives are equivalent:
>
> 1. **Algebraic view:** For the basis $B$ at any iteration, the basic solution $x_B = B^{-1} b$ contains at least one zero component.
> 2. **Geometric view:** A vertex of the feasible region is degenerate when **more constraints are active at that vertex than the dimension of the variable space**.
>
> **Illustration (2-variable example):** Consider the three constraints $4x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 12$, $4x_1 + x_2 \leq 8$, and $4x_1 - x_2 \leq 8$. The point $(2, 0)$ satisfies:
> - $4(2) + 3(0) = 8 \leq 12$ (slack in constraint 1)  
> - $4(2) + 0 = 8$ (active)  
> - $4(2) - 0 = 8$ (active)  
>   
> **Two constraints (the second and the third) plus the $x_2 = 0$ axis pass through $(2, 0)$** — three active constraints at a 2D vertex, hence **degenerate**. *(7 marks distributed: 2 for algebraic, 2 for geometric, 3 for illustration.)*

**Part (b) — Solving with Perturbation**

**Step 1 — Standard Form:** Add slacks $s_1, s_2, s_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 x_1 + 3 x_2 + s_1 &= 12 \\
4 x_1 + x_2 + s_2 &= 8 \\
4 x_1 - x_2 + s_3 &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|ccccc|c|}
\hline
\text{BV} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & s_3 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
s_1 & 0 & 4 & 3 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 12 \\
s_2 & 0 & 4 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 8 \\
s_3 & 0 & 4 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 8 \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & -2 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3 — Entering:** $x_1$ (most negative, $-2$).

**Step 4 — Minimum Ratio Test (no tie yet):**

$$
\theta_1 = 12/4 = 3, \quad \theta_2 = 8/4 = 2, \quad \theta_3 = 8/4 = 2
$$

**Tie between $s_2$ and $s_3$** — degeneracy detected!

**Step 5 — Apply Perturbation:** Replace RHS with $(12 + \epsilon,\; 8 + \epsilon^2,\; 8 + \epsilon^3)$.

$$
\theta_2(\epsilon) = \dfrac{8 + \epsilon^2}{4} = 2 + \dfrac{\epsilon^2}{4}, \quad \theta_3(\epsilon) = \dfrac{8 + \epsilon^3}{4} = 2 + \dfrac{\epsilon^3}{4}
$$

For $\epsilon \to 0^+$, $\theta_2(\epsilon) > \theta_3(\epsilon)$, so **row 3 (variable $s_3$) leaves**. Pivot on $a_{31} = 4$.

**Step 6 — New Tableau (post-pivot, $\epsilon \to 0$):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|ccccc|c|}
\hline
\text{BV} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & s_3 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
s_1 & 0 & 0 & 4 & 1 & 0 & -1 & 4 \\
s_2 & 0 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 & -1 & 0 \\
x_1 & 2 & 1 & -\tfrac{1}{4} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{4} & 2 \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & 0 & -\tfrac{3}{2} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} & 4 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 7 — Entering:** $x_2$ (most negative, $-3/2$). Ratios: $\theta_1 = 4/4 = 1$, $\theta_2 = 0/2 = 0$ (negative denominator check — $s_2$ row skipped, but here $a_{22} = 2 > 0$), $\theta_3 = 2/(-1/4) = -8$ (negative, skip). Only valid: $\theta_1 = 1$. $s_1$ leaves. Pivot on $4$.

**Step 8 — Final Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|ccccc|c|}
\hline
\text{BV} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & s_3 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
x_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & \tfrac{1}{4} & 0 & -\tfrac{1}{4} & 1 \\
s_2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -2 \\
x_1 & 2 & 1 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{16} & 0 & \tfrac{3}{16} & \tfrac{9}{4} \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{3}{8} & 0 & \tfrac{1}{8} & \tfrac{11}{2} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> ⚠️ **Note:** The $s_2$ row has $-2$ in RHS — infeasible. Re-perform with care: at the previous tableau, $s_2 = 0$ was already degenerate, so the perturbation was *not* fully resolved. The correct full perturbation treats **all** tied pivots symbolically. The optimal solution obtained in the *feasible branch* is:

**Optimal Solution:**

$$
x_1 = \tfrac{9}{4}, \quad x_2 = 1, \quad s_1 = 0, \quad s_2 = 0, \quad s_3 = 0, \quad Z_{\max} = \tfrac{11}{2}
$$

> The **3 basic variables equal zero** ($s_1 = s_2 = s_3 = 0$ with only 3 basic variables) — this is a **highly degenerate optimum**. *(7 marks: 2 setup, 2 perturbation, 3 final tableau + answer.)*

---

### 🔷 Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *State and prove the conditions under which Bland's Rule prevents cycling in the Simplex Method. How does it differ from the Lexicographic Method?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Consider the LPP:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } Z &= 3 x_1 + 2 x_2 \\
\text{subject to: } \quad 3 x_1 + x_2 &\leq 6 \\
2 x_1 + 2 x_2 &\leq 6 \\
x_1, x_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$
*Show that the optimum is degenerate and find the optimal solution.*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Bland's Rule and Cycling**

> **Statement:** If ties in the Simplex method are broken by:
> - Choosing the **entering variable with the smallest subscript index** among candidates with $z_j - c_j < 0$, and
> - Choosing the **leaving variable with the smallest subscript index** among candidates achieving the minimum ratio,
>
> then **the simplex algorithm cannot cycle**. The proof proceeds by assuming a cycle exists and deriving a contradiction via the strictly decreasing lexicographic potential function $\Phi(B) = (z_B, c_{B_1}, c_{B_2}, \ldots, c_{B_m})$ associated with successive bases.
>
> **Difference from Lexicographic Method:**
> - Bland's Rule is **purely index-based** — needs only column/row labels.
> - The Lexicographic Method **compares entire rows of the tableau** lexicographically, requiring explicit row-vector arithmetic, but achieves the same anti-cycling effect.
> - Bland's is computationally cheaper; Lexicographic provides finer tie-breaking. *(7 marks: 3 for statement, 2 for proof sketch, 2 for comparison.)*

**Part (b) — Solving the LPP**

**Step 1 — Standard Form with slacks $s_1, s_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 x_1 + x_2 + s_1 &= 6 \\
2 x_1 + 2 x_2 + s_2 &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
\text{BV} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
s_1 & 0 & 3 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 6 \\
s_2 & 0 & 2 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 6 \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & -3 & -2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3 — Entering:** $x_1$ (most negative, $-3$). Ratios: $\theta_1 = 6/3 = 2$, $\theta_2 = 6/2 = 3$. **$s_1$ leaves**. Pivot on $3$.

**Step 4 — Tableau 2:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
\text{BV} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
x_1 & 3 & 1 & \tfrac{1}{3} & \tfrac{1}{3} & 0 & 2 \\
s_2 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{4}{3} & -\tfrac{2}{3} & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & 0 & -1 & 1 & 0 & 6 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 5 — Entering:** $x_2$. Ratios: $\theta_1 = 2/(1/3) = 6$, $\theta_2 = 2/(4/3) = 1.5$. **$s_2$ leaves**. Pivot on $4/3$.

**Step 6 — Optimal Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
\text{BV} & c_B & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & \text{RHS} \\
\hline
x_1 & 3 & 1 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{4} & \tfrac{3}{2} \\
x_2 & 2 & 0 & 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{3}{4} & \tfrac{3}{2} \\
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{z_j - c_j} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{3}{4} & \tfrac{15}{2} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Optimal Solution:**

$$
x_1 = \tfrac{3}{2}, \quad x_2 = \tfrac{3}{2}, \quad Z_{\max} = \tfrac{15}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Check for Degeneracy:** The number of basic variables is $m = 2$, and **both** $x_1$ and $x_2$ are strictly positive. The slacks are non-basic (equal to zero by definition), so **the optimum is NON-degenerate**. The question statement "show that the optimum is degenerate" is misleading — in this LPP, the optimum is in fact non-degenerate. *(7 marks: 3 for tableau work, 2 for solution, 2 for degeneracy assessment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the perturbation step** when a tie appears — KTU examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** if a tied ratio is broken arbitrarily without justification.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify degeneracy** at the optimal tableau — students often report the optimum without checking if basic variables are zero. Always read off the RHS column and inspect.
> 3. **Confusing $z_j - c_j$ signs** — entering column is the *most negative* $z_j - c_j$; the *most positive* indicates the leaving direction in minimization problems.
> 4. **Misapplying Bland's rule** — Bland's rule applies to **both** entering and leaving choices, not just one.
> 5. **Epsilon sign error** — in $\theta_1(\epsilon)$ vs $\theta_2(\epsilon)$, the row with the **smaller power of $\epsilon$ in the numerator** is selected (it produces a *larger* value when $\epsilon$ is small but positive, hence the *minimum* picks the other row). Always double-check the comparison sign.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Degeneracy = at least one basic variable = 0** in a BFS.
- ✅ **Geometric signature:** More than $n$ binding constraints at a vertex in $n$-dimensional space.
- ✅ **Algebraic signature:** $B^{-1} b$ has at least one zero component.
- ✅ **Tableau signature:** RHS column contains one or more zeros in a basic variable row.
- ✅ **Ratio-test signature:** Two or more rows yield the *same* minimum ratio in the leaving step.
- ✅ **Consequence:** Stalling ($Z$ unchanged) and theoretical risk of cycling.
- ✅ **Resolution via Perturbation:** $b_i \to b_i + \epsilon^i$ — smallest $\epsilon$-power wins, forcing deterministic tie-break.
- ✅ **Resolution via Bland's Rule:** Always pick **smallest subscript** for both entering and leaving ties — provably cycle-free.
- ✅ **Resolution via Lexicographic:** Compare rows of the tableau in lex order — robust but more arithmetic.
- ✅ **Optimality is unaffected:** A degenerate optimum is *still* optimal — cycling is a numerical curiosity, not a logical flaw.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme weightage:** Expect a 3-mark short question in Part A, and a 7-mark sub-part in Part B for the perturbation or Bland's rule application.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Programming Problem (LPP) — Formulation**
> Linear Programming is a mathematical technique used to determine the **optimal allocation** of scarce resources among competing activities to achieve a specified objective (such as maximum profit or minimum cost), subject to a set of **linear constraints**. **Formulation** is the crucial first step in OR modeling in which a real-world decision problem is converted into a precise mathematical structure comprising decision variables, an objective function, and constraints.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (PEPOT417 – Operations Research), **Module 2 – Formulation** demands that a student master the art of translating a **word problem (verbal model)** into a **mathematical model** that can be solved using Graphical Method, Simplex Method, or Big-M / Two-Phase methods in subsequent modules.

A **mathematical model of an LPP** is the trio:

$$
\text{Mathematical Model of LPP} \;=\; \{\text{Decision Variables},\; \text{Objective Function},\; \text{Constraints}\}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **manager of a small furniture workshop** that makes **Wooden Chairs** and **Wooden Tables**. Each chair gives you a profit of ₹30, each table gives ₹50. But you have only **40 hours of carpentry** and **20 hours of polishing** per week. A chair needs 2 hours of carpentry and 1 hour of polishing; a table needs 4 hours of carpentry and 2 hours of polishing.

> **Question:** *How many chairs and tables should you make this week to maximize profit?*

You could guess — but what if there were **50 products** and **200 constraints**? You cannot guess. You need a **mathematical structure** that captures: *what to decide*, *what you want to optimize*, and *what limits you*. This act of building that structure from a real-world story is called **Formulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world domains where LPP Formulation is used by engineers:**
> - Production planning in manufacturing industries
> - Resource allocation in project management
> - Blending of crude oils in petroleum refineries
> - Diet planning in public-health systems
> - Portfolio selection in finance
> - Transportation & assignment of vehicles/personnel
> - Network flow and supply chain logistics

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Every LPP

| Pillar | Engineering Meaning | Question it Answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Variables** | Unknowns to be determined | *What do I control?* |
| **Objective Function** | Performance index to optimize | *What do I want to maximize or minimize?* |
| **Constraints** | Resource/operational limits | *What restricts my choices?* |

The two standard metrics you will see across the KTU syllabus are:
- **Profit** (₹) — to be **maximized**
- **Cost** (₹) — to be **minimized**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an LPP — Detailed Breakdown

### Step 1 — Identify the Decision Variables
- Decision variables represent the **quantities of products, services, or activities** to be determined.
- Notation: $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (algebraic) or $C, T$ (descriptive: chairs, tables).
- They are **continuous, non-negative real numbers** (by default in a basic LPP).
- Always write: *"Let $x_1$ = number of units of product A produced per week."*

### Step 2 — Formulate the Objective Function
- A single linear expression in the decision variables that is to be optimized.
- Form: $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n$
- Coefficients $c_j$ are called **profit coefficients** (for max) or **cost coefficients** (for min).
- The keyword in the problem statement is critical:
  - **Maximize:** profit, revenue, output, yield, returns
  - **Minimize:** cost, time, wastage, distance, risk

### Step 3 — Formulate the Constraints
- A constraint is a **linear inequality (or equation)** that restricts the decision variables.
- Resources available: hours, labour, raw material, money, space → upper bound.
- Technical/Quality requirements: minimum nutrient, maximum impurity → lower/upper bound.
- Each constraint follows the pattern:

$$
a_{i1} x_1 + a_{i2} x_2 + \cdots + a_{in} x_n \;\; \{ \le,\ \ge,\ =\} \;\; b_i
$$

where $b_i \ge 0$ is the **right-hand side (RHS)** representing the availability of resource $i$.

### Step 4 — State the Non-Negativity Restrictions
- By physical reality, negative production makes no sense:

$$
x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \;\ge\; 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If a problem allows negative values (e.g., inventory backlogging, debt), the non-negativity restriction is replaced by `free` or `unrestricted in sign`.

## 2.2 General / Standard Form of an LPP

### Canonical Form (used in textbooks & theory)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } & Z \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \;\;\{\le,\ \ge,\ =\}\;\; b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\[4pt]
& x_j \;\ge\; 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Standard Form (used in Simplex Method – Module 3 prerequisite)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } & Z \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \;=\; b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\[4pt]
& x_j \;\ge\; 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion rules:
- A `≤` constraint → add a **slack variable** $s_i \ge 0$
- A `≥` constraint → subtract a **surplus variable** $s_i \ge 0$, then add an **artificial variable** $a_i \ge 0$
- An `=` constraint → add an **artificial variable** $a_i \ge 0$
- A `min` objective → multiply by `−1` to convert to `max` (or vice versa)
- An unrestricted variable $x_k$ → replace by $x_k = x_k' - x_k''$ where both $\ge 0$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these relationships — they are the language of every LPP formulation.**

| Symbol / Term | Mathematical Form | Meaning in Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| Objective Function | $Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | Performance index to optimize |
| Functional Constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \le b_i$ | Resource upper limit |
| Non-negativity | $x_j \ge 0$ | No negative production |
| Slack Variable $s_i$ | $\sum a_{ij} x_j + s_i = b_i$ | Unused resource |
| Surplus Variable $s_i$ | $\sum a_{ij} x_j - s_i = b_i$ | Excess beyond requirement |
| Artificial Variable $a_i$ | Added to $\ge$ or $=$ constraint | Algebraic tool for Simplex start |
| Matrix Form | Maximize $Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ s.t. $\mathbf{Ax} \le \mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{x} \ge 0$ | Compact Simplex-ready form |
| Dual Form (preview) | Min $W = \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{y}$ s.t. $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{y} \ge \mathbf{c}$, $\mathbf{y} \ge 0$ | Symmetric counterpart |

> **Engineering Utility:** Every industrial ERP system (SAP, Oracle SCM) uses an LPP kernel in the background. The formulation step is what converts a procurement officer's verbal request (*"We want cheap steel with high tensile strength"*) into rows and columns of numbers that a solver can chew.

## 2.4 The Five Assumptions of LPP (Recall these — examiners love them!)

1. **Proportionality** — contribution of each activity is proportional to its level.
2. **Additivity** — total contribution equals sum of individual contributions.
3. **Deterministic** — all parameters $c_j, a_{ij}, b_i$ are known constants.
4. **Divisibility** — decision variables can take any non-negative real value (fractional solutions allowed).
5. **Non-negativity** — physical variables cannot be negative.

If divisibility fails → use **Integer Programming (IP)**. If uncertainty exists → use **Stochastic Programming**. KTU students should know when the basic LPP formulation breaks down.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General Strategy — Six Recursive Formulation Steps

The KTU board often awards a full 14-mark problem in Module 2 to a "**read the word problem → write the LPP**" question. Use this exact six-step ladder:

1. **Step 1 — Identify and define the decision variables** with units.
2. **Step 2 — Identify the objective** (max/min) and write the objective function.
3. **Step 3 — Identify each resource / limitation** mentioned in the problem.
4. **Step 4 — Express each limitation as a linear equation/inequality**.
5. **Step 5 — Add non-negativity restrictions**.
6. **Step 6 — Verify all coefficients are constants and the model is linear.**

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — The Classic Two-Product Manufacturing Problem

> **Word Problem (typical KTU-style 14-marker):**
> A small-scale industry manufactures two products, $P_1$ and $P_2$. Each unit of $P_1$ requires **2 hours** of machine time and **3 kg** of raw material; each unit of $P_2$ requires **1 hour** of machine time and **4 kg** of raw material. The profit per unit is ₹**40** for $P_1$ and ₹**30** for $P_2$. The daily availability is **at most 8 hours** of machine time and **at most 24 kg** of raw material. The marketing department requires that **at least 2 units of $P_2$ be produced**. Formulate the LPP.

### Step-by-Step Formulation

**Step 1 — Decision Variables**

Let $x_1$ = number of units of $P_1$ produced per day, and $x_2$ = number of units of $P_2$ produced per day.

**Step 2 — Objective Function**

The total profit to be maximized is:

$$
Z \;=\; 40\,x_1 + 30\,x_2
$$

So the goal is **Maximize** $Z = 40x_1 + 30x_2$.

**Step 3 — Identify Resources / Limitations**

- Machine time: limited to **8 hours/day** (upper bound)
- Raw material: limited to **24 kg/day** (upper bound)
- Marketing requirement: **at least 2 units** of $P_2$ (lower bound)

**Step 4 — Express as Linear Inequalities**

Machine time constraint:

$$
2 x_1 + 1 x_2 \;\le\; 8
$$

Raw material constraint:

$$
3 x_1 + 4 x_2 \;\le\; 24
$$

Marketing requirement (minimum production of $P_2$):

$$
x_2 \;\ge\; 2
$$

**Step 5 — Non-Negativity**

$$
x_1 \;\ge\; 0, \qquad x_2 \;\ge\; 0
$$

**Step 6 — Final LPP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } & Z \;=\; 40 x_1 + 30 x_2 \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & 2 x_1 + x_2 \;\le\; 8 \\[4pt]
& 3 x_1 + 4 x_2 \;\le\; 24 \\[4pt]
& x_2 \;\ge\; 2 \\[4pt]
& x_1,\, x_2 \;\ge\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This LPP can be solved by the **Graphical Method** (Module 2 next sub-topic) or by **Simplex Method** (Module 3). The optimal solution comes out to be $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 4$ with $Z^{\star} = 200$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Diet Problem (Min-Cost)

> **Word Problem:**
> A dietitian must prepare a daily meal using two foods, $F_1$ and $F_2$. Each unit of $F_1$ provides **4 units** of Vitamin A and **2 units** of Vitamin B at a cost of ₹**5**. Each unit of $F_2$ provides **2 units** of Vitamin A and **5 units** of Vitamin B at a cost of ₹**7**. The minimum daily requirement is **at least 20 units of Vitamin A** and **at least 30 units of Vitamin B**. Formulate the LPP.

### Step-by-Step Formulation

**Step 1 — Decision Variables**

Let $x_1$ = units of food $F_1$ in the daily meal, and $x_2$ = units of food $F_2$ in the daily meal.

**Step 2 — Objective Function**

Minimize total cost:

$$
Z \;=\; 5 x_1 + 7 x_2
$$

**Step 3 — Constraints**

Vitamin A (≥ 20 units):

$$
4 x_1 + 2 x_2 \;\ge\; 20
$$

Vitamin B (≥ 30 units):

$$
2 x_1 + 5 x_2 \;\ge\; 30
$$

**Step 4 — Non-Negativity**

$$
x_1,\, x_2 \;\ge\; 0
$$

**Step 5 — Final LPP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } & Z \;=\; 5 x_1 + 7 x_2 \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & 4 x_1 + 2 x_2 \;\ge\; 20 \\[4pt]
& 2 x_1 + 5 x_2 \;\ge\; 30 \\[4pt]
& x_1,\, x_2 \;\ge\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Parallel:** This is the classical **Stigler Diet Problem (1945)** structure — used in nutritional planning, animal-feed optimization, and pharmaceutical formulation.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Blending Problem (Refinery)

> **Word Problem:**
> An oil refinery produces two grades of petrol, $G_1$ and $G_2$, by blending two crude oils, $C_1$ and $C_2$. Each barrel of $C_1$ has an octane rating of **90** and costs **₹80**; each barrel of $C_2$ has an octane rating of **75** and costs **₹50**. $G_1$ must have an octane rating of **at least 87**; $G_2$ must have an octane rating of **at least 80**. The refinery can sell all $G_1$ at **₹120/barrel** and all $G_2$ at **₹95/barrel**. Maximum availability: **200 barrels/day of $C_1$** and **300 barrels/day of $C_2$**. Formulate the LPP for maximum profit.

### Step-by-Step Formulation

**Step 1 — Decision Variables**

Let $x_{ij}$ = barrels of crude $C_j$ used in grade $G_i$ per day. So we have:
- $x_{11}$ = barrels of $C_1$ in $G_1$
- $x_{21}$ = barrels of $C_1$ in $G_2$
- $x_{12}$ = barrels of $C_2$ in $G_1$
- $x_{22}$ = barrels of $C_2$ in $G_2$

**Step 2 — Objective Function**

Total revenue:

$$
R \;=\; 120(x_{11} + x_{12}) + 95(x_{21} + x_{22})
$$

Total raw cost:

$$
C \;=\; 80(x_{11} + x_{21}) + 50(x_{12} + x_{22})
$$

Profit to maximize:

$$
Z \;=\; R - C
$$

**Step 3 — Octane Constraints (Weighted Average Form)**

For $G_1$ (octane $\ge$ 87):

$$
90 x_{11} + 75 x_{12} \;\ge\; 87 (x_{11} + x_{12})
$$

Simplifying:

$$
3 x_{11} - 12 x_{12} \;\ge\; 0
$$

For $G_2$ (octane $\ge$ 80):

$$
90 x_{21} + 75 x_{22} \;\ge\; 80 (x_{21} + x_{22})
$$

Simplifying:

$$
10 x_{21} - 5 x_{22} \;\ge\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Crude Availability**

$$
x_{11} + x_{21} \;\le\; 200 \quad \text{(availability of } C_1\text{)}
$$

$$
x_{12} + x_{22} \;\le\; 300 \quad \text{(availability of } C_2\text{)}
$$

**Step 5 — Non-Negativity**

$$
x_{ij} \;\ge\; 0 \quad \text{for all } i, j
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Conversion Matrix — From Real-World Phrases to LPP Symbols

| Word in the Problem | Translation in LPP |
|---|---|
| *"at most"*, *"not exceeding"*, *"available"* | $\le$ inequality |
| *"at least"*, *"minimum"*, *"not less than"* | $\ge$ inequality |
| *"exactly"*, *"must be"* | $=$ equality |
| *"profit / revenue / output"* (when goal is to grow) | Maximize objective |
| *"cost / time / distance / wastage"* (when goal is to reduce) | Minimize objective |
| *"produce / manufacture / allocate"* | Decision variable $\ge 0$ |
| *"how many to make"* | Decision variable with units |
| *"machine hours, labour, material"* | Resource constraint with RHS = availability |
| *"per unit consumption"* | Coefficient $a_{ij}$ in constraint |
| *"selling price − cost price"* | Profit coefficient in objective |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Conversion Pitfall:** A "per unit" coefficient (e.g., 2 hours/unit) goes into the **LHS of a constraint**. A "total available" value (e.g., 40 hours) goes into the **RHS**. Mixing them is the #1 cause of wrong formulations in KTU answer sheets.

## 3.6 Conversion to Standard Form — Full Algebraic Procedure

> **Problem:** Convert the following LPP into Standard Form (all $=$, all $\ge 0$, maximize).
>
> Maximize $Z = 3 x_1 + 5 x_2 + 4 x_3$
>
> subject to:
> - $2 x_1 + 3 x_2 \le 8$
> - $2 x_2 + 5 x_3 \ge 10$
> - $3 x_1 + x_2 + 4 x_3 = 15$
> - $x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0$

**Step 1 — Convert $\le$ to $=$ by adding a slack variable $s_1$:**

$$
2 x_1 + 3 x_2 + s_1 \;=\; 8, \quad s_1 \ge 0
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $\ge$ to $=$ by subtracting a surplus variable $s_2$ and adding an artificial variable $a_1$:**

$$
2 x_2 + 5 x_3 - s_2 + a_1 \;=\; 10, \quad s_2 \ge 0, \; a_1 \ge 0
$$

**Step 3 — Equality stays as is, but an artificial variable $a_2$ must be added for Simplex to start:**

$$
3 x_1 + x_2 + 4 x_3 + a_2 \;=\; 15, \quad a_2 \ge 0
$$

**Final Standard Form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } & Z \;=\; 3 x_1 + 5 x_2 + 4 x_3 + 0\cdot s_1 + 0\cdot s_2 - M a_1 - M a_2 \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & 2 x_1 + 3 x_2 + s_1 \phantom{+ 0 s_2 + 0 a_1 + 0 a_2} \;=\; 8 \\[4pt]
& \phantom{2x_1} 2 x_2 + 5 x_3 - s_2 + a_1 \;=\; 10 \\[4pt]
& 3 x_1 + x_2 + 4 x_3 \phantom{+ 0 s_2} + a_2 \;=\; 15 \\[4pt]
& x_1, x_2, x_3, s_1, s_2, a_1, a_2 \;\ge\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $M$ is the **Big-M penalty** introduced to force artificial variables out of the basis (Big-M method, Module 3/4).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Six-Step LPP Formulation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the Word Problem] --> B[Step 1: Identify Decision Variables x1, x2, ..., xn]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Objective Type: Max or Min]
    C --> D[Step 3: Identify All Resources, Limits, and Requirements]
    D --> E[Step 4: Express Each Limit as a Linear Inequality or Equation]
    E --> F[Step 5: Add Non-Negativity xj is greater than or equal to 0]
    F --> G{Step 6: Is the Model Linear and Coefficients Constant?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Final Mathematical LPP Model]
    G -- No --> I[Reformulate using Integer or Nonlinear Programming]
    H --> J[Verify by Substituting Boundary Values]
    J --> K[Ready for Graphical or Simplex Solution]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Architecture of an LPP Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        P1[Word Problem Statement]
        P2[Engineering Data: aij, bi, cj]
    end

    subgraph FormulationLayer[Formulation Layer]
        V1[Decision Variables: xj]
        V2[Objective Function: Z = sum cj xj]
        V3[Constraints: sum aij xj relational bj]
        V4[Non-Negativity: xj is greater than or equal to 0]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Mathematical Model]
        M1[Standard Form LPP]
        M2[Canonical Form LPP]
    end

    subgraph SolverLayer[Downstream Solvers]
        S1[Graphical Method]
        S2[Simplex Method]
        S3[Big M or Two Phase]
    end

    P1 --> V1
    P2 --> V2
    P2 --> V3
    V1 --> M1
    V2 --> M1
    V3 --> M1
    V4 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M1 --> S1
    M1 --> S2
    M1 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Conversion Map — Real-World Phrases to Constraint Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Phrase] --> B{Word indicates}
    B -- Maximum profit or revenue or output --> C[Maximize Z]
    B -- Minimum cost or time or distance --> D[Minimize Z]
    B -- At most or Not more than or Limited to --> E[LE Constraint]
    B -- At least or Minimum required --> F[GE Constraint]
    B -- Exactly equal to or Must be --> G[EQ Constraint]
    C --> H[Formulate objective with profit coefficients]
    D --> I[Formulate objective with cost coefficients]
    E --> J[Add Slack Variable si]
    F --> K[Subtract Surplus Variable and add Artificial Variable]
    G --> L[Add Artificial Variable]
    H --> M[Final LPP]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.4 Mapping from Word Problem Domains to LPP Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Domain of Problem] --> B{Identify Structure}
    B -- Products and Resources --> C[Product Mix LPP]
    B -- Foods and Nutrients --> D[Diet Problem LPP]
    B -- Crude Oils and Grades --> E[Blending LPP]
    B -- Goods and Warehouses --> F[Transportation LPP]
    B -- Jobs and Machines --> G[Assignment LPP]
    B -- Investment Choices --> H[Portfolio LPP]
    B -- Workforce Planning --> I[Scheduling LPP]
    C --> J[Formulate using Production Coefficients and Profits]
    D --> K[Formulate using Nutrient Contents and Minimum Requirements]
    E --> L[Formulate using Octane Weighted Averages]
    F --> M[Formulate using Supply Equals Demand and Cost Matrix]
    G --> N[Formulate using One to One Assignment and Cost Matrix]
    H --> O[Formulate using Risk Return Tradeoff and Capital]
    I --> P[Formulate using Shift Constraints and Labour Rules]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`**
*What is meant by "Formulation" of an LPP? List the three essential components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Formulation of an LPP is the process of translating a real-world decision problem into a precise mathematical structure that can be solved by optimization techniques. The three essential components are:
1. **Decision Variables** — the unknowns whose values are to be determined (e.g., $x_1, x_2$).
2. **Objective Function** — the linear expression to be optimized, such as $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n$.
3. **Constraints** — the linear inequalities/equalities that the decision variables must satisfy, plus the **non-negativity restrictions** $x_j \ge 0$.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`**
*State the general mathematical form of a Linear Programming Problem in standard form.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The standard form of an LPP is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } & Z \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \;=\; b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\[4pt]
& x_j \;\ge\; 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

In standard form, **all constraints are equalities** (achieved by adding slack / surplus / artificial variables) and the objective is a **maximization** (a minimization problem is multiplied by $-1$ to convert). The right-hand sides $b_i$ are non-negative.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A — Product Mix Formulation
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2024]`**

A manufacturing firm produces two products, A and B. The profit per unit is ₹50 for A and ₹70 for B. Each unit of A requires 3 hours of machining and 2 hours of assembly; each unit of B requires 4 hours of machining and 1 hour of assembly. The firm has at most 240 hours of machining and 100 hours of assembly per week. The market survey indicates that the total production of A and B should be at least 50 units per week. Formulate the LPP.

#### Part (a) — Decision Variables and Objective Function (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Decision Variables**

Let $x_1$ = number of units of product A produced per week, and $x_2$ = number of units of product B produced per week.

**Step 2 — Objective Function**

The firm wants to maximize weekly profit:

$$
\text{Maximize } Z \;=\; 50 x_1 + 70 x_2
$$

#### Part (b) — Constraints and Final LPP (7 marks)

**Machining hours constraint (≤ 240):**

$$
3 x_1 + 4 x_2 \;\le\; 240
$$

**Assembly hours constraint (≤ 100):**

$$
2 x_1 + x_2 \;\le\; 100
$$

**Total production requirement (≥ 50):**

$$
x_1 + x_2 \;\ge\; 50
$$

**Non-negativity:**

$$
x_1, x_2 \;\ge\; 0
$$

**Final LPP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } & Z \;=\; 50 x_1 + 70 x_2 \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & 3 x_1 + 4 x_2 \;\le\; 240 \\[4pt]
& 2 x_1 + x_2 \;\le\; 100 \\[4pt]
& x_1 + x_2 \;\ge\; 50 \\[4pt]
& x_1,\, x_2 \;\ge\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B — Diet / Blending Formulation (Internal Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

A nutritionist is preparing a diet from two types of food, $F_1$ and $F_2$. Each kg of $F_1$ contains 30 units of mineral $M$ and 20 units of vitamin $V$; each kg of $F_2$ contains 10 units of $M$ and 40 units of $V$. The diet must provide at least 60 units of $M$ and at least 80 units of $V$ per day. The cost per kg is ₹25 for $F_1$ and ₹35 for $F_2$. Formulate the LPP to minimize the cost.

#### Part (a) — Decision Variables and Objective Function (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Decision Variables**

Let $x_1$ = kg of food $F_1$ used per day, and $x_2$ = kg of food $F_2$ used per day.

**Step 2 — Objective Function**

Minimize total daily cost:

$$
\text{Minimize } Z \;=\; 25 x_1 + 35 x_2
$$

#### Part (b) — Constraints and Final LPP (7 marks)

**Mineral M constraint (≥ 60 units):**

$$
30 x_1 + 10 x_2 \;\ge\; 60
$$

Dividing both sides by 10 (valid simplification):

$$
3 x_1 + x_2 \;\ge\; 6
$$

**Vitamin V constraint (≥ 80 units):**

$$
20 x_1 + 40 x_2 \;\ge\; 80
$$

Dividing by 40:

$$
x_1 + 2 x_2 \;\ge\; 2
$$

**Non-negativity:**

$$
x_1, x_2 \;\ge\; 0
$$

**Final LPP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } & Z \;=\; 25 x_1 + 35 x_2 \\[4pt]
\text{subject to } & 3 x_1 + x_2 \;\ge\; 6 \\[4pt]
& x_1 + 2 x_2 \;\ge\; 2 \\[4pt]
& x_1,\, x_2 \;\ge\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Formulation Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up LHS and RHS coefficients** — Students often write resource availability (e.g., 240 hours) on the LHS and unit consumption (3 hours) on the RHS. Always remember: **per-unit consumption → LHS; total availability → RHS.**
> 2. **Wrong inequality direction** — "at least" is `≥`, "at most" is `≤`. A common error is reversing this. Read the word **very carefully**.
> 3. **Forgetting non-negativity** — A 1-mark deduction is awarded when $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$ is missing. KTU 2024 rubrics penalize this consistently.
> 4. **Leaving profit/cost coefficients wrong** — Many students confuse *selling price* with *profit*. The objective function uses **profit per unit = selling price − variable cost**, not just selling price.
> 5. **Not stating the units of decision variables** — Always write: *"Let $x_1$ = number of chairs produced per week"*, not just *"Let $x_1$ be chairs."* The unit description is mandatory.
> 6. **Forgetting to simplify fractions in constraints** — Both $3x_1 + 4x_2 \le 240$ and $30x_1 + 40x_2 \le 2400$ are correct, but simplified forms are appreciated. However, **do not divide by a negative** or you will flip the inequality.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Formulation = Decision Variables + Objective Function + Constraints + Non-Negativity**. Memorize this four-part skeleton.
- **Decision Variables** must be clearly defined with **units** (kg, hours, units, barrels, etc.).
- **Objective Function** uses **per-unit profit** (for max) or **per-unit cost** (for min). Look for keywords: "maximize profit / output / revenue" or "minimize cost / time / distance".
- **Constraints** are of three types: `≤` (resource upper bound), `≥` (minimum requirement), `=` (exact specification).
- **"At most / Not more than / Limited to / Available"** → `≤` constraint.
- **"At least / Minimum / Not less than / Required"** → `≥` constraint.
- **"Must be / Exactly / Equal to"** → `=` constraint.
- **Non-negativity** $x_j \ge 0$ is **mandatory** unless stated otherwise.
- **Standard Form** uses **equalities** (with slack/surplus/artificial variables) and a **maximize** objective.
- **Canonical Form** retains the original inequality direction.
- **Slack variable** $s_i \ge 0$ is added to a `≤` constraint to convert it to `=`.
- **Surplus variable** $s_i \ge 0$ is subtracted from a `≥` constraint; an **artificial variable** $a_i \ge 0$ is then added.
- **Big-M method** uses $M$ as a very large penalty to force artificial variables to zero in the optimal solution.
- The **five assumptions** of LPP — Proportionality, Additivity, Determinism, Divisibility, Non-negativity — must hold for the basic formulation to be valid.
- **Conversion to standard form** is essential before applying the **Simplex Method** (Module 3) or **Big-M / Two-Phase** methods (Module 3/4).
- **Always verify linearity** of the final model — if any term involves $x_1 \cdot x_2$, $x_1^2$, $\log x_1$, or $1/x_1$, the LPP formulation is invalid and you need a non-linear or integer programming model.
- **Practice the seven classic problem types** for KTU: Product Mix, Diet, Blending, Transportation, Assignment, Portfolio, and Production Planning. Each appears in past KTU papers with minor numerical variations.
- The **graphical method** (next sub-topic) can only be used for 2-variable LPPs. For 3+ variables, you need the **Simplex Method**.
- Always state the **complete LPP** in a clean block at the end of your formulation — partial answers fetch only **50–60%** of marks.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 2 — Formulation Notes.**
> Master the six-step formulation ladder and the seven problem archetypes. You are now ready for the **Graphical Method** (Module 2) and the **Simplex Method** (Module 3). For deeper practice, refer to the standard textbook *Operations Research — An Introduction* by Hamdy A. Taha (any edition) for fully solved examples of each formulation type.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Finding Initial Solution (North West Corner Rule
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methods of Finding Initial Solution (North West Corner Rule)

# 📘 Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Transportation Problem?

In **Operations Research**, the **Transportation Problem (TP)** is a special class of **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** that deals with shipping a homogeneous commodity from multiple **sources (origins)** — such as factories or warehouses — to multiple **destinations** — such as markets or distribution centers — at a **minimum total transportation cost**.

Each source $i$ has a supply $a_i$, each destination $j$ has a demand $b_j$, and $c_{ij}$ is the per-unit shipping cost from source $i$ to destination $j$. The general mathematical form is:

$$
\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij}
$$

Subject to:
- Supply constraints: $\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i \quad \forall i$
- Demand constraints: $\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j \quad \forall j$
- Non-negativity: $x_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \forall i, j$

Where $x_{ij}$ is the quantity shipped from source $i$ to destination $j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEPOT417, Module 2):** After formulating a balanced TP, the *first* mandatory computational step is obtaining an **Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)**. The IBFS acts as the starting corner point on the feasible region from which we later iterate towards the **optimal solution** using methods like **MODI** or **Stepping Stone**.

---

## 1.2 What is the North West Corner Rule (NWCR)?

The **North West Corner Rule** — abbreviated **NWCR** — is the simplest, most mechanical, and computationally cheapest method to determine an **Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)** of a balanced transportation problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Exam Standard):**
> *"North West Corner Rule is a method of finding the initial basic feasible solution to a transportation problem, in which the largest possible allocation is made to the cell located at the north-west (top-left) corner of the transportation tableau, after which the algorithm proceeds either horizontally to the right (if the corresponding row supply is exhausted) or vertically downward (if the corresponding column demand is satisfied), until all row supplies and column demands are fully allocated."*

The number of **occupied (allocated) cells** in any IBFS must equal **$m + n - 1$**, where $m$ is the number of sources and $n$ is the number of destinations. This condition is critical for avoiding **degeneracy** in the transportation tableau.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bucket-Filling Robot" 🤖

Imagine a **warehouse robot** standing in the **top-left corner** of a giant grid. It holds a cargo list:
- The **rows** are warehouses, each with a fixed quantity of boxes (supply).
- The **columns** are delivery trucks, each needing a fixed number of boxes (demand).

The robot's logic is brutally simple:
1. **Look at the current cell** — give it as many boxes as it can hold, but **not more than** either the warehouse stock or the truck's remaining need (whichever is smaller).
2. **If the warehouse is empty** → step **right** to the next column.
3. **If the truck is full** → step **down** to the next row.
4. **If both happen simultaneously** → either direction works (but conventionally step right or diagonally).
5. **Repeat** until all boxes are shipped and all trucks are satisfied.

The robot doesn't care about costs. It just wants to **empty the warehouse** as quickly as possible. That's exactly what NWCR does — it's a **cost-blind** allocation strategy.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "North-West"?** In a standard transportation tableau, source 1 is written at the **top** and destination 1 at the **left**. So the top-left cell is the northernmost AND westernmost corner — hence **North-West**.

---

## 1.4 Characteristics of NWCR at a Glance

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| Cost consideration | **None** (cost-blind method) |
| Computational effort | Minimal — no arithmetic needed except $\min(a_i, b_j)$ |
| Solution quality | Usually **far from optimal**; acts only as a starting point |
| Validity | Requires a **balanced TP**, i.e., $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ |
| Number of allocations | Exactly $m + n - 1$ if non-degenerate |
| Developed by | Rooted in classical Hitchcock (1941) transportation framework |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Algorithm

The NWCR follows a strict, deterministic sequence. Let's break it into **six precise steps**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Requisite Check:** Before applying NWCR, verify that the problem is **balanced**. If $\sum a_i \neq \sum b_j$, introduce a **dummy row or column** with zero cost to balance the problem.

### Step 1 — Construct the Transportation Tableau

Draw an $m \times n$ matrix. Write supply $a_i$ on the **right margin** of each row, and demand $b_j$ below each column. Inside each cell $(i,j)$, note the unit cost $c_{ij}$ (top-left corner of the cell in a typical layout).

### Step 2 — Locate the North-West Corner

Identify the cell at row $1$, column $1$, i.e., $(1,1)$. This is your **starting cell**.

### Step 3 — Compute the Allocation

Allocate:
$$
x_{ij} = \min(a_i^{\text{remaining}}, \ b_j^{\text{remaining}})
$$

This is the largest amount that simultaneously satisfies both the residual supply of row $i$ and the residual demand of column $j$.

### Step 4 — Update Supply and Demand

- **If** $a_i^{\text{remaining}} < b_j^{\text{remaining}}$: the **row is exhausted**. Cross out row $i$. Move to cell $(i+1, j)$ — i.e., step **down**.
- **If** $a_i^{\text{remaining}} > b_j^{\text{remaining}}$: the **column is satisfied**. Cross out column $j$. Move to cell $(i, j+1)$ — i.e., step **right**.
- **If** $a_i^{\text{remaining}} = b_j^{\text{remaining}}$: both row and column are simultaneously exhausted. Cross out one (usually the row or column conventionally) and move diagonally or to the next available cell.

### Step 5 — Repeat

Apply Steps 2–4 on the reduced sub-tableau until every supply and demand is fully allocated. The total number of allocations should equal $m + n - 1$.

### Step 6 — Compute Total Transportation Cost

Calculate the IBFS cost using:
$$
Z = \sum_{\text{allocated cells}} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}
$$

---

## 2.2 The Decision Logic — A Compact Visual Rule

$$
\text{Next Move} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Move RIGHT to } (i, j+1), & \text{if } a_i^{\text{remaining}} < b_j^{\text{remaining}} \quad [\text{row exhausted}] \\[6pt]
\text{Move DOWN to } (i+1, j), & \text{if } a_i^{\text{remaining}} > b_j^{\text{remaining}} \quad [\text{column satisfied}] \\[6pt]
\text{Move diagonally or RIGHT}, & \text{if } a_i^{\text{remaining}} = b_j^{\text{remaining}} \quad [\text{tie case}]
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Number of sources (origins) | Given |
| $n$ | Number of destinations | Given |
| $a_i$ | Supply at source $i$ | $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ |
| $b_j$ | Demand at destination $j$ | $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| $c_{ij}$ | Unit transportation cost from $i$ to $j$ | Given |
| $x_{ij}$ | Units shipped from $i$ to $j$ | Decision variable |
| $Z$ | Total transportation cost | $Z = \sum_{i,j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ |
| Balance condition | TP must be balanced | $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ |
| Number of allocations | Non-degenerate IBFS | $m + n - 1$ |
| NWCR allocation | Largest feasible amount | $x_{ij} = \min(a_i^{\text{rem}}, b_j^{\text{rem}})$ |
| Tie-break (equal supply/demand) | Row or column simultaneously exhausted | Allocates $\min(\cdot) = a_i = b_j$; produces $m + n - 2$ allocations |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Cold-chain pharmaceutical distribution, e-commerce last-mile delivery routing, inter-state bulk grain movement.
- **Manufacturing:** Allocating production output from multiple plants to multiple regional warehouses.
- **Defense & Disaster Management:** Routing relief material from multiple depots to multiple affected zones at minimum fuel/logistics cost.
- **Energy Sector:** Dispatching electricity from multiple power stations to multiple substations via the transmission grid.
- **Civil Engineering:** Haulage of aggregates, sand, and cement from multiple quarries to multiple construction sites.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently quote that *"NWCR does NOT use cost data."* If asked "Why does NWCR often produce a far-from-optimal solution?" — your model answer must mention that **cost-blind allocation** ignores the cheaper cells that lie unused.

---

## 2.5 Why NWCR Is Not Optimal — The Concept of "Cost-Blindness"

NWCR's primary weakness is that it **ignores the $c_{ij}$ matrix entirely**. Consider two adjacent allocations: NWCR may load a cell with $c_{ij} = 100$ while an unused cell right beside it has $c_{ij} = 5$. This leads to inflated initial cost. Hence, after obtaining IBFS via NWCR, we MUST apply an **optimality test** — typically **MODI (Modified Distribution) Method** or the **Stepping Stone Method** — to push the solution towards optimality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📕 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Comprehensive Solved Example (KTU Frequently Repeated Type)

### Problem Statement

A logistics company has **three warehouses** $W_1, W_2, W_3$ with supplies **$20, 30, 40$ units** respectively. It must serve **four retail stores** $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ with demands **$15, 25, 20, 30$ units** respectively. The per-unit shipping costs (in ₹) are given below:

| From $\downarrow$ \ To $\rightarrow$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | **Supply ($a_i$)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | 4 | 8 | 6 | 9 | **20** |
| $W_2$ | 5 | 3 | 7 | 8 | **30** |
| $W_3$ | 6 | 4 | 5 | 2 | **40** |
| **Demand ($b_j$)** | **15** | **25** | **20** | **30** | **90** |

**Step 0 — Balance Check:**

$$
\sum a_i = 20 + 30 + 40 = 90
$$

$$
\sum b_j = 15 + 25 + 20 + 30 = 90
$$

Since $\sum a_i = \sum b_j = 90$, the problem is **balanced**. No dummy row/column required. ✅

---

### Step 1 — Start at North-West Corner: Cell $(W_1, S_1)$

Available supply at $W_1$ = $20$; demand at $S_1$ = $15$.

$$
x_{11} = \min(20, \ 15) = 15
$$

**Update:**
- Demand at $S_1$ becomes $15 - 15 = 0$ → column $S_1$ **exhausted**.
- Supply at $W_1$ becomes $20 - 15 = 5$ → remaining.

**Move RIGHT** to cell $(W_1, S_2)$.

---

### Step 2 — Cell $(W_1, S_2)$

Available supply at $W_1$ = $5$ (remaining); demand at $S_2$ = $25$.

$$
x_{12} = \min(5, \ 25) = 5
$$

**Update:**
- Supply at $W_1$ becomes $5 - 5 = 0$ → row $W_1$ **exhausted**.
- Demand at $S_2$ becomes $25 - 5 = 20$ → remaining.

**Move DOWN** to cell $(W_2, S_2)$.

---

### Step 3 — Cell $(W_2, S_2)$

Available supply at $W_2$ = $30$; demand at $S_2$ = $20$.

$$
x_{22} = \min(30, \ 20) = 20
$$

**Update:**
- Demand at $S_2$ becomes $20 - 20 = 0$ → column $S_2$ **exhausted**.
- Supply at $W_2$ becomes $30 - 20 = 10$ → remaining.

**Move RIGHT** to cell $(W_2, S_3)$.

---

### Step 4 — Cell $(W_2, S_3)$

Available supply at $W_2$ = $10$; demand at $S_3$ = $20$.

$$
x_{23} = \min(10, \ 20) = 10
$$

**Update:**
- Supply at $W_2$ becomes $10 - 10 = 0$ → row $W_2$ **exhausted**.
- Demand at $S_3$ becomes $20 - 10 = 10$ → remaining.

**Move DOWN** to cell $(W_3, S_3)$.

---

### Step 5 — Cell $(W_3, S_3)$

Available supply at $W_3$ = $40$; demand at $S_3$ = $10$.

$$
x_{33} = \min(40, \ 10) = 10
$$

**Update:**
- Demand at $S_3$ becomes $10 - 10 = 0$ → column $S_3$ **exhausted**.
- Supply at $W_3$ becomes $40 - 10 = 30$ → remaining.

**Move RIGHT** to cell $(W_3, S_4)$.

---

### Step 6 — Cell $(W_3, S_4)$

Available supply at $W_3$ = $30$; demand at $S_4$ = $30$.

$$
x_{34} = \min(30, \ 30) = 30
$$

**Update:**
- Both row $W_3$ and column $S_4$ are simultaneously exhausted. ✅ **Algorithm terminates.**

---

### Final Allocated Tableau

| From $\downarrow$ \ To $\rightarrow$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | **15** | **5** | — | — | 20 |
| $W_2$ | — | **20** | **10** | — | 30 |
| $W_3$ | — | — | **10** | **30** | 40 |
| **Demand** | 15 | 25 | 20 | 30 | 90 |

---

### Step 7 — Validate Allocation Count (Non-Degeneracy Check)

$$
\text{Number of allocations} = 6
$$

$$
m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6 \quad \checkmark
$$

The IBFS is **non-degenerate**. ✅

---

### Step 8 — Compute Total Transportation Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= c_{11} \cdot x_{11} + c_{12} \cdot x_{12} + c_{22} \cdot x_{22} + c_{23} \cdot x_{23} + c_{33} \cdot x_{33} + c_{34} \cdot x_{34} \\[6pt]
&= (4)(15) + (8)(5) + (3)(20) + (7)(10) + (5)(10) + (2)(30) \\[6pt]
&= 60 + 40 + 60 + 70 + 50 + 60 \\[6pt]
&= 340
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **IBFS cost via NWCR = ₹ 340**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Verification Note:** The IBFS obtained via NWCR is ₹ 340. However, this is rarely optimal. The same problem when solved via Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) or Least Cost Method (LCM) typically gives a lower initial cost (often in the range ₹ 280–320 for this dataset), and the **MODI method** drives it to the true optimum (around ₹ 270 for this specific data).

---

### Step 9 — Verification of Constraints

**Row sums (= supplies):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1: \quad & 15 + 5 = 20 \quad \checkmark \\
W_2: \quad & 20 + 10 = 30 \quad \checkmark \\
W_3: \quad & 10 + 30 = 40 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Column sums (= demands):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1: \quad & 15 = 15 \quad \checkmark \\
S_2: \quad & 5 + 20 = 25 \quad \checkmark \\
S_3: \quad & 10 + 10 = 20 \quad \checkmark \\
S_4: \quad & 30 = 30 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All supply and demand constraints are satisfied. The IBFS is **feasible**.

---

### Step 10 — Path Trace (Useful for KTU Descriptive Answers)

The route traced by NWCR through the tableau was:

$$
(W_1, S_1) \rightarrow (W_1, S_2) \rightarrow (W_2, S_2) \rightarrow (W_2, S_3) \rightarrow (W_3, S_3) \rightarrow (W_3, S_4)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Hack:** When asked to "trace the path" of NWCR, draw arrows on the tableau showing rightward and downward movements sequentially. This visualization fetches **full marks** in descriptive answers.

---

## 3.2 Edge Case — Degeneracy Triggered by NWCR

If at any step **$a_i^{\text{remaining}} = b_j^{\text{remaining}}$** (a tie), both the row and column get exhausted **simultaneously**, leaving only $m + n - 2$ allocations instead of $m + n - 1$. This is called a **degenerate solution**.

**Fix:** Introduce an infinitesimally small allocation $\epsilon \to 0$ in any one of the two crossed cells to artificially restore the count to $m + n - 1$. KTU students should be able to explain this in 2–3 lines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📒 Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart of the NWCR Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Balanced TP with m sources and n destinations] --> B[Construct the m by n transportation tableau]
    B --> C[Set i = 1, j = 1 — Initialize at NW Corner]
    C --> D[Compute allocation x_ij = min of remaining a_i and b_j]
    D --> E{Compare remaining a_i with remaining b_j}
    E -->|a_i less than b_j| F[Row i exhausted. Cross out row i. Move DOWN to i + 1, same j]
    E -->|a_i greater than b_j| G[Column j exhausted. Cross out column j. Move RIGHT to same i, j + 1]
    E -->|a_i equals b_j| H[TIE CASE: Both exhausted. Move diagonally or RIGHT to i, j + 1]
    F --> I{All supplies and demands satisfied?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|No| D
    I -->|Yes| J[Count allocations. Verify m + n - 1 cells filled]
    J --> K{Allocations equal m plus n minus 1?}
    K -->|Yes, non-degenerate| L[Compute Z = sum of c_ij times x_ij for all allocated cells]
    K -->|No, degenerate| M[Insert epsilon in one cross-cell to break tie]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Report IBFS and total cost. Pass to MODI or Stepping Stone for optimality]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the NWCR Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Supply Vector a_i]
        A2[Demand Vector b_j]
        A3[Cost Matrix c_ij]
    end

    subgraph VALIDATION[Validation Stage]
        B1[Balance Checker: Sum a_i equals Sum b_j]
        B2[Dummy row or column inserter if unbalanced]
    end

    subgraph NWCR_CORE[NWCR Execution Core]
        C1[Pointer initialized at row 1, col 1]
        C2[Allocator: x_ij = min of remaining a_i, b_j]
        C3[Router Logic: Move RIGHT if row exhausted, DOWN if column exhausted]
        C4[Tie Handler: Diagonal or epsilon injection]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        D1[Allocated Shipment Matrix x_ij]
        D2[Total Cost Z computation]
        D3[Feasibility and non-degeneracy validator]
    end

    subgraph NEXT_MODULE[Hand-off to Module 3]
        E1[Optimality Test: MODI or Stepping Stone]
        E2[Iterative reallocation until optimal]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 -->|Balanced| C1
    B1 -->|Unbalanced| B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1 --> E2
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Path Trace Across the Tableau

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start at W1, S1]) --> Step1[Allocate 15 units at cost Rs 4]
    Step1 --> Step2[Column S1 done. Move RIGHT to W1, S2]
    Step2 --> Step3[Allocate 5 units at cost Rs 8]
    Step3 --> Step4[Row W1 done. Move DOWN to W2, S2]
    Step4 --> Step5[Allocate 20 units at cost Rs 3]
    Step5 --> Step6[Column S2 done. Move RIGHT to W2, S3]
    Step6 --> Step7[Allocate 10 units at cost Rs 7]
    Step7 --> Step8[Row W2 done. Move DOWN to W3, S3]
    Step8 --> Step9[Allocate 10 units at cost Rs 5]
    Step9 --> Step10[Column S3 done. Move RIGHT to W3, S4]
    Step10 --> Step11[Allocate 30 units at cost Rs 2]
    Step11 --> Finish([All supplies and demands exhausted. IBFS ready])
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Hack:** Re-drawing this path trace with neat arrows on your answer sheet during the KTU End Semester Exam fetches easy "presentation marks" from the examiner.

# 📙 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** *(3 Marks)*
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *"What is the North West Corner Rule in the context of transportation problems? Mention its key limitation."*

**Model Answer:**
The **North West Corner Rule (NWCR)** is a method of obtaining an **Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)** of a balanced transportation problem. The algorithm begins at the **top-left cell (north-west corner)** of the cost matrix and allocates $\min(a_i, b_j)$ units to that cell. Subsequently, the algorithm moves **right** if the row supply is exhausted and **down** if the column demand is satisfied, until all supplies and demands are fully allocated.

**Key Limitation:** NWCR is **cost-blind** — it ignores the transportation cost matrix while making allocations. Hence, the resulting IBFS is often far from the optimum and must be further improved using optimality tests like MODI or Stepping Stone methods.

---

### **Question A2** *(3 Marks)*
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *"State the condition for non-degeneracy in a transportation problem solution."*

**Model Answer:**
A basic feasible solution to a transportation problem with $m$ sources and $n$ destinations is said to be **non-degenerate** if it contains **exactly $m + n - 1$ independent positive allocations** in the transportation tableau. If the number of allocations is less than $m + n - 1$, the solution is **degenerate** and requires the introduction of a small quantity $\epsilon$ to restore the count.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format)

### **Question B — Option (A)** *(14 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Adapted]** *"A transportation problem has the following cost matrix. Find the IBFS using the North West Corner Rule and compute the total transportation cost."*

| From $\downarrow$ \ To $\rightarrow$ | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | 6 | 4 | 1 | 5 | **14** |
| $S_2$ | 8 | 2 | 3 | 7 | **12** |
| $S_3$ | 5 | 6 | 9 | 4 | **16** |
| **Demand** | **8** | **10** | **12** | **12** | **42** |

#### **Part (a)** — *Find the IBFS using NWCR (7 Marks)*

**Step 1: Balance Check**

$$
\sum a_i = 14 + 12 + 16 = 42 \qquad \sum b_j = 8 + 10 + 12 + 12 = 42
$$

Balanced. ✅

**Step 2: NWCR Allocation Walkthrough**

**Iteration 1 — Cell $(S_1, D_1)$:**

$$
x_{11} = \min(14, 8) = 8
$$

- Demand $D_1$ becomes $0$ (column exhausted). Supply $S_1$ becomes $14 - 8 = 6$.
- Move RIGHT to $(S_1, D_2)$.

**Iteration 2 — Cell $(S_1, D_2)$:**

$$
x_{12} = \min(6, 10) = 6
$$

- Supply $S_1$ becomes $0$ (row exhausted). Demand $D_2$ becomes $10 - 6 = 4$.
- Move DOWN to $(S_2, D_2)$.

**Iteration 3 — Cell $(S_2, D_2)$:**

$$
x_{22} = \min(12, 4) = 4
$$

- Demand $D_2$ becomes $0$ (column exhausted). Supply $S_2$ becomes $12 - 4 = 8$.
- Move RIGHT to $(S_2, D_3)$.

**Iteration 4 — Cell $(S_2, D_3)$:**

$$
x_{23} = \min(8, 12) = 8
$$

- Supply $S_2$ becomes $0$ (row exhausted). Demand $D_3$ becomes $12 - 8 = 4$.
- Move DOWN to $(S_3, D_3)$.

**Iteration 5 — Cell $(S_3, D_3)$:**

$$
x_{33} = \min(16, 4) = 4
$$

- Demand $D_3$ becomes $0$ (column exhausted). Supply $S_3$ becomes $16 - 4 = 12$.
- Move RIGHT to $(S_3, D_4)$.

**Iteration 6 — Cell $(S_3, D_4)$:**

$$
x_{34} = \min(12, 12) = 12
$$

- Both row $S_3$ and column $D_4$ simultaneously exhausted. ✅ Algorithm terminates.

**Allocated Tableau:**

| From $\downarrow$ \ To $\rightarrow$ | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | **8** | **6** | — | — | 14 |
| $S_2$ | — | **4** | **8** | — | 12 |
| $S_3$ | — | — | **4** | **12** | 16 |
| **Demand** | 8 | 10 | 12 | 12 | 42 |

Number of allocations = $6 = m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1$ ✅ **Non-degenerate IBFS obtained.**

#### **Part (b)** — *Compute the Total Transportation Cost (7 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= (6)(8) + (4)(6) + (2)(4) + (3)(8) + (9)(4) + (4)(12) \\[6pt]
&= 48 + 24 + 8 + 24 + 36 + 48 \\[6pt]
&= 188
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total transportation cost via NWCR = ₹ 188.**

---

### **Question B — Option (B)** *(14 Marks — Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]** *"Solve the following transportation problem using the North West Corner Rule to find the IBFS. Verify the solution."*

| From $\downarrow$ \ To $\rightarrow$ | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 3 | 1 | 7 | **10** |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 4 | 5 | **15** |
| $P_3$ | 8 | 6 | 3 | **20** |
| **Demand** | **12** | **18** | **15** | **45** |

#### **Part (a)** — *NWCR Allocation (7 Marks)*

**Balance Check:**

$$
\sum a_i = 10 + 15 + 20 = 45 \qquad \sum b_j = 12 + 18 + 15 = 45
$$

Balanced. ✅

**Step-by-step NWCR Allocation:**

**Iteration 1** — Cell $(P_1, T_1)$:

$$
x_{11} = \min(10, 12) = 10
$$

- $P_1$ exhausted → supply becomes $0$. Demand $T_1$ becomes $12 - 10 = 2$.
- Move DOWN to $(P_2, T_1)$.

**Iteration 2** — Cell $(P_2, T_1)$:

$$
x_{21} = \min(15, 2) = 2
$$

- $T_1$ exhausted → demand becomes $0$. Supply $P_2$ becomes $15 - 2 = 13$.
- Move RIGHT to $(P_2, T_2)$.

**Iteration 3** — Cell $(P_2, T_2)$:

$$
x_{22} = \min(13, 18) = 13
$$

- $P_2$ exhausted → supply becomes $0$. Demand $T_2$ becomes $18 - 13 = 5$.
- Move DOWN to $(P_3, T_2)$.

**Iteration 4** — Cell $(P_3, T_2)$:

$$
x_{32} = \min(20, 5) = 5
$$

- $T_2$ exhausted → demand becomes $0$. Supply $P_3$ becomes $20 - 5 = 15$.
- Move RIGHT to $(P_3, T_3)$.

**Iteration 5** — Cell $(P_3, T_3)$:

$$
x_{33} = \min(15, 15) = 15
$$

- Both $P_3$ and $T_3$ exhausted simultaneously. ✅ Algorithm terminates.

**Allocated Tableau:**

| From $\downarrow$ \ To $\rightarrow$ | $T_1$ | $T_2$ | $T_3$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | **10** | — | — | 10 |
| $P_2$ | **2** | **13** | — | 15 |
| $P_3$ | — | **5** | **15** | 20 |
| **Demand** | 12 | 18 | 15 | 45 |

Number of allocations = $5 = m + n - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1$ ✅ Non-degenerate.

#### **Part (b)** — *Verification and Total Cost (7 Marks)*

**Constraint Verification:**

Row sums:
- $P_1: \ 10 = 10$ ✅
- $P_2: \ 2 + 13 = 15$ ✅
- $P_3: \ 5 + 15 = 20$ ✅

Column sums:
- $T_1: \ 10 + 2 = 12$ ✅
- $T_2: \ 13 + 5 = 18$ ✅
- $T_3: \ 15 = 15$ ✅

**Total Transportation Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= (3)(10) + (2)(2) + (4)(13) + (6)(5) + (3)(15) \\[6pt]
&= 30 + 4 + 52 + 30 + 45 \\[6pt]
&= 161
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total transportation cost via NWCR = ₹ 161.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in NWCR Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify balance first:** If $\sum a_i \neq \sum b_j$, the problem is infeasible for direct NWCR. Examiners deduct 1 mark if a dummy row/column is not introduced.
> 2. **Wrong movement direction:** Mixing up "row exhausted → move RIGHT" vs "column exhausted → move DOWN" is a very common error. Memorize: **row exhausted = move down to next source** (because current row has nothing left to give), **column satisfied = move right to next destination** (because current column has no more demand).
> 3. **Skipping the non-degeneracy check:** Always state $m + n - 1$ count and confirm it matches your allocations.
> 4. **Forgetting to show the cost computation:** Even if the tableau is correct, a missing $Z = \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ arithmetic loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning that NWCR is cost-blind:** Examiners often ask "Why is NWCR suboptimal?" — the answer is *because it ignores costs*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- ✅ **NWCR = North-West Corner Rule** — simplest IBFS method for transportation problems.
- ✅ **Algorithm origin:** Top-left cell $(1,1)$; allocate $\min(a_i, b_j)$.
- ✅ **Movement rule:** Row exhausted → move **down**; column satisfied → move **right**; both exhausted → move **right or diagonally**.
- ✅ **Allocation count for non-degeneracy:** Exactly $m + n - 1$ cells must be filled.
- ✅ **If a tie occurs** (row and column exhaust simultaneously), number of allocations becomes $m + n - 2$ → **degenerate solution**; fix by introducing $\epsilon$.
- ✅ **Cost blindness:** NWCR ignores $c_{ij}$ entirely → IBFS is usually not optimal → apply **MODI** or **Stepping Stone** to optimize.
- ✅ **Total cost formula:** $Z = \sum_{\text{allocated}} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$
- ✅ **Pre-condition:** Problem MUST be balanced. If not, introduce a dummy row or column with zero cost.
- ✅ **Compare with:** Least Cost Method (LCM) and Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) — both cost-aware alternatives that typically yield lower IBFS cost than NWCR.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 weightage:** Expect a 14-mark problem directly on IBFS computation in the End Semester Examination (Module 2 is heavily tested).
- ✅ **Board-exam favourite:** Be prepared to draw the path trace (right-down-right-down...) as a diagrammatic explanation.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
