| **State probability** | $P_n=(1-\rho)\rho^n$ | $P_n=P_0\rho^n$ | $P_n=\binom{K}{n}(\lambda/\mu)^n P_0$ |
| **Steady state** | Always stable for ρ<1 | Always exists | Always exists |
| **Stability condition** | $\lambda < \mu$ | None (always exists) | None (always exists) |
| **Application** | Open system, e.g., public counter | Bounded system, e.g., call center | Closed system, e.g., machines needing repair |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Single Server Queuing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ARRIVING CUSTOMERS] -->|Poisson rate lambda| B[QUEUE WAITING LINE]
    B --> C[SINGLE SERVER]
    C -->|Service rate mu| D[DEPARTED CUSTOMERS]
    E[BLOCKED CUSTOMERS] -.->|When N exceeded| A
    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 State Transition Rate Diagram (Birth-Death Process for M/M/1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0((State 0)) -- lambda --> S1((State 1))
    S1((State 1)) -- mu --> S0((State 0))
    S1((State 1)) -- lambda --> S2((State 2))
    S2((State 2)) -- mu --> S1((State 1))
    S2((State 2)) -- lambda --> S3((State n))
    S3((State n)) -- mu --> S2((State 2))
    S3((State n)) -- lambda --> S4((State N))
    S4((State N)) -- mu --> S3((State n))
    style S0 fill:#90EE90
    style S1 fill:#FFFF99
    style S2 fill:#FFFF99
    style S3 fill:#FFFF99
    style S4 fill:#FF7F7F
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow: M/M/1 Performance Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[INPUT: lambda, mu, N optional] --> P2{Check rho less than 1?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Compute P0 using normalization]
    P2 -- No --> P4[UNSTABLE: Queue grows to infinity]
    P3 --> P5[Compute all Pn for n = 0 to N]
    P5 --> P6[Compute L, Lq, W, Wq]
    P6 --> P7[OUTPUT: Performance Metrics]
    P4 --> P8[Add another server or speed up service]
    P8 --> P2
    style P1 fill:#E6E6FA
    style P4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style P7 fill:#90EE90
    style P8 fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow: Which Single Server Model to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Single Server Problem] --> Q2{Is population finite?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is waiting room infinite?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use M/M/1 K/K Model]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5[Use M/M/1 Model]
    Q3 -- No --> Q6[Use M/M/1 N Model]
    Q4 --> Q7[Machine repair / maintenance]
    Q5 --> Q8[Open system e.g. Bank counter]
    Q6 --> Q9[Bounded system e.g. Parking lot]
    style Q4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Q5 fill:#90EE90
    style Q6 fill:#FFD700
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Kendall's notation for classifying queuing systems and list the assumptions of an M/M/1 queueing model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Kendall's Notation:** A/B/c : d/e
> - A = Inter-arrival time distribution
> - B = Service time distribution
> - c = Number of servers
> - d = Maximum system capacity
> - e = Queue discipline

**Assumptions of M/M/1:**
1. Arrivals follow Poisson process with rate λ
2. Service times are exponentially distributed with rate μ
3. Single server with FIFO discipline
4. Infinite queue length and infinite calling population
5. Steady-state condition: ρ = λ/μ < 1

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define traffic intensity in a queuing system. Why must it be less than 1 for an M/M/1 system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Traffic Intensity (ρ):** It is the ratio of average arrival rate (λ) to average service rate (μ), representing the fraction of time the server is busy.

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$$

**Why ρ < 1?**
For steady-state to exist, the server must be able to handle customers **faster** than they arrive on average. If ρ ≥ 1 (i.e., λ ≥ μ), the queue length grows **unbounded** with time and no finite steady-state probability distribution exists. The series $\sum \rho^n$ diverges when ρ ≥ 1, making $P_0$ undefined.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state probabilities $P_n$ for an M/M/1 queueing model. Hence obtain expressions for L, Lq, W, and Wq. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A small workshop has a single drilling machine. Jobs arrive at the rate of 4 per hour (Poisson) and the drilling time averages **10 minutes** (Exponential). Determine:
   (i) Average number of jobs in the queue
   (ii) Average waiting time in the queue
   (iii) Average number of jobs in the system
   (iv) Probability that there are 3 or more jobs in the system
   (v) Average time a job spends in the system **[7 marks]**

---

#### 🔑 SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Define state probabilities $P_n = P\{N = n\}$ at steady state.

**Step 2:** Birth-death rates: λₙ = λ, μₙ = μ (for n ≥ 1).

**Step 3:** Balance equation for n = 0:
$$\mu P_1 = \lambda P_0 \Rightarrow P_1 = \rho P_0$$

**Step 4:** For n ≥ 1, balance gives:
$$\mu P_{n+1} + \lambda P_{n-1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$$
$$\Rightarrow P_{n+1} - P_n = \rho(P_n - P_{n-1})$$

**Step 5:** By induction: $P_n = \rho^n P_0$

**Step 6:** Normalization:
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n P_0 = P_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} = 1 \Rightarrow P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{P_n = (1 - \rho)\rho^n}$$

**Step 7:** Performance measures:
$$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$

$$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \quad \text{(by Little's Law)}$$

$$W_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:** λ = 4/hr, Service time = 10 min ⇒ μ = 60/10 = 6/hr
**Compute:** ρ = 4/6 = **2/3 = 0.6667**

**(i) Average number in queue:**
$$L_q = \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{16}{6 \times 2} = \frac{16}{12} = 1.333$$

**(ii) Average waiting time in queue:**
$$W_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{4}{6 \times 2} = \frac{4}{12} = 0.333 \text{ hr} = 20 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) Average number in system:**
$$L = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ jobs}$$

**(iv) Probability of 3 or more jobs:**
$$P(N \geq 3) = \sum_{n=3}^{\infty} \rho^n (1-\rho) = \rho^3 = (2/3)^3 = 8/27 = 0.2963$$

(Using the geometric tail formula $P(N \geq k) = \rho^k$ for k ≥ 1)

**(v) Average time in system:**
$$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ hr} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the M/M/1 : N (finite capacity) queuing model. Derive the steady-state probabilities and the performance measures. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Customers arrive at a barber shop in Poisson pattern at a rate of **5 per hour**. The service time is exponential with mean **8 minutes**. The shop can hold a maximum of **4 customers** (including the one in service). Find:
   (i) Probability that the shop is empty
   (ii) Effective arrival rate
   (iii) Average number of customers in the queue
   (iv) Average waiting time of a customer in the queue
   (v) Probability that an arriving customer is turned away **[7 marks]**

---

#### 🔑 SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

### Part (a) — Theory [7 marks]

In **M/M/1 : N**, the system has a maximum capacity of N customers (including the one being served). When all N positions are occupied, an arriving customer is **rejected (balks)** and leaves the system permanently.

**Birth-Death rates:**
- $\lambda_n = \lambda$ for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ and $\lambda_n = 0$ for $n \geq N$
- $\mu_n = \mu$ for $n = 1, 2, \ldots, N$

**Derivation of $P_n$:** Using the recursive birth-death formula:
$$P_n = P_0 \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = P_0 \rho^n, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N$$

**Normalization** $\sum_{n=0}^{N} P_n = 1$:
- If $\rho \neq 1$: $P_0 = \dfrac{1-\rho}{1-\rho^{N+1}}$
- If $\rho = 1$: $P_0 = \dfrac{1}{N+1}$

**Effective arrival rate:** $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda(1 - P_N)$

**Performance measures:**
$$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \frac{(N+1)\rho^{N+1}}{1-\rho^{N+1}}$$

$$L_q = L - (1 - P_0)$$

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda(1 - P_N)}, \quad W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda(1 - P_N)}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:** λ = 5/hr, Service mean = 8 min ⇒ μ = 60/8 = 7.5/hr, N = 4
**Compute:** ρ = 5/7.5 = 2/3 ≈ 0.6667

**(i) Probability shop is empty:**
$$P_0 = \frac{1 - 2/3}{1 - (2/3)^5} = \frac{1/3}{1 - 0.1317} = \frac{0.3333}{0.8683} = 0.3839$$

**(ii) Effective arrival rate:**
First, $P_4 = P_0 \cdot \rho^4 = 0.3839 \times (0.6667)^4 = 0.3839 \times 0.1975 = 0.0758$

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda(1 - P_4) = 5(1 - 0.0758) = 5 \times 0.9242 = 4.621 \text{ /hr}$$

**(iii) Average number in queue:**
$$L = \frac{2/3}{1/3} - \frac{5 \times (2/3)^5}{0.8683} = 2 - \frac{5 \times 0.1317}{0.8683} = 2 - 0.7582 = 1.2418$$

$$L_q = L - (1 - P_0) = 1.2418 - (1 - 0.3839) = 1.2418 - 0.6161 = 0.6257$$

**(iv) Average waiting time in queue:**
$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{0.6257}{4.621} = 0.1354 \text{ hr} = 8.12 \text{ min}$$

**(v) Probability of being turned away:**
$$P(\text{turned away}) = P_4 = 0.0758 \approx 7.58\%$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing λ and μ units:** Always convert to a **common time unit** (per hour or per minute). Mixing units gives a wrong ρ.
> 2. **Forgetting to use λ_eff in Little's Law** for M/M/1:N. Many students use the original λ instead of λ(1−P_N), causing 2-3 mark loss.
> 3. **Using ρ ≥ 1:** If ρ ≥ 1, the system is unstable — say "no steady-state exists" and stop. Do not compute P₀.
> 4. **M/M/1 : K/K vs M/M/1 : N confusion:** K/K = finite population (machines), N = finite capacity (parking lot). Different state probabilities!
> 5. **Forgetting L = Lq + ρ:** Always cross-check using this identity.
> 6. **Wrong Lq formula for M/M/1:N:** Many use $L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ (M/M/1 formula) instead of the corrected formula — major mistake.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 HIGH-FREQUENCY EXAM POINTS — SINGLE SERVER MODELS**

- ✅ **M/M/1 (infinite):** $P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$, $P_0 = 1-\rho$, **stable only if ρ < 1**
- ✅ **L = ρ/(1−ρ)** and **W = 1/(μ−λ)** are the two MOST IMPORTANT formulas
- ✅ **Little's Law:** L = λW and Lq = λWq (always true)
- ✅ **Identity:** L = Lq + ρ (system = queue + the one in service)
- ✅ **W = Wq + 1/μ** (time in system = waiting + service time)
- ✅ **M/M/1:N:** Use **λ_eff = λ(1−P_N)** in Little's Law — this is a top favorite KTU question!
- ✅ **Tail probability:** $P(N \geq k) = \rho^k$ for M/M/1 (geometric distribution)
- ✅ **Waiting time tail:** $P(W_q > t) = e^{-(\mu-\lambda)t}$ (exponential with parameter (μ−λ))
- ✅ **M/M/1:K/K** (machine repair): Arrival rate depends on state: λₙ = (K−n)λ
- ✅ **Special case ρ = 1** in M/M/1:N: $P_0 = 1/(N+1)$
- ✅ **Quality check:** Always verify $L = Lq + \rho$ after computation
- ✅ **Key difference:** Finite **capacity** (N) limits waiting room; finite **population** (K) limits the source.
- ✅ **Typical KTU marks split:** Theory 3-4 marks + Numerical 10-11 marks (Part B total 14 marks).

> 🎓 **Master Tip:** For M/M/1 problems, **always start** by computing ρ first. If ρ < 1, the system is solvable; if ρ ≥ 1, immediately state "no steady-state exists." This single step can earn you 1 mark for free!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi Server Problems. Inventory Control: Variables - Deterministic Inventory Models - Purchasing Model Without Shortages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multi Server Problems. Inventory Control: Variables - Deterministic Inventory Models - Purchasing Model Without Shortages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multi-Server Queuing Problems (M/M/c Model)

#### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multi-Server Queuing Model** is a stochastic service system in which **two or more identical parallel service channels (servers)** are available to attend to customers arriving from a single or multiple queues. Under the standard birth-death process assumptions, the model is designated as the **M/M/c : FIFO/∞/∞** queue, where arrivals follow a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$, service times are **exponentially distributed** with rate $\mu$ per server, and there are $c$ parallel servers, an infinite queue capacity, and an infinite calling population.

The **traffic intensity** is defined as:

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c \cdot \mu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Condition (KTU Board Favourite):** The system reaches a **steady-state statistical equilibrium** if and only if $\rho < 1$. If $\rho \geq 1$, the queue length grows unbounded with time and the system is **unstable**.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bank branch with **3 cash counters** (servers) and a **single common queue** of customers. Customers arrive randomly (Poisson) and the service time at any counter is variable (exponential). The question is: "On average, how long must a customer wait, and what is the probability that all 3 counters are simultaneously idle?" The M/M/c model answers exactly this — the bank manager can decide whether 3 counters are enough or whether a 4th counter is justified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Insight:** Adding a second server is *not* twice as efficient as one server. The gain is *non-linear* because of queue dynamics. As $c$ increases for fixed $\lambda$, the average waiting time falls rapidly but the utilisation per server also falls. KTU questions frequently test this trade-off.

---

### 1.2 Inventory Control — Core Variables

#### Formal Definition

**Inventory Control** is the scientific methodology of planning, organising, and controlling the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods so as to **minimise the total cost** (ordering + carrying + shortage + purchase) while maintaining a desired service level.

#### Inventory Variables (KTU 2024 — Module 3 Highlights)

| Variable | Symbol | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Demand rate | $D$ | Units consumed per year (assumed deterministic in this model) |
| Order quantity | $Q$ | Lot size placed per purchase order |
| Ordering cost | $C_0$ | Fixed cost per order (paperwork, transport, inspection) |
| Carrying (holding) cost | $C_c$ | Cost to hold one unit in stock for one year (rent, insurance, spoilage) |
| Purchase cost | $C_p$ | Unit price of the item |
| Shortage cost | $C_s$ | Penalty when demand exceeds stock |
| Lead time | $L$ | Time between placing and receiving an order |
| Reorder point | $R$ | Stock level that triggers a new order |
| Cycle time | $T$ | Time between two consecutive orders |
| Number of orders/year | $N$ | $N = D/Q$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deterministic vs Probabilistic Models (KTU Classification):**
> - **Deterministic Models** — Demand $D$ and lead time $L$ are *known with certainty*.
> - **Probabilistic Models** — Demand is a *random variable* with a known distribution.
> This module covers **Deterministic Purchasing Model Without Shortages (EOQ Model)**.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **kirana shop owner** who sells rice. Every week, customers take a known quantity (say 200 kg/week). The owner places orders from a wholesaler. Ordering too frequently → high transport cost. Ordering too much at once → high godown rent and risk of spoilage. The **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** is the "sweet spot" lot size that minimises the **sum of ordering + holding cost**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples:** EOQ is used in supply chain management for spare parts inventory in manufacturing plants, blood bank management in hospitals, reorder planning in retail chains, and procurement planning in construction material logistics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 M/M/c Model — Step-by-Step Logic

The M/M/c model is derived from the **birth–death process** with transition rates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_n &= \lambda \quad \text{for all } n \geq 0 \\[2pt]
\mu_n &=
\begin{cases}
n\mu, & 0 \leq n < c \\[2pt]
c\mu, & n \geq c
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steady-state probability** $P_n$ (probability of exactly $n$ customers in the system):

$$
P_n =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!}\, P_0, & 0 \leq n < c \\[8pt]
\dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{c!\, c^{\,n-c}}\, P_0, & n \geq c
\end{cases}
$$

**Why two different cases?**
- For $n < c$: there are fewer customers than servers, so each is being served by one server → effective service rate is $n\mu$.
- For $n \geq c$: all $c$ servers are busy, additional customers form the queue → effective service rate is capped at $c\mu$.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — M/M/c

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic Intensity | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu}$ | Server utilisation; must satisfy $\rho < 1$ |
| Idle Probability $P_0$ | $P_0 = \left[\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{c-1}\dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} \;+\; \dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1-\rho}\right]^{-1}$ | Probability system is empty |
| Average number in queue $L_q$ | $L_q = \dfrac{P_0\,(\lambda/\mu)^c\,\rho}{c!\,(1-\rho)^2}$ | Customers waiting (not yet served) |
| Average number in system $L_s$ | $L_s = L_q + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Customers waiting + being served |
| Average waiting time $W_q$ | $W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda}$ | Time spent in queue only |
| Average time in system $W_s$ | $W_s = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Total time in bank/hospital |
| Probability of waiting | $P(w > 0) = \dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)}\,P_0$ | Customer must join queue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Little's Law (KTU Quick Check):** $L_s = \lambda \cdot W_s$ and $L_q = \lambda \cdot W_q$. These two identities are extremely useful for cross-verification in numerical problems.

---

### 2.3 Purchasing Model Without Shortages — Logic Steps

**Model Assumptions (State explicitly in every KTU answer):**

1. Demand $D$ is **uniform** and **known** (deterministic).
2. Lead time is either **zero** or **constant**.
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate).
4. **No shortages are permitted** — inventory never hits zero before the next order arrives.
5. Ordering cost $C_0$ is **fixed per order** (independent of $Q$).
6. Carrying cost $C_c$ is **linear** in average inventory and time.
7. Purchase cost per unit is **constant** (no quantity discount).

**The Inventory Cycle (Saw-Tooth Pattern):**

- At $t = 0$, stock = $Q$ (maximum).
- Stock depletes at rate $D$ per year → at $t = T$, stock = 0.
- Immediately a new order of $Q$ arrives, restoring stock to $Q$.
- **Average inventory** over one cycle $= \dfrac{Q}{2}$.

**Cost Components per Year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ordering Cost} &= \underbrace{\left(\frac{D}{Q}\right)}_{\text{No. of orders}} \cdot C_0 \\[4pt]
\text{Carrying Cost} &= \underbrace{\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)}_{\text{Avg. inventory}} \cdot C_c
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why minimise these two only?** The purchase cost $\frac{D}{Q} \cdot Q \cdot C_p = D \cdot C_p$ is **constant** and does not depend on $Q$. It cancels out of the optimisation. Shortage cost is **zero** by assumption of this model. Hence Total Relevant Cost (TRC) is the sum of ordering and carrying costs only.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Purchasing Model Without Shortages (EOQ)

| Quantity | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_0}{C_c}}$ | Optimal lot size |
| Optimal cycle time (years) | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0}{D \cdot C_c}}$ | Time between two orders |
| Optimal number of orders/year | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot C_c}{2 C_0}}$ | Frequency of ordering |
| Minimum total cost | $TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c}$ | Sum of ordering + carrying cost |
| Time between orders (days) | $t_0 = \dfrac{365 \cdot Q^*}{D}$ | Useful for practical planning |
| Reorder point | $R = D \cdot L$ | Stock level at which to reorder |

> [!NOTE]
> **EOQ is also called "Wilson's Formula" or "Harris Model".** KTU answers that mention this name earn extra impression. The formula is symmetric: at $Q^*$, **Ordering Cost = Carrying Cost** (a useful verification trick).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Determines optimal batch size for production runs.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Minimises expiry-loss of medicines.
- **E-commerce Warehouses:** Amazon/Flipkart use EOQ variants for SKU replenishment.
- **Aviation:** Spare-parts inventory for aircraft maintenance.
- **Construction:** Bulk material procurement (cement, steel, aggregate).

The choice of $Q$ directly affects **working capital lock-up**, **warehouse space**, **order processing load**, and **stockout risk**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of EOQ (Purchasing Model Without Shortages)

Let the order quantity be $Q$ (units per order). Then:

**Step 1 — Number of orders per year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{D}{Q} \quad \text{orders per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cycle time (length of one inventory cycle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{N} = \frac{Q}{D} \quad \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ordering cost per year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OC}(Q) &= N \cdot C_0 = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Carrying cost per year:** Since inventory drops linearly from $Q$ to $0$, the average inventory is $\frac{Q}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CC}(Q) &= \frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total relevant cost per year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(Q) &= \text{OC}(Q) + \text{CC}(Q) = \frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot C_c}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — First-order optimality condition:** Differentiate $TC(Q)$ w.r.t. $Q$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} &= -\frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q^2} + \frac{C_c}{2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $Q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{D \cdot C_0}{Q^2} &= \frac{C_c}{2} \\[4pt]
Q^2 &= \frac{2 D C_0}{C_c} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Second-order confirmation (minimum check):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 TC}{dQ^2} &= \frac{2 D C_0}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{for } Q > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $TC(Q)$ is **convex** and $Q^*$ is a true **minimum** (not a maximum).

**Step 9 — Substituting $Q^*$ back into $TC(Q)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\min} &= \frac{D C_0}{Q^*} + \frac{Q^* C_c}{2} = \frac{D C_0}{\sqrt{2DC_0/C_c}} + \frac{\sqrt{2DC_0/C_c}\,C_c}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{D^2 C_0^2 C_c}{2 D C_0}} + \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0 C_c^2}{4 C_c}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{D C_0 C_c}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{D C_0 C_c}{2}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{D C_0 C_c}{2}} \\[4pt]
TC_{\min} &= \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Verification identity (KTU Board Favourite):**

At $Q = Q^*$, ordering cost exactly equals carrying cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left.\frac{D C_0}{Q}\right|_{Q=Q^*} &= \left.\frac{Q C_c}{2}\right|_{Q=Q^*} = \frac{TC_{\min}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Example 1 — EOQ Model (Full Working)

**Problem:** A factory uses **10,000 units/year** of a particular component. The cost of placing one order is **₹200** and the annual holding cost is **₹4 per unit**. Find the EOQ, the optimum number of orders, the optimum cycle time, and the minimum total cost.

**Given:**

$$
D = 10{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad C_0 = ₹200/\text{order}, \quad C_c = ₹4/\text{unit/yr}
$$

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: EOQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= 1000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Optimum number of orders per year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N^* &= \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{1000} = 10 \text{ orders/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Optimum cycle time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{1000}{10{,}000} = 0.1 \text{ year} = 1.2 \text{ months} = 36.5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Minimum total cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\min} &= \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 4} = \sqrt{16{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
TC_{\min} &= ₹4000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verification — Ordering cost = Carrying cost?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OC} &= \frac{10{,}000}{1000} \times 200 = ₹2000 \\[4pt]
\text{CC} &= \frac{1000}{2} \times 4 = ₹2000 \\[4pt]
\text{OC} + \text{CC} &= ₹4000 = TC_{\min} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example 2 — M/M/c Multi-Server Queue (Full Working)

**Problem:** Customers arrive at a service centre at the rate of $\lambda = 30$/hour following a Poisson distribution. There are $c = 2$ identical servers, each serving at an exponential rate of $\mu = 20$/hour. Find $P_0$, $L_q$, $L_s$, $W_q$, $W_s$, and the probability that a customer has to wait.

**Given:**

$$
\lambda = 30/\text{hr}, \quad \mu = 20/\text{hr}, \quad c = 2
$$

**Step 1: Traffic Intensity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c \mu} = \frac{30}{2 \times 20} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\rho < 1$, the system is **stable**. ✓

**Step 2: Compute $P_0$**

Here, $\lambda/\mu = 30/20 = 1.5$, so $(\lambda/\mu)^c = 1.5^2 = 2.25$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} + \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} \right]^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \left[ \frac{1.5^0}{0!} + \frac{1.5^1}{1!} + \frac{1.5^2}{2!}\cdot \frac{1}{1-0.75} \right]^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \left[ 1 + 1.5 + \frac{2.25}{2} \times 4 \right]^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \left[ 1 + 1.5 + 1.125 \times 4 \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1 + 1.5 + 4.5 \right]^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \left[ 7.0 \right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Average number in queue, $L_q$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_q &= \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c! \cdot (1-\rho)^2} = \frac{0.1429 \times 2.25 \times 0.75}{2 \times (0.25)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.2411}{2 \times 0.0625} = \frac{0.2411}{0.125} \approx 1.9286 \text{ customers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Average number in system, $L_s$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = 1.9286 + 1.5 = 3.4286 \text{ customers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Average waiting time in queue, $W_q$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_q &= \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{1.9286}{30} = 0.06429 \text{ hr} \approx 3.857 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Average total time in system, $W_s$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_s &= W_q + \frac{1}{\mu} = 0.06429 + 0.05 = 0.11429 \text{ hr} \approx 6.857 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Probability of waiting**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{wait}) &= \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!(1-\rho)} P_0 = \frac{2.25}{2 \times 0.25} \times 0.1429 \\[4pt]
&= 4.5 \times 0.1429 = 0.6429
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via Little's Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= \lambda \cdot W_s = 30 \times 0.11429 = 3.4287 \;\;\checkmark \\[4pt]
L_q &= \lambda \cdot W_q = 30 \times 0.06429 = 1.9287 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** About 64.3 % of customers must wait in queue. Average wait is ~3.86 minutes. The manager may consider adding a 3rd server if customer satisfaction targets demand it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Multi-Server Queuing System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ARRIVAL PROCESS\nPoisson lambda"] --> nodeB["WAITING QUEUE\nFIFO Discipline\nInfinite Capacity"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"DISPATCHER\nRoutes to next\nfree server"}
    nodeC --> nodeS1["SERVER 1\nExp Service\nRate mu"]
    nodeC --> nodeS2["SERVER 2\nExp Service\nRate mu"]
    nodeC --> nodeSc["SERVER c\nExp Service\nRate mu"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeD["DEPARTURES\nServed Customers"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeD
    nodeSc --> nodeD
```

> **Figure 1:** Block-level architecture of an M/M/c multi-server queue. The dispatcher routes customers to the *first available* server. Effective service rate when all $c$ servers are busy is $c\mu$.

---

### 4.2 Inventory Cycle — Saw-Tooth Pattern (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START OF CYCLE\nReceive Order Q units\nStock Level = Q MAX"] --> nodeB["CONSUMPTION PHASE\nDemand at constant rate D\nStock declines linearly"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["DECISION POINT\nStock Level = 0\nTrigger Next Order"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ORDER PLACEMENT\nOrdering Cost C0 incurred\nLead Time L elapses"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STOCK REPLENISHED\nStock Level jumps back to Q\nCycle Repeats"]
    nodeE --> nodeA
```

> **Figure 2:** Functional flow of the deterministic purchasing model **without shortages**. The cycle is a perfect saw-tooth because stock never goes below zero and replenishment is instantaneous.

---

### 4.3 Cost Behaviour in EOQ Model (Analytical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ORDER QUANTITY Q"] --> nodeB["Ordering Cost\nDC0 by Q\nHYPERBOLIC DECREASE"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Carrying Cost\nQCc by 2\nLINEAR INCREASE"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["TOTAL COST TC"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["MINIMUM TC*\nAT Q* = EOQ\nOC = CC"]
```

> **Figure 3:** The total cost curve is **U-shaped (convex)**. The minimum occurs precisely where the decreasing hyperbola of ordering cost intersects the increasing line of carrying cost. **At EOQ, Ordering Cost = Carrying Cost.**

---

### 4.4 Birth–Death State Transition Diagram for M/M/c

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> st0
    st0 --> st1 : arrival rate lambda
    st1 --> st0 : service rate 1 mu
    st1 --> st2 : arrival rate lambda
    st2 --> st1 : service rate 2 mu
    st2 --> st3 : arrival rate lambda
    st3 --> st2 : service rate c mu
    st3 --> st4 : arrival rate lambda
    st4 --> st3 : service rate c mu
    note right of st2
        For n is less than c,
        service rate is n times mu
    end note
    note right of st3
        For n is greater than or equal to c,
        service rate is fixed at c times mu
    end note
```

> **Figure 4:** Birth-death transitions. Birth (arrival) rate is always $\lambda$. Death (service) rate is $n\mu$ for $n < c$ and saturates at $c\mu$ for $n \geq c$, which is the mathematical heart of the M/M/c model.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define: (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), (ii) Reorder Point, (iii) Carrying Cost.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ):** It is the fixed order quantity $Q^*$ that minimises the total annual inventory cost (sum of ordering and carrying costs) in a deterministic purchasing model without shortages. Mathematically,

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}}$$

where $D$ is annual demand, $C_0$ is ordering cost per order, and $C_c$ is carrying cost per unit per year.

**(ii) Reorder Point (R):** It is the inventory level at which a fresh order must be placed to avoid stockouts, computed as $R = D \times L$ where $L$ is the lead time.

**(iii) Carrying Cost:** The cost incurred by holding inventory in stock, comprising storage, insurance, depreciation, obsolescence, and the opportunity cost of locked-up capital. It is usually expressed as a fraction or fixed amount per unit per year.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the steady-state condition for an M/M/c queue. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The steady-state condition for an M/M/c queue is $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu} < 1$.

This is **necessary** because:
- If $\rho \geq 1$, the average number of arrivals per unit time equals or exceeds the maximum service capacity of the $c$ servers combined, so the queue length grows without bound and no statistical equilibrium exists.
- The birth–death process then becomes **transient and divergent**.
- Only when $\rho < 1$ does the system reach a **statistical steady state** with finite, computable performance measures $L_q$, $W_q$, $P_0$, etc.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has internal choice (Q. A or Q. B). Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** A company uses **8,000 components per year**. The cost of placing an order is **₹150** and the carrying cost is **₹5 per unit per year**. Calculate:
(i) Economic Order Quantity
(ii) Optimum number of orders per year
(iii) Optimum cycle time
(iv) Minimum total cost.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the company wants to place only **4 orders per year** instead of the EOQ-derived number, calculate the additional cost incurred and comment. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** At a toll plaza, vehicles arrive at a Poisson rate of $\lambda = 40$/hr. There are **$c = 3$ toll booths**, each with exponential service time of mean **3 minutes** (i.e., $\mu = 20$/hr). Calculate:
(i) Traffic intensity $\rho$
(ii) Probability that the system is empty, $P_0$
(iii) Expected number of vehicles in the queue, $L_q$
(iv) Average waiting time in the system, $W_s$.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Comment on the system utilisation. If a 4th toll booth is added, how would the average waiting time change? Justify qualitatively. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

---

#### SOLUTION — Question A

**Data:** $D = 8000$ units/yr, $C_0 = ₹150$/order, $C_c = ₹5$/unit/yr.

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**(i) EOQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 150}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}400{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{480{,}000} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \sqrt{480000} \approx 692.82 \approx 693 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Optimum number of orders:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N^* &= \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8000}{692.82} \approx 11.547 \approx 12 \text{ orders/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Optimum cycle time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{692.82}{8000} \text{ yr} \approx 0.0866 \text{ yr} \approx 31.6 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Minimum total cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\min} &= \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_c} = \sqrt{2 \times 8000 \times 150 \times 5} = \sqrt{12{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
TC_{\min} &= ₹3464.10 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $OC = (8000/692.82)\times 150 = ₹1732.05$ ; $CC = (692.82/2)\times 5 = ₹1732.05$ ✓

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

If the company fixes $N = 4$ orders/year, then:

$$
Q = \frac{D}{N} = \frac{8000}{4} = 2000 \text{ units}
$$

**Total cost at $Q = 2000$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(2000) &= \frac{D C_0}{Q} + \frac{Q C_c}{2} = \frac{8000 \times 150}{2000} + \frac{2000 \times 5}{2} \\[4pt]
&= 600 + 5000 = ₹5600/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Additional cost incurred:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta TC &= TC(2000) - TC_{\min} = 5600 - 3464.10 = ₹2135.90/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comment:** Ordering only 4 times/year inflates inventory and nearly **doubles** the total cost. This demonstrates the steep penalty of deviating from EOQ on the high-inventory side. The cost penalty is asymmetric — cost grows much faster when $Q > Q^*$ than when $Q < Q^*$ in the same proportion.

---

#### SOLUTION — Question B

**Data:** $\lambda = 40$/hr, $\mu = 20$/hr, $c = 3$.

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**(i) Traffic intensity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \frac{40}{3 \times 20} = \frac{40}{60} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\rho < 1$, system is **stable**. ✓

**(ii) Compute $P_0$.** Here $\lambda/\mu = 2$, so $(\lambda/\mu)^c = 2^3 = 8$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{2} \frac{2^n}{n!} + \frac{2^3}{3!}\cdot\frac{1}{1-\rho} \right]^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \left[ \frac{1}{0!} + \frac{2}{1!} + \frac{4}{2!} + \frac{8}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{1/3} \right]^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \left[ 1 + 2 + 2 + \frac{8}{6} \times 3 \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1 + 2 + 2 + 4 \right]^{-1} = \left[ 9 \right]^{-1} \\[4pt]
P_0 &= \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average number in queue, $L_q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_q &= \frac{P_0 (\lambda/\mu)^c \rho}{c!(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{(1/9) \times 8 \times (2/3)}{6 \times (1/3)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{16/27}{6 \times 1/9} = \frac{16/27}{2/3} = \frac{16}{27} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{48}{54} = \frac{8}{9} \\[4pt]
L_q &\approx 0.8889 \text{ vehicles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Average waiting time in the system, $W_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{8}{9} + 2 = \frac{26}{9} \approx 2.8889 \\[4pt]
W_s &= \frac{L_s}{\lambda} = \frac{26/9}{40} = \frac{26}{360} = \frac{13}{180} \approx 0.0722 \text{ hr} \\[4pt]
W_s &\approx 4.33 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**System utilisation:** The combined utilisation of all 3 booths is $\rho = 0.6667$, i.e., the booths are busy **66.67 %** of the time. Each individual booth has utilisation $0.6667$ (since all booths share the load equally due to exponential assumption).

**Effect of adding a 4th booth** ($c = 4$): New utilisation $\rho' = 40/(4 \times 20) = 0.5$, and $(\lambda/\mu)^4 = 16$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0' &= \left[1 + 2 + 2 + \frac{4}{3} + \frac{16}{24}\cdot\frac{1}{0.5}\right]^{-1} = \left[1+2+2+1.333+1.333\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{7.666} \approx 0.1304
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_q' &= \frac{P_0' \cdot 16 \cdot 0.5}{24 \cdot (0.5)^2} = \frac{0.1304 \times 8}{24 \times 0.25} = \frac{1.0432}{6} \approx 0.1739
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
W_s' = \frac{L_q' + 2}{40} = \frac{2.1739}{40} \approx 0.0543 \text{ hr} \approx 3.26 \text{ min}
$$

**Qualitative conclusion:** Adding a 4th booth **reduces** average waiting time from ~4.33 min to ~3.26 min (a saving of ~1.07 min/vehicle) but also reduces individual server utilisation from 66.67 % to 50 %. The trade-off depends on whether the cost of an extra booth is justified by the customer-time savings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** of the EOQ model — *always list the 6–7 assumptions before solving.* Partial marks are awarded for this.
> 2. **Mixing up OC and CC identities** — at EOQ, OC = CC = $TC_{\min}/2$. Use this as a quick **verification step**.
> 3. **Forgetting the M/M/c stability check** $\rho < 1$ before computing $P_0$. If $\rho \geq 1$, the answer is *"system is unstable, no steady state."*
> 4. **Confusing $W_q$ and $W_s$** — $W_q$ is *waiting in queue only*, $W_s$ is *total time in system (queue + service)*.
> 5. **Unit consistency** — in inventory problems, demand $D$ must be in the *same time unit* as the carrying cost $C_c$ (usually per year).
> 6. **EOQ must be a positive integer** — if a fractional answer is obtained, the *nearest integer* is typically taken; the cost difference is negligible.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **EOQ formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DC_0}{C_c}}$ — Wilson's formula, deterministic, no shortages.
- ✅ **Verification identity at EOQ:** Ordering Cost = Carrying Cost = $\dfrac{TC_{\min}}{2}$.
- ✅ **Optimum cycle time:** $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2C_0}{DC_c}}$.
- ✅ **Optimum orders/year:** $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DC_c}{2C_0}}$.
- ✅ **Minimum total cost:** $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DC_0C_c}$ (excluding constant purchase cost).
- ✅ **Reorder point:** $R = D \times L$ where $L$ is lead time.
- ✅ **M/M/c model** has Poisson arrivals, exponential service, $c$ parallel servers.
- ✅ **Traffic intensity** $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c\mu}$; **stability requires** $\rho < 1$.
- ✅ **Idle probability:** $P_0 = \left[\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{c-1}\dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} + \dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1-\rho}\right]^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Queue length:** $L_q = \dfrac{P_0(\lambda/\mu)^c\rho}{c!(1-\rho)^2}$.
- ✅ **System length:** $L_s = L_q + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$.
- ✅ **Little's Law:** $L_s = \lambda W_s$ and $L_q = \lambda W_q$ — *always cross-verify*.
- ✅ **Inventory cycle is a saw-tooth** for the no-shortage model.
- ✅ **Assumptions** of deterministic purchasing model must be stated in every answer.
- ✅ **Penalty of deviating from EOQ** is asymmetric — cost rises faster on the high-inventory side.
- ✅ **EOQ and the M/M/c model are the two pillars of Module 3** — both are 14-mark favourites in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Model Without Shortages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Model Without Shortages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Manufacturing Model Without Shortages (EMQ / Production Lot Size Model)** is a deterministic inventory control model in which a finite production rate gradually replenishes stock while demand is met concurrently, and stock-outs are **not permitted** in any cycle. The model is formally known as the **Economic Manufacturing Quantity (EMQ)** model or **Production Order Quantity (POQ)** model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** This model is a *generalization* of the classical Wilson's EOQ model. The classical EOQ assumes **instantaneous replenishment** (infinite production rate). The EMQ relaxes this assumption by allowing the production rate $P$ to be finite, where $P > D$ (production rate strictly greater than the demand rate).

**Parameter Nomenclature (KTU Convention):**

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $D$ | Annual demand rate | units/year |
| $P$ | Production rate | units/year |
| $Q$ | Production lot size (order quantity) | units |
| $C_1$ | Setup cost per production run | ₹ / setup |
| $C_2$ | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹ / unit / year |
| $t_1$ | Production time per cycle | year |
| $T$ | Cycle length (time between setups) | year |
| $I_{max}$ | Maximum inventory level | units |
| $K$ | Carrying cost fraction (sometimes $K = C_2$) | dimensionless |

**Assumptions of the Model:**

1. Demand rate $D$ is **uniform** and **deterministic** (known with certainty).
2. Production rate $P$ is **finite and constant**, with $P > D$.
3. Lead time $L$ is either zero or known and constant.
4. **No shortages are permitted** — the inventory never falls below zero.
5. The planning horizon is **infinite** (steady-state, long-run average cost analysis).
6. Setup cost $C_1$ and holding cost $C_2$ are **constant** per cycle.
7. Replenishment is **gradual and continuous** at rate $P$ during production window $t_1$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bakery Production Line 🍞

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a bakery that produces fresh bread. The oven bakes bread at a rate of **100 loaves/hour** (this is the *production rate, $P$*). Customers arrive and buy bread at a rate of **30 loaves/hour** (this is the *demand rate, $D$*). While the oven is running, bread is simultaneously being added to the shelf **and** being bought off the shelf — so the *net* accumulation on the shelf is only $100 - 30 = 70$ loaves/hour. The moment the oven stops, the shelf inventory begins to drop purely at the customer demand rate of 30 loaves/hour, until it reaches zero and the next batch begins.

**Why is the maximum inventory $I_{max}$ smaller than the lot size $Q$?**

Because during production, customers are already eating into the freshly baked output. So out of the $Q$ loaves produced in one batch, only a **fraction** actually lands on the shelf as buffer stock. That fraction is $(1 - D/P)$, and therefore:

$$I_{max} = Q \left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right)$$

In the bakery example: $I_{max} = Q(1 - 30/100) = 0.7Q$. Only **70% of the batch size** ever sits on the shelf as a peak — the other 30% was already sold *while* baking was happening.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If $P \to \infty$ (instantaneous replenishment), the manufacturing model **collapses** to the classical EOQ model. Verify: $I_{max} = Q(1 - D/P) \to Q(1 - 0) = Q$, which matches EOQ. ✔

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inventory Behavior Across One Cycle

A single production cycle of length $T$ decomposes into **two distinct phases**:

**Phase 1 — Production Phase ($0 \le t \le t_1$):**
- Inventory rises from $0$ to $I_{max}$.
- **Net accumulation rate** = Production rate − Demand rate = $P - D$.
- Duration of this phase: $t_1 = \dfrac{Q}{P}$.

**Phase 2 — Depletion Phase ($t_1 \le t \le T$):**
- Production has stopped; no fresh stock arrives.
- Inventory falls from $I_{max}$ to $0$ at the demand rate $D$.
- Duration of this phase: $T - t_1 = \dfrac{I_{max}}{D}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the cycle start and end at zero inventory?** Because the model forbids shortages. Each new production run starts exactly when the previous cycle's inventory hits zero, so the system is in a *steady-state sawtooth* waveform.

## 2.2 Cost Structure

The total relevant cost per unit time consists of **two** components only (no shortage penalty term because $C_3 = 0$):

**(a) Setup Cost per Unit Time:** Since $D/Q$ setups occur per year, each costing $C_1$:

$$\text{Setup cost rate} = \frac{C_1 \cdot D}{Q}$$

**(b) Holding Cost per Unit Time:** The average inventory over one cycle is the area of the triangle divided by $T$:

$$\text{Average inventory} = \frac{I_{max} \cdot T}{2 \cdot T} = \frac{I_{max}}{2} = \frac{Q(1 - D/P)}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Holding cost rate} = \frac{C_2 \cdot Q(1 - D/P)}{2}$$

**Total Cost Function:**

$$\boxed{\;TC(Q) = \frac{C_1 D}{Q} + \frac{C_2\, Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right)\;}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU University Examinations.** Memorize the master formula for $Q^*$ and the related optimal values.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / KTU Mnemonic |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Cycle length | $T = \dfrac{Q}{D}$ | Time between two consecutive setups |
| 2 | Production time per cycle | $t_1 = \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | Duration the machine actually runs |
| 3 | Idle / depletion time | $T - t_1 = \dfrac{Q}{D} - \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | Time machine is switched off |
| 4 | Maximum inventory | $I_{max} = Q\left(1 - \dfrac{D}{P}\right)$ | Peak shelf level in the cycle |
| 5 | Average inventory | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{I_{max}}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{D}{P}\right)$ | Used in holding cost |
| 6 | **Optimal lot size $Q^*$** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_1 D}{C_2 (1 - D/P)}}$ | **MASTER FORMULA** |
| 7 | **Optimal cycle time $T^*$** | $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_1}{C_2 D}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1 - D/P}}$ | Time between production runs |
| 8 | **Optimal production time $t_1^*$** | $t_1^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{P}$ | Machine run time per batch |
| 9 | **Optimal max inventory $I_{max}^*$** | $I_{max}^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_1 D (1 - D/P)}{C_2}}$ | Peak stock in optimal cycle |
| 10 | **Minimum total cost $TC^*$** | $TC^* = \sqrt{2 C_1 C_2 D \left(1 - \dfrac{D}{P}\right)}$ | Per unit time |
| 11 | Number of setups per year | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*}$ | Integer-rounded if fractional setups are infeasible |
| 12 | Time ratio | $\dfrac{t_1}{T} = \dfrac{D}{P}$ | Useful sanity check ($< 1$) |

**Special Case — Recovery of EOQ:**

When $P \to \infty$:

$$Q^* \longrightarrow \sqrt{\frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2}} \quad \text{(Wilson's EOQ formula)}$$

This confirms the EMQ model is a strict generalization of the EOQ model.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is this model applied in industry?**
> - **Automotive assembly lines** — parts manufactured in batches, consumed continuously by the next station.
> - **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** — drug bottles filled gradually, shipped steadily to distributors.
> - **Dairy & beverage industries** — bottled at a finite rate, distributed at a slower but steady rate.
> - **Semiconductor fabs** — wafer production rate is much higher than packaging/shipping rate.
> - **Steel mills** — coils produced continuously, consumed by stamping plants at a lower rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Formula $Q^*$

**Step 1 — Write the Total Cost per unit time:**

The relevant costs are setup (ordering) and holding. The shortage cost is zero by assumption.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(Q) &= \underbrace{\frac{C_1 D}{Q}}_{\text{Setup cost / unit time}} + \underbrace{\frac{C_2}{2}\cdot Q\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right)}_{\text{Holding cost / unit time}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the first derivative with respect to $Q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} &= -\frac{C_1 D}{Q^2} + \frac{C_2}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the first-order necessary condition for a minimum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} &= 0 \\
-\frac{C_1 D}{Q^2} + \frac{C_2}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q^*$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_1 D}{Q^2} &= \frac{C_2}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right) \\
Q^2 &= \frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2 (1 - D/P)} \\
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2 (1 - D/P)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the second-order condition (concavity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2\,TC(Q)}{dQ^2} &= \frac{2 C_1 D}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{for all } Q > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The second derivative is strictly positive, confirming that the critical point is a **global minimum** on $(0, \infty)$.

**Step 6 — Substitute $Q^*$ back into $TC(Q)$ to obtain the minimum cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC^* &= \frac{C_1 D}{Q^*} + \frac{C_2 Q^*}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right) \\
&= \frac{C_1 D}{\sqrt{\frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2(1-D/P)}}} + \frac{C_2}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right)\sqrt{\frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2(1-D/P)}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{C_1 D \cdot C_2(1-D/P)}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{C_1 D \cdot C_2(1-D/P)}{2}} \\
&= 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_1 C_2 D(1-D/P)}{2}} \\
TC^* &= \sqrt{2 C_1 C_2 D \left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Compare $TC^*$ with the EOQ minimum cost $\sqrt{2 C_1 C_2 D}$. The EMQ minimum cost is **smaller** by the factor $\sqrt{1 - D/P}$. Physically, this is because a finite production rate reduces the average inventory held, which in turn reduces holding cost.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A company produces a component at a rate of **200 units/day** and the demand rate is **80 units/day**. The setup cost per production run is ₹ **5,000**, and the holding cost is ₹ **2 per unit per year**. The company operates 250 days per year. Determine:
> **(a)** The economic manufacturing quantity $Q^*$.
> **(b)** The optimal cycle time $T^*$, production time $t_1^*$, and number of setups per year $N^*$.
> **(c)** The maximum inventory $I_{max}^*$ and minimum total cost $TC^*$.

**Given Data:**

- $D = 80 \times 250 = 20{,}000$ units/year
- $P = 200 \times 250 = 50{,}000$ units/year
- $C_1 = ₹\,5{,}000$ per setup
- $C_2 = ₹\,2$ per unit per year
- Production-to-demand ratio: $D/P = 20{,}000/50{,}000 = 0.4$

**Solution — Part (a): Optimal Lot Size $Q^*$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2 (1 - D/P)}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 20{,}000}{2 \times (1 - 0.4)}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^8}{1.2}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.6667 \times 10^8} \\
Q^* &\approx 12{,}910 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Part (b): Cycle Parameters**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{12{,}910}{20{,}000} = 0.6455 \text{ year} \approx 161.4 \text{ days} \\[4pt]
t_1^* &= \frac{Q^*}{P} = \frac{12{,}910}{50{,}000} = 0.2582 \text{ year} \approx 64.6 \text{ days} \\[4pt]
N^* &= \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{20{,}000}{12{,}910} \approx 1.55 \text{ setups/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Part (c): Inventory and Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{max}^* &= Q^* \left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right) = 12{,}910 \times 0.6 = 7{,}746 \text{ units} \\[4pt]
TC^* &= \sqrt{2 C_1 C_2 D (1 - D/P)} \\
&= \sqrt{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 2 \times 20{,}000 \times 0.6} \\
&= \sqrt{2.4 \times 10^8} \\
TC^* &\approx ₹\,15{,}492 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (sanity check):**

- Setup cost rate: $C_1 D / Q^* = 5{,}000 \times 20{,}000 / 12{,}910 \approx ₹\,7{,}746$/year.
- Holding cost rate: $C_2 Q^*(1-D/P)/2 = 2 \times 12{,}910 \times 0.6 / 2 \approx ₹\,7{,}746$/year.
- Both are **equal at optimum** ✔ — this is a universal property of EOQ/EMQ models.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    Peak --> P2["Phase 2: Production OFF\nDepletion rate = D\nDuration = T - t1"]
    P2 --> End([Cycle End: Inventory = 0])
    End -.->|"Next setup triggered"| Start

    subgraph CALC["Optimal Lot Size Calculation"]
