---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Q:** Distinguish between the classical EOQ model and the Economic Manufacturing Quantity (EMQ) model. Under what limiting condition do the two models become equivalent?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | EOQ Model | EMQ Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Replenishment | Instantaneous (lot arrives all at once) | Gradual (lot is produced at rate $P$) |
| Production rate $P$ | Considered infinite | Finite, with $P > D$ |
| Max inventory | $I_{max} = Q$ | $I_{max} = Q(1 - D/P)$ |
| Average inventory | $Q/2$ | $Q(1 - D/P)/2$ |
| Optimal order quantity | $\sqrt{2C_1D/C_2}$ | $\sqrt{2C_1D/[C_2(1-D/P)]}$ |

The two models become equivalent in the limiting case when $P \to \infty$, i.e., when replenishment is instantaneous.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Q:** Why is the maximum inventory in the EMQ model strictly less than the production lot size $Q$? Explain with a suitable expression.

**Model Answer:**

During the production phase (duration $t_1 = Q/P$), the system is simultaneously producing at rate $P$ and satisfying demand at rate $D$. The **net accumulation rate** of inventory is therefore $(P - D)$, not $P$. Hence, only a portion of the manufactured lot of size $Q$ ever accumulates as buffer stock in the warehouse; the remainder is consumed concurrently.

$$I_{max} = Q \left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right) < Q \quad \text{since } \frac{D}{P} > 0$$

This reduction in peak inventory is the **key economic advantage** of the EMQ model over the classical EOQ.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: Select ONE of the following)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** A factory produces a product at the rate of **30,000 units/year**. The demand is at the rate of **20,000 units/year**. The setup cost per production run is ₹ **4,000** and the holding cost is ₹ **5 per unit per year**.
**(a)** Determine the **economic manufacturing quantity**, the **maximum inventory**, the **cycle time**, and the **production time per cycle**.
**(b)** If the holding cost rises to ₹ **10 per unit per year**, recalculate $Q^*$ and comment on the effect.

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $P = 30{,}000$ units/year, $D = 20{,}000$ units/year, $C_1 = ₹\,4{,}000$, $C_2 = ₹\,5$/unit/year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \frac{D}{P} &= 1 - \frac{20{,}000}{30{,}000} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Optimal Lot Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2(1-D/P)}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4{,}000 \times 20{,}000}{5 \times (1/3)}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1.6 \times 10^8}{5/3}} \\
&= \sqrt{9.6 \times 10^7} \\
Q^* &\approx 9{,}798 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Inventory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{max}^* &= Q^* \left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right) = 9{,}798 \times \frac{1}{3} \approx 3{,}266 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{9{,}798}{20{,}000} \approx 0.4899 \text{ year} \approx 4.9 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Production Time per Cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1^* &= \frac{Q^*}{P} = \frac{9{,}798}{30{,}000} \approx 0.3266 \text{ year} \approx 3.27 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Setups/Year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N^* &= \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{20{,}000}{9{,}798} \approx 2.04 \text{ setups/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**New holding cost:** $C_2' = ₹\,10$/unit/year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q'^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4{,}000 \times 20{,}000}{10 \times (1/3)}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1.6 \times 10^8}{10/3}} \\
&= \sqrt{4.8 \times 10^7} \\
Q'^* &\approx 6{,}928 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** When the holding cost **doubles** (from ₹5 to ₹10), the optimal lot size **decreases** (from 9,798 to 6,928 units, a ~29% drop). This is intuitive: higher storage cost penalizes large buffers, so the factory produces smaller, more frequent batches. Conversely, **lower** holding cost favors **larger** batches.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** The annual demand of an item is **10,000 units**. The production rate is **25,000 units/year** and the setup cost is ₹ **2,000 per production run**. If the holding cost is **20% of the unit cost** and the unit cost is ₹ **100**, find the optimal order quantity, the optimal cycle time, and the minimum total cost. How many production runs will be made per year?

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- $D = 10{,}000$ units/year
- $P = 25{,}000$ units/year
- $C_1 = ₹\,2{,}000$ per setup
- $C_2 = 0.20 \times 100 = ₹\,20$ per unit per year
- $1 - D/P = 1 - 10{,}000/25{,}000 = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Optimal Lot Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 C_1 D}{C_2 (1 - D/P)}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2{,}000 \times 10{,}000}{20 \times 0.6}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^7}{12}} \\
&= \sqrt{3.333 \times 10^6} \\
Q^* &\approx 1{,}826 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Optimal Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{1{,}826}{10{,}000} = 0.1826 \text{ year} \approx 66.6 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Production Runs per Year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N^* &= \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}826} \approx 5.48 \text{ runs/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC^* &= \sqrt{2 C_1 C_2 D (1 - D/P)} \\
&= \sqrt{2 \times 2{,}000 \times 20 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.6} \\
&= \sqrt{4.8 \times 10^8} \\
TC^* &\approx ₹\,21{,}909 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Verification that setup cost = holding cost at optimum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup cost rate} &= \frac{C_1 D}{Q^*} = \frac{2{,}000 \times 10{,}000}{1{,}826} \approx ₹\,10{,}954 \\[4pt]
\text{Holding cost rate} &= \frac{C_2 Q^*(1-D/P)}{2} = \frac{20 \times 1{,}826 \times 0.6}{2} \approx ₹\,10{,}956
\end{aligned}
$$

The two components are **equal** (within rounding error), confirming optimality. ✔

**Additional Metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1^* &= \frac{Q^*}{P} = \frac{1{,}826}{25{,}000} = 0.073 \text{ year} \approx 26.7 \text{ days} \\[4pt]
I_{max}^* &= Q^*(1 - D/P) = 1{,}826 \times 0.6 \approx 1{,}096 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to compute $(1 - D/P)$ and using the classical EOQ formula. This yields **inflated $Q^*$** and **inflated total cost**. Maximum deduction: 4 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $T^*$ (cycle time) with $t_1^*$ (production time). Remember: $T^* = Q^*/D$ and $t_1^* = Q^*/P$. The machine is OFF for the duration $T^* - t_1^*$.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Not computing the holding cost as a fraction of unit cost when only the percentage is given. Always compute $C_2 = (\text{fraction}) \times (\text{unit cost})$ first.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Reporting $N^* = 5.48$ runs/year without rounding. In practice, take $N = 6$ and recompute $Q = D/N$, then verify cost. In KTU theory papers, fractional $N$ is acceptable as a continuous approximation.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to **square-root the entire RHS** when simplifying $Q^*$. Show all intermediate algebraic steps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ The EMQ model is the EMQ model is a **generalization of EOQ** for **finite production rate** $P$ with $P > D$.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2C_1D / [C_2(1 - D/P)]}$ — the only formula you absolutely must memorize.
- ✅ **Maximum inventory:** $I_{max} = Q(1 - D/P)$ — always less than $Q$.
- ✅ **Cycle time vs production time:** $T = Q/D$, $t_1 = Q/P$. The ratio $t_1/T = D/P$ is a useful sanity check.
- ✅ **At optimum, setup cost rate = holding cost rate** (universal property of EOQ-family models).
- ✅ **Holding cost calculation:** When given as a percentage, $C_2 = (\text{fraction}) \times (\text{unit cost})$.
- ✅ **Limiting case:** $P \to \infty$ recovers Wilson's EOQ — a frequently asked conceptual question.
- ✅ **No shortages** means inventory never goes negative; $C_3 = 0$ (no stock-out penalty term).
- ✅ **Number of setups per year:** $N^* = D/Q^*$; for whole numbers, round up to the next integer and adjust $Q$.
- ✅ **Diagram labeling:** Always annotate the inventory-vs-time graph with $I_{max}$, $T$, $t_1$, and $T - t_1$.
- ✅ **Units consistency:** If $D$ is in units/month and $C_2$ in ₹/unit/year, **convert** all rates to the same time basis before plugging into formulas.
- ✅ **Minimum cost formula:** $TC^* = \sqrt{2 C_1 C_2 D (1 - D/P)}$ — useful for quick cross-verification.
- ✅ **Assumption audit:** State all 7 assumptions clearly in any 7-mark or 14-mark answer that derives $Q^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Note:** The EMQ model is one of the **most frequently tested models** in KTU Module 3, alongside the classical EOQ and the model *with* shortages. Practice at least 4–5 numerical problems covering variations in $P$, $C_1$, $C_2$, and unit-cost-based holding costs to be fully exam-ready.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Purchasing Model With Shortages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Purchasing Model With Shortages ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Purchasing Model with Shortages** (also known as the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model with Backorders** or **EOQ Model with Planned Shortages**) is a deterministic inventory control model in which the decision-maker **deliberately allows inventory shortages** during a portion of the replenishment cycle. These unmet demands are **backordered** and fulfilled when the next lot arrives. The model seeks to determine the optimal order quantity **Q\*** and the maximum allowable shortage level **S\*** that jointly minimize the **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** — comprising **Ordering Cost, Holding (Carrying) Cost, and Shortage (Backorder) Cost**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3 – Inventory Control):** > Under the 2024 scheme, this topic falls under **deterministic continuous-review models**. The standard assumptions are: constant demand rate, infinite replenishment rate, instantaneous delivery, and *no quantity discounts*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you run a small grocery store that sells bottled water. The supplier delivers a fresh batch only when you place an order. Instead of ordering so much that your shelves are always full (which costs you storage space and insurance), you **intentionally let the stock hit zero for a few days** before reordering. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Newspaper Stall:** > A newspaper vendor does not store yesterday's papers. Customers who arrive after the stock is sold are served the next morning's lot. The vendor balances three costs: **(a)** the trip cost to the wholesaler, **(b)** the cost of keeping unsold papers (wastage / holding), and **(c)** the goodwill loss from turning customers away (shortage cost). The "sweet spot" is the order size that makes the *sum* of these three costs minimum. ### 1.3 Model Assumptions (KTU Standard List) | # | Assumption | Engineering Interpretation | |---|------------|----------------------------| | 1 | Demand rate **D** is known and constant | D units consumed per year | | 2 | Lead time is zero (instantaneous replenishment) | Stock arrives the moment order is placed | | 3 | Order cost **C₀** is fixed per order | Independent of order size | | 4 | Holding cost **Cₕ** per unit per unit time | Proportional to average inventory | | 5 | Shortage cost **Cₛ** per unit per unit time | Proportional to average backorder | | 6 | Shortages are allowed and backordered | Customers wait, no lost sales | | 7 | Planning horizon is infinite & continuous | Long-run steady-state analysis | > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metric:** The demand rate **D** in KTU problems is conventionally given in **units/year**. If given in monthly terms, it must be converted using **D_annual = D_monthly × 12**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Structure of One Inventory Cycle The replenishment cycle of length **T** is split into **two distinct phases**: 1. **Phase 1 — Inventory Build-up Phase (0 ≤ t ≤ t₁):** - Inventory rises linearly from **−S** (maximum backorder) to **+I_max** (maximum positive stock) at the instant the order arrives. - Stock is consumed at rate **D**, depleting linearly until it hits zero at time **t₁**. 2. **Phase 2 — Shortage Phase (t₁ ≤ t ≤ T):** - Inventory is zero (stock-out), but customers keep arriving. - Backorders accumulate from **0** at time **t₁** up to a maximum of **S** at time **T**. - The next replenishment instantaneously clears the backlog and starts Phase 1 of the next cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Why the two phases?** Holding cost is incurred only when positive stock exists; shortage cost is incurred only during the backorder phase. Splitting the cycle lets the analyst charge each cost to the correct sub-interval. ### 2.2 Cost Components — Conceptual Breakdown - **Ordering Cost per Cycle (OC):** Incurred exactly once per cycle → **OC = C₀** - **Holding Cost per Cycle (HC):** Average positive inventory × Cₕ × t₁ - **Shortage Cost per Cycle (SC):** Average backorder × Cₛ × t₂ The Total Cost per unit time (TC) is the sum of these divided by the cycle length **T = t₁ + t₂**. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Reference Table — Memorize for ESE (End Semester Examination).** | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | |---|----------|--------|---------| | 1 | Cycle length | T | $T = \dfrac{Q}{D} = t_1 + t_2$ | | 2 | Inventory build-up time | t₁ | $t_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0}{D C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}}$ | | 3 | Shortage duration | t₂ | $t_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0}{D C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}}$ | | 4 | Optimal Order Quantity | Q\* | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_0}{C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_h + C_s}{C_s}}$ | | 5 | Maximum Inventory | I_max | $I_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_0}{C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}}$ | | 6 | Maximum Backorder (Shortage) | S\* | $S^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_0}{C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}}$ | | 7 | Minimum Total Cost per Year | TC\* | $TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_h \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}}$ | | 8 | Number of orders per year | N\* | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D C_h}{2 C_0} \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}}$ | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Examiners almost always award a **2-mark bonus** if you express the answer in both the *basic form* and the *compact TC\* form*. Always present the final TC\* in the boxed compact form. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing:** Raw-material procurement where a small safety-stock buffer is too expensive. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Critical drugs where stock-outs are tolerable for a few days if the customer is willing to wait. - **Automotive Spare Parts:** Dealers intentionally backorder slow-moving parts to reduce showroom clutter. - **E-Commerce Warehousing:** Amazon-style fulfillment hubs use this model to balance warehouse rent vs. delivery-delay penalties. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Average Inventory and Average Backorder Let us define: - **Q** = order quantity (lot size) - **S** = maximum backorder level (shortage) - **D** = demand rate (units / unit time) - **I_max = Q − S** = maximum positive inventory at the start of cycle - **t₁** = time to clear the positive stock = I_max / D - **t₂** = time to accumulate backorder = S / D - **T = t₁ + t₂ = Q / D** **Step A — Average Positive Inventory over a cycle:** During the build-up phase, inventory drops linearly from **I_max** to **0**. The average over the triangle of base **t₁** and height **I_max** is: $$ \bar{I} = \dfrac{I_{max} + 0}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t_1}{T} = \dfrac{I_{max}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t_1}{T} $$ Substituting **I_max = Q − S** and **t₁/T = (Q − S)/Q** (using proportional time): $$ \bar{I} = \dfrac{(Q - S)^2}{2Q} $$ **Step B — Average Backorder over a cycle:** During the shortage phase, backorder rises linearly from **0** to **S**. The average over the triangle of base **t₂** and height **S** is: $$ \bar{B} = \dfrac{S}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t_2}{T} = \dfrac{S^2}{2Q} $$ ### 3.2 Total Cost per Unit Time Function The total cost per unit time is the sum of ordering, holding, and shortage costs per cycle, divided by **T = Q/D**: $$ TC(Q, S) = \underbrace{\dfrac{C_0 D}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{C_h \cdot \dfrac{(Q-S)^2}{2Q}}_{\text{Holding}} + \underbrace{C_s \cdot \dfrac{S^2}{2Q}}_{\text{Shortage}} $$ ### 3.3 Optimization — Partial Derivatives For optimal **Q** and **S**, take partial derivatives and set them to zero. **Step C — Differentiate w.r.t. S and set to zero:** $$ \dfrac{\partial TC}{\partial S} = C_h \cdot \dfrac{2(Q - S)(-1)}{2Q} + C_s \cdot \dfrac{2S}{2Q} = 0 $$ $$ -\dfrac{C_h (Q - S)}{Q} + \dfrac{C_s S}{Q} = 0 $$ Multiply both sides by **Q**: $$ -C_h (Q - S) + C_s S = 0 $$ $$ -C_h Q + C_h S + C_s S = 0 $$ $$ S(C_h + C_s) = C_h Q \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{S^* = \dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}\, Q^*} $$ **Step D — Substitute S\* back into TC and differentiate w.r.t. Q:** First compute **Q − S\***: $$ Q - S^* = Q - \dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}\,Q = \dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}\,Q $$ Now plug into **TC**: $$ TC(Q) = \dfrac{C_0 D}{Q} + \dfrac{C_h}{2Q}\left(\dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}\,Q\right)^2 + \dfrac{C_s}{2Q}\left(\dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}\,Q\right)^2 $$ $$ TC(Q) = \dfrac{C_0 D}{Q} + \dfrac{C_h C_s^2 \, Q}{2(C_h + C_s)^2} + \dfrac{C_s C_h^2 \, Q}{2(C_h + C_s)^2} $$ Factor the last two terms: $$ TC(Q) = \dfrac{C_0 D}{Q} + \dfrac{C_h C_s}{2(C_h + C_s)^2}\,(C_s + C_h)\,Q $$ $$ TC(Q) = \dfrac{C_0 D}{Q} + \dfrac{C_h C_s}{2(C_h + C_s)}\,Q $$ Now differentiate w.r.t. Q: $$ \dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{C_0 D}{Q^2} + \dfrac{C_h C_s}{2(C_h + C_s)} = 0 $$ $$ \dfrac{C_h C_s}{2(C_h + C_s)} = \dfrac{C_0 D}{Q^{*2}} $$ $$ Q^{*2} = \dfrac{2 C_0 D (C_h + C_s)}{C_h C_s} $$ $$ \boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0 D}{C_h} \cdot \dfrac{C_h + C_s}{C_s}}} $$ **Step E — Recover S\*, t₁, t₂, I_max, TC\* using Q\*:** $$ S^* = \dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}\,Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0 D}{C_h} \cdot \dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}} $$ $$ I_{max} = Q^* - S^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0 D}{C_h} \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}} $$ $$ t_1 = \dfrac{I_{max}}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0}{D C_h} \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}} $$ $$ t_2 = \dfrac{S^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0}{D C_h} \cdot \dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}} $$ $$ TC^* = \dfrac{C_0 D}{Q^*} + \dfrac{C_h C_s}{2(C_h + C_s)} Q^* = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_h \cdot \dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}} $$ ### 3.4 Limiting-Case Sanity Check (Board Favourite) | Condition | Behaviour | Reduces To | |-----------|-----------|-----------| | $C_s \to \infty$ | No shortages tolerated | Classical EOQ: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_0/C_h}$ | | $C_s = 0$ | Free shortage | $Q^* \to \infty$ (never order — always backorder) | | $C_h = 0$ | Free holding | $Q^* \to \infty$ (over-stock — never run out) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Verification Trick:** A correct answer must collapse to the classical EOQ when **C_s → ∞**. Always perform this check on the side margin of your answer sheet — examiners reward methodological rigour. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Inventory vs. Time Profile (Mermaid Plot Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Cycle["One Replenishment Cycle of Length T"] direction LR P1["Phase 1: Inventory Build-up
Duration t1
Stock falls from I_max to 0"] P2["Phase 2: Shortage
Duration t2
Backorder grows from 0 to S"] end O1["Order Placed
Stock jumps to I_max"] O2["Next Order Placed
Cycle Repeats"] O1 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> O2 O2 -.-> O1 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Cost Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Decision Variables
Q and S"] --> B["Compute t1 and t2
t1 = I_max / D
t2 = S / D"] B --> C["Cost Engine"] subgraph CostEngine["Cost Computation Module"] C1["Ordering Cost
Co D / Q"] C2["Holding Cost
Ch * I_avg"] C3["Shortage Cost
Cs * B_avg"] end C --> C1 C --> C2 C --> C3 C1 --> D["Total Cost TC"] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E{"Partial Derivatives
dTC/dQ = 0
dTC/dS = 0"} E -->|"Optimum Found"| F["Output:
Q-star, S-star, I-max, t1, t2, TC-star"] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Logic Flow (Algorithm Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Start: Given D, Co, Ch, Cs"]) --> P1["Read all four parameters"] P1 --> P2["Compute S-fact = Ch divided by Ch plus Cs"] P2 --> P3["Compute Q-star = sqrt of 2DCo over Ch times Ch plus Cs over Cs"] P3 --> P4["Compute S-star = S-fact times Q-star"] P4 --> P5["Compute I-max = Q-star minus S-star"] P5 --> P6["Compute t1 and t2 using D"] P6 --> P7["Compute TC-star using compact formula"] P7 --> Stop(["End: Print all optimal values"]) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Phase 1 occupies time **t₁** during which the on-hand inventory depletes from **I_max** down to **0**. Phase 2 occupies time **t₂** during which backorder accumulates from **0** up to **S**. The moment the next lot is delivered, the backorder **S** is cleared and the cycle restarts. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]** **State the assumptions of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model with shortages.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Demand rate is constant and known deterministically — **1 mark**. 2. Lead time is zero; replenishment is instantaneous — **1 mark**. 3. Order cost is fixed and shortage cost is linear in the backorder quantity — **1 mark**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** **Why is the classical EOQ model with no shortages a limiting case of the model with shortages?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** When the shortage cost **C_s → ∞**, the penalty for backordering becomes prohibitively large, so the model refuses to allow shortages. Substituting **C_s → ∞** in the formula $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_0/C_h \cdot (C_h + C_s)/C_s}$ yields $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_0/C_h}$, which is the **classical EOQ without shortages** — **3 marks** (1 for the substitution idea, 1 for the algebra, 1 for the conclusion). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. You must attempt **ONE full question** out of the internal choice pair. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted — Full Length Numerical]** A retail store sells an item at a constant demand rate of **10,000 units/year**. The ordering cost is **₹200 per order**, the holding cost is **₹6 per unit per year**, and the shortage cost is **₹4 per unit per year**. **(a) [7 marks]** Determine the optimal order quantity **Q\***, the maximum shortage level **S\***, and the maximum inventory **I_max**. **(b) [7 marks]** Compute the optimal cycle length **T\***, the inventory build-up time **t₁**, the shortage duration **t₂**, and the minimum total cost **TC\*** per year. --- #### **Solution to Question A** **Given:** D = 10,000 units/yr, C₀ = ₹200, C_h = ₹6/unit/yr, C_s = ₹4/unit/yr. **Part (a) Solution — Optimal Q\*, S\*, I_max** $$ Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_0}{C_h} \cdot \dfrac{C_h + C_s}{C_s}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10000 \times 200}{6} \cdot \dfrac{6 + 4}{4}} $$ **Step 1 — Compute the inner ratio:** $$ \dfrac{C_h + C_s}{C_s} = \dfrac{6 + 4}{4} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $2 D C_0 / C_h$:** $$ \dfrac{2 \times 10000 \times 200}{6} = \dfrac{4{,}000{,}000}{6} = 666{,}666.67 $$ **Step 3 — Multiply and take the square root:** $$ Q^* = \sqrt{666{,}666.67 \times 2.5} = \sqrt{1{,}666{,}666.67} \approx 1290.99 \approx \mathbf{1291 \text{ units}} $$ **Step 4 — Compute S\***: $$ S^* = \dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s}\,Q^* = \dfrac{6}{10} \times 1291 = 0.6 \times 1291 \approx \mathbf{774.6 \text{ units}} $$ **Step 5 — Compute I_max:** $$ I_{max} = Q^* - S^* = 1291 - 774.6 \approx \mathbf{516.4 \text{ units}} $$ **Part (b) Solution — T\*, t₁, t₂, TC\*** **Step 6 — Compute T\* = Q\*/D:** $$ T^* = \dfrac{1291}{10000} \text{ years} = 0.1291 \text{ years} = 0.1291 \times 365 \approx \mathbf{47.13 \text{ days}} $$ **Step 7 — Compute t₁ = I_max / D:** $$ t_1 = \dfrac{516.4}{10000} \text{ years} = 0.05164 \text{ years} = 0.05164 \times 365 \approx \mathbf{18.85 \text{ days}} $$ **Step 8 — Compute t₂ = S\* / D:** $$ t_2 = \dfrac{774.6}{10000} \text{ years} = 0.07746 \text{ years} = 0.07746 \times 365 \approx \mathbf{28.28 \text{ days}} $$ **Verification:** t₁ + t₂ = 18.85 + 28.28 ≈ 47.13 days ≈ T\* ✓ **Step 9 — Compute TC\* using the compact formula:** $$ TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_0 \cdot \dfrac{C_h C_s}{C_h + C_s}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10000 \times 200 \times \dfrac{6 \times 4}{10}} $$ $$ TC^* = \sqrt{4{,}000{,}000 \times 2.4} = \sqrt{9{,}600{,}000} \approx \mathbf{₹\ 3{,}098.4 \text{ per year}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative TC\* verification (full method):** > Ordering cost = 200 × (10000/1291) = ₹1549.2 > Holding cost = 6 × (516.4)²/(2 × 1291) = 6 × 266668.96/2582 = ₹619.7 > Shortage cost = 4 × (774.6)²/(2 × 1291) = 4 × 600000.516/2582 = ₹929.5 > **Sum ≈ ₹3098.4** ✓ (matches compact form) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]** A manufacturer uses **6,000 units/year** of a component. The cost of placing one order is **₹150**, the inventory carrying cost is **₹5 per unit per year**, and the shortage cost is **₹3 per unit per year**. **(a) [7 marks]** Find the EOQ when shortages are permitted and the corresponding maximum backorder level. **(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the optimal time between orders, the number of orders per year, and the minimum total annual cost. --- #### **Solution to Question B** **Given:** D = 6000 units/yr, C₀ = ₹150, C_h = ₹5, C_s = ₹3. **Part (a):** $$ Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 6000 \times 150}{5} \cdot \dfrac{5 + 3}{3}} = \sqrt{360000 \times \dfrac{8}{3}} = \sqrt{360000 \times 2.6667} = \sqrt{960000} \approx \mathbf{979.8 \text{ units}} $$ $$ S^* = \dfrac{5}{8} \times 979.8 = 0.625 \times 979.8 \approx \mathbf{612.4 \text{ units}} $$ $$ I_{max} = 979.8 - 612.4 = \mathbf{367.4 \text{ units}} $$ **Part (b):** $$ T^* = \dfrac{979.8}{6000} \text{ years} = 0.1633 \text{ years} = 0.1633 \times 365 \approx \mathbf{59.6 \text{ days}} $$ $$ N^* = \dfrac{6000}{979.8} \approx \mathbf{6.124 \text{ orders/year}} $$ $$ TC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 6000 \times 150 \times \dfrac{5 \times 3}{8}} = \sqrt{1800000 \times 1.875} = \sqrt{3{,}375{,}000} \approx \mathbf{₹\ 1{,}837.4 \text{ per year}} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid these to secure full 14/14):** > 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** If demand is in *units/month*, students forget to convert to *units/year* before applying the formula. Always check the time-base of **D** first. **−1 mark** > 2. **Ratio Inversion Error:** Some students write $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DC_0}{C_s} \cdot \frac{C_s}{C_h + C_s}}$, which collapses to the **basic EOQ with C_s in the wrong place**. Re-derive the formula from **∂TC/∂S = 0** on your rough sheet to confirm. **−2 marks** > 3. **t₁ + t₂ Verification Omission:** Examiners give a bonus if you verify that **t₁ + t₂ = T\*** numerically. Skipping this is a small but visible mark loss. **−1 mark** > 4. **Cost-Component Sum Verification:** Always cross-check TC\* by separately computing Ordering + Holding + Shortage cost and summing. If the two methods disagree, one of your algebraic steps has an error. **−2 marks** > 5. **Confusion between Q and I_max:** Students often report **Q\*** as the "maximum inventory" — they are different. **I_max = Q\* − S\***. **−1 mark** --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Definition:** The Purchasing Model with Shortages allows backorders and optimizes **Q\*** and **S\*** jointly. - **Two-phase cycle:** Inventory phase (t₁) followed by shortage phase (t₂). - **Core Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_0}{C_h} \cdot \dfrac{C_h + C_s}{C_s}}$ — the **canonical KTU board formula**. - **Companion Formula:** $S^* = \dfrac{C_h}{C_h + C_s} \cdot Q^*$. - **Maximum Inventory:** $I_{max} = Q^* - S^* = \dfrac{C_s}{C_h + C_s} \cdot Q^*$. - **Minimum Total Cost:** $TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_0 \cdot \dfrac{C_h C_s}{C_h + C_s}}$ — the **single most important compact result**. - **Limiting Case:** As $C_s \to \infty$, the model **collapses to the classical EOQ** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_0/C_h}$. - **Demand Rate Convention:** Always use **D in units/year**; convert monthly to annual by multiplying by 12. - **Optimal Number of Orders:** $N^* = D / Q^*$. - **Backorder Cost Logic:** A higher **C_s** penalizes stock-outs more, **reducing S\*** and **increasing I_max**. - **Sanity Check Rule:** Always verify $t_1 + t_2 = T^*$ numerically before submitting.

topics.title: Manufacturing Model With Shortages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Model With Shortages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Manufacturing Model With Shortages (Production Lot Sizing with Backorders)

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Model with Shortages (EMQ with Backorders / Production Lot Size Model with Planned Shortages)** is a deterministic inventory model in which the firm produces a lot of size $Q$ at a finite rate $P$ (where $P > D$) and **deliberately allows a portion $Q_1$ of the demand to be backordered** (shortage/backorder) during each production cycle. The remaining $(Q - Q_1)$ units are held as inventory. The backorder is fulfilled at the start of the next production run.

**Parameters involved:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $D$ | Annual demand rate | units/year |
| $P$ | Production rate | units/year, with $P > D$ |
| $K$ | Setup (ordering) cost per run | ₹/setup |
| $h$ | Holding (carrying) cost | ₹/unit/year |
| $s$ | Shortage (backorder) cost | ₹/unit/year |
| $Q$ | Lot size produced per cycle | units |
| $Q_1$ | Backorder quantity per cycle | units |
| $I_{\max}$ | Maximum inventory | units |
| $B_{\max}$ | Maximum backorder | units |
| $T$ | Cycle length | year |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🛒

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bakery that Bakes Tomorrow's Bread Today**
>
> Imagine a small bakery in Kochi that bakes `P` loaves per day but sells only `D` loaves per day. If the baker insists on *never* running out of bread, she must bake smaller, more frequent batches — wasting oven setup time. However, the baker realises that loyal customers are happy to **pre-order and wait one day**. So she allows a *backlog* of $Q_1$ loaves: customers book today, she bakes tomorrow. This deliberate shortage:
> - Reduces the number of production runs (saving setup cost $K$),
> - Keeps inventory lower (saving holding cost $h$),
> - But incurs a small penalty $s$ per backordered loaf (customer goodwill loss).
>
> The optimal policy balances these three competing costs. **This is exactly the Manufacturing Model with Shortages.**

## 1.3 Standard Assumptions

1. Demand rate $D$ is **uniform and known** (continuous review).
2. Production rate $P$ is **finite and constant**, with $P > D$.
3. Shortages are **permissible** and are backlogged (not lost sales).
4. Lead time is **zero**; replenishment is instantaneous at the start of a production run.
5. Holding cost $h$ and shortage cost $s$ are **linear** in the respective quantities.
6. Setup cost $K$ is **fixed** per production run, independent of lot size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inventory Behaviour Over One Cycle

A single production cycle has **three distinct phases**:

| Phase | Duration | Inventory Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phase 1 — Backorder Build-up** | $t_3 = \dfrac{Q_1}{D}$ | No production; backorders accumulate at rate $D$ from $0$ up to $B_{\max}=Q_1$ |
| **Phase 2 — Production Run** | $t_1 = \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | Production at rate $P$, demand at rate $D$; net build-up at rate $(P-D)$ |
| **Phase 3 — Inventory Depletion** | $t_2 = \dfrac{Q - Q_1}{D}$ | No production; inventory falls from $I_{\max}$ to $0$ at rate $D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During **Phase 2** (production), the first $Q_1$ units produced clear the entire backlog, and the next $(Q-Q_1)$ units build inventory up to $I_{\max}$.

**Cycle time:** $T = t_2 + t_3 = \dfrac{Q - Q_1}{D} + \dfrac{Q_1}{D} = \dfrac{Q}{D}$

## 2.2 Cost Structure

The **total variable cost per cycle** is the sum of three components:

1. **Setup cost** — incurred once per cycle:
$$C_{\text{setup}} = K$$

2. **Holding cost** — average inventory $(I_{\max}/2)$ held for time $t_2$:
$$C_{\text{hold}} = h \cdot \frac{I_{\max}}{2} \cdot t_2 = \frac{h(Q - Q_1)^2}{2D}$$

3. **Shortage cost** — average backorder $(Q_1/2)$ held for time $t_3$:
$$C_{\text{short}} = s \cdot \frac{Q_1}{2} \cdot t_3 = \frac{s Q_1^2}{2D}$$

**Total cost per unit time** (i.e., annual total cost):
$$\text{TCU}(Q, Q_1) = \frac{D}{Q}\left[K + \frac{h(Q - Q_1)^2}{2D} + \frac{s Q_1^2}{2D}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{TCU}(Q, Q_1) = \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{h(Q - Q_1)^2}{2Q} + \frac{s Q_1^2}{2Q}\;}$$

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table — these are the high-yield expressions that appear in **every** KTU question on this topic.

| Quantity | Optimal Formula | Expression in $(K, D, h, s)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Optimal lot size $Q^{*}$ | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2KD}{h}\cdot\dfrac{h+s}{s}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2KD(h+s)}{hs}}$ |
| Optimal backorder $Q_1^{*}$ | $Q_1^{*} = \dfrac{h}{h+s}\,Q^{*}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2KDh}{s(h+s)}}$ |
| Maximum inventory $I_{\max}^{*}$ | $I_{\max}^{*} = \dfrac{s}{h+s}\,Q^{*}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2KDs}{h(h+s)}}$ |
| Maximum backorder $B_{\max}^{*}$ | $B_{\max}^{*} = Q_1^{*}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2KDh}{s(h+s)}}$ |
| Optimal cycle time $T^{*}$ | $T^{*} = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{D}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2K(h+s)}{Dhs}}$ |
| Production time $t_1^{*}$ | $t_1^{*} = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{P}$ | $\dfrac{1}{P}\sqrt{\dfrac{2KD(h+s)}{hs}}$ |
| Minimum TCU$^{*}$ | $\text{TCU}^{*} = \sqrt{2KD}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{hs}{h+s}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2KDhs}{h+s}}$ |
| Ratio $Q_1^{*}/Q^{*}$ | $\dfrac{h}{h+s}$ | — |
| Ratio $I_{\max}^{*}/Q^{*}$ | $\dfrac{s}{h+s}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** As $s \to \infty$ (i.e., shortages are *very* expensive), $Q_1^{*} \to 0$ and $Q^{*} \to \sqrt{2KD/h}$, which is the **classical EMQ without shortages**. Conversely, as $h \to \infty$ (i.e., inventory is *very* expensive), the firm backorders almost everything. This limiting behaviour is a favourite KTU sub-question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This model is used in **batch production industries** (pharmaceuticals, FMCG, electronics, automobile spares) where:
- Production is **continuous-flow** rather than instantaneous (so $P$ is finite).
- Customers accept **deferred delivery** (industrial B2B orders).
- The goal is to minimise the joint cost of *setup + holding + shortage*.
- Compared to the no-shortage EMQ, it **reduces the maximum inventory level by a factor of $s/(h+s)$**, freeing up working capital and warehouse space.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations of the Optimal Policies

## 3.1 Derivation of $Q_1^{*}$ in terms of $Q$ (the **Horizontal Tangent**)

We have
$$\text{TCU}(Q, Q_1) = \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{h(Q - Q_1)^2}{2Q} + \frac{s Q_1^2}{2Q}.$$

Treat $Q$ as fixed and differentiate w.r.t. $Q_1$:
$$\frac{\partial \text{TCU}}{\partial Q_1} = \frac{h \cdot 2(Q - Q_1)(-1)}{2Q} + \frac{s \cdot 2 Q_1}{2Q} \cdot 1 - \frac{s Q_1^2}{2Q^2}\cdot 0.$$

Using the **quotient rule** on the third term is unnecessary since $Q_1$ appears only in the numerator there (with $Q$ held). Simplifying the first two terms:
$$\frac{\partial \text{TCU}}{\partial Q_1} = -\frac{h(Q - Q_1)}{Q} + \frac{s Q_1}{Q}.$$

Setting $\partial \text{TCU}/\partial Q_1 = 0$ gives the **first-order condition**:
$$-h(Q - Q_1) + s Q_1 = 0.$$

Expand:
$$-hQ + h Q_1 + s Q_1 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (h+s)Q_1 = hQ.$$

Hence the **optimal backorder fraction** is:
$$\boxed{\;Q_1^{*} = \frac{h}{h+s}\,Q\;}\qquad\text{(Eq. A)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of $Q^{*}$ (the **Vertical Tangent**)

Substitute Eq. (A) back into the total cost. Note that
$$Q - Q_1^{*} = Q - \frac{h}{h+s}Q = \frac{s}{h+s}Q.$$

Plug into TCU:
$$\text{TCU} = \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{h}{2Q}\left(\frac{s}{h+s}Q\right)^{\!2} + \frac{s}{2Q}\left(\frac{h}{h+s}Q\right)^{\!2}.$$

Expand the squares:
$$= \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{h}{2Q}\cdot\frac{s^2 Q^2}{(h+s)^2} + \frac{s}{2Q}\cdot\frac{h^2 Q^2}{(h+s)^2}.$$

Cancel $Q$ in the last two fractions:
$$= \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{s^2 h\, Q}{2(h+s)^2} + \frac{s h^2\, Q}{2(h+s)^2}.$$

Factor $\dfrac{sh\,Q}{2(h+s)^2}$ from the last two terms:
$$= \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{sh\,Q}{2(h+s)^2}\,(s + h).$$

Since $(s+h)$ cancels one factor of $(h+s)^2$:
$$\text{TCU} = \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{sh\,Q}{2(h+s)}.$$

Now differentiate w.r.t. $Q$:
$$\frac{d\,\text{TCU}}{dQ} = -\frac{DK}{Q^2} + \frac{sh}{2(h+s)}.$$

Set to zero:
$$\frac{DK}{Q^2} = \frac{sh}{2(h+s)} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad Q^2 = \frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh}.$$

Taking the positive square root:
$$\boxed{\;Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh}}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of $Q_1^{*}$ and $I_{\max}^{*}$

Using Eq. (A):
$$Q_1^{*} = \frac{h}{h+s}\sqrt{\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh}}.$$

Pull $\sqrt{1/(h+s)}$ out:
$$Q_1^{*} = \frac{h}{h+s}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{h+s}}\sqrt{\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh}}\cdot\sqrt{h+s}.$$

Easier: square the expression first and simplify:
$$Q_1^{*2} = \frac{h^2}{(h+s)^2}\cdot\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh} = \frac{2DKh}{(h+s)\cdot s}.$$

So:
$$\boxed{\;Q_1^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DKh}{s(h+s)}}\;}$$

Similarly,
$$I_{\max}^{*} = Q^{*} - Q_1^{*} = \frac{s}{h+s}Q^{*}.$$

Squaring and simplifying:
$$I_{\max}^{*2} = \frac{s^2}{(h+s)^2}\cdot\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh} = \frac{2DKs}{h(h+s)}.$$

$$\boxed{\;I_{\max}^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DKs}{h(h+s)}}\;}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Minimum TCU$^{*}$

Substitute $Q^{*}$ into $\text{TCU} = \dfrac{DK}{Q} + \dfrac{shQ}{2(h+s)}$:
$$\text{TCU}^{*} = \frac{DK}{\sqrt{2DK(h+s)/(sh)}} + \frac{sh}{2(h+s)}\sqrt{\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh}}.$$

Let $X = \sqrt{2DK(h+s)/(sh)}$ for brevity.
$$\text{TCU}^{*} = \frac{DK}{X} + \frac{sh}{2(h+s)}\,X.$$

But $DK/X = DK \cdot \sqrt{sh/(2DK(h+s))} = \sqrt{D^2K^2 \cdot sh/(2DK(h+s))} = \sqrt{DKsh/(2(h+s))}$.

And the second term:
$$\frac{sh}{2(h+s)}\sqrt{\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh}} = \sqrt{\frac{s^2h^2}{4(h+s)^2}\cdot\frac{2DK(h+s)}{sh}} = \sqrt{\frac{DKsh}{2(h+s)}}.$$

Both terms are equal; adding them:
$$\text{TCU}^{*} = 2\sqrt{\frac{DKsh}{2(h+s)}} = \sqrt{\frac{4DKsh}{2(h+s)}} = \sqrt{\frac{2DKsh}{h+s}}.$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{TCU}^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DKsh}{h+s}}\;}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data:** $D = 10{,}000$ units/yr, $P = 25{,}000$ units/yr, $K = \text{₹}\,2{,}000$, $h = \text{₹}\,10$/unit/yr, $s = \text{₹}\,15$/unit/yr.

**Step 1 — Compute $Q^{*}$.**
$$Q^{*2} = \frac{2 \times 2000 \times 10000 \times (10+15)}{10 \times 15} = \frac{4 \times 10^{7} \times 25}{150} = \frac{10^9}{150}.$$
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{6.6\overline{6}\times 10^6} \approx 2581.99 \text{ units}.$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_1^{*}$.**
$$Q_1^{*2} = \frac{2 \times 2000 \times 10000 \times 10}{15 \times 25} = \frac{4\times 10^8}{375} = 1.0\overline{6}\times 10^6.$$
$$Q_1^{*} \approx 1032.80 \text{ units}.$$

**Step 3 — Compute $I_{\max}^{*}$.**
$$I_{\max}^{*} = Q^{*} - Q_1^{*} = 2581.99 - 1032.80 \approx 1549.19 \text{ units.}$$

**Cross-check using the alternate formula:**
$$I_{\max}^{*2} = \frac{2\times 2000\times 10000\times 15}{10 \times 25} = \frac{6\times 10^8}{250} = 2.4\times 10^6 \;\Rightarrow\; I_{\max}^{*}\approx 1549.19.\;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute cycle time $T^{*}$.**
$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{2581.99}{10000} \approx 0.2582 \text{ yr } \approx 14.18 \text{ weeks.}$$

**Step 5 — Compute production time $t_1^{*}$.**
$$t_1^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{P} = \frac{2581.99}{25000} \approx 0.1033 \text{ yr } \approx 5.67 \text{ weeks.}$$

**Step 6 — Compute minimum TCU$^{*}$.**
$$\text{TCU}^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2000 \times 10000 \times 10 \times 15}{10+15}} = \sqrt{\frac{6\times 10^9}{25}} = \sqrt{2.4\times 10^8} = \text{₹}\,15{,}491.93/\text{year.}$$

**Step 7 — Cost breakdown at optimum (verification).**
- Setup cost per year: $\dfrac{D}{Q^{*}}K = \dfrac{10000}{2581.99}\times 2000 \approx \text{₹}\,7{,}745.97.$
- Holding cost per year: $\dfrac{h(I_{\max}^{*})^2}{2Q^{*}} = \dfrac{10\times (1549.19)^2}{2\times 2581.99} \approx \text{₹}\,4{,}645.78.$
- Shortage cost per year: $\dfrac{s(Q_1^{*})^2}{2Q^{*}} = \dfrac{15\times (1032.80)^2}{2\times 2581.99} \approx \text{₹}\,3{,}100.18.$
- Sum $\approx \text{₹}\,15{,}491.93.\;\checkmark$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Notice that at the optimum, the **setup cost alone** is roughly **50 %** of the total cost, while holding and shortage share the remainder. This is the universal "square-root law" of inventory theory: optimum occurs when *marginal* setup cost equals *marginal* combined holding + shortage cost.

## 3.6 Comparison with the No-Shortage EMQ

| Metric | No-Shortage EMQ | With Shortages | Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lot size $Q$ | $\sqrt{2KD/h}$ | $\sqrt{2KD(h+s)/(hs)}$ | **Larger** (by factor $\sqrt{(h+s)/s}$) |
| Max inventory | $Q/2$ | $\sqrt{2KDs/(h(h+s))}$ | **Smaller** |
| Min TCU | $\sqrt{2KDh}$ | $\sqrt{2KDhs/(h+s)}$ | **Smaller** |
| Production time | $Q/P$ | $Q/P$ | Same fraction, larger $Q$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check for the examiner:** *Allowing shortages always reduces the minimum total cost* — provided the shortage cost $s$ is finite. The percentage saving is
> $$\frac{\text{TCU}_{\text{no-short}}^{*} - \text{TCU}_{\text{short}}^{*}}{\text{TCU}_{\text{no-short}}^{*}} = 1 - \sqrt{\frac{s}{h+s}} \;\times\; 100\%.$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory–Time Profile (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Cycle Start: Backorder Exists Bmax = Q1"] --> B["Phase 3: Backorder Build-up t3 = Q1 / D"]
    B --> C["Phase 1: Production Run t1 = Q / P, Net Build rate P minus D"]
    C --> D["Imax Reached: Q minus Q1 Units in Stock"]
    D --> E["Phase 2: Inventory Depletion t2 = Imax / D"]
    E --> F["Inventory Reaches Zero"]
    F --> G["Cycle End: Same State as Start"]
    G --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the **closed-loop cyclic nature** of the system. Production begins exactly when backorders reach $Q_1$, and ends exactly when inventory peaks at $I_{\max}$. The cycle then repeats.

## 4.2 Decision-Flow for Solving a Manufacturing-With-Shortages Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Read D, P, K, h, s"] --> S2["Verify P greater than D and s greater than zero"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute factor f = h plus s divided by h times s"]
    S3 --> S4["Q star = sqrt of 2 K D f"]
    S4 --> S5["Q1 star = h divided by h plus s times Q star"]
    S5 --> S6["Imax star = s divided by h plus s times Q star"]
    S6 --> S7["T star = Q star divided by D"]
    S7 --> S8["t1 star = Q star divided by P"]
    S8 --> S9["TCU star = sqrt of 2 K D h s divided by h plus s"]
    S9 --> S10["Output: List Q star, Q1 star, Imax star, T star, t1 star, TCU star"]
```

## 4.3 Cost-Component Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs["Decision Variables"]
        Q["Lot Size Q"]
        Q1["Backorder Q1"]
    end
    subgraph Costs["Annual Cost Components"]
        C1["Setup Cost: DK / Q"]
        C2["Holding Cost: h (Q minus Q1) squared divided by 2Q"]
        C3["Shortage Cost: s Q1 squared divided by 2Q"]
    end
    subgraph Sum["Minimisation"]
        Obj["TCU equals C1 plus C2 plus C3"]
    end
    Q --> C1
    Q --> C2
    Q1 --> C2
    Q --> C3
    Q1 --> C3
    Q --> C1
    C1 --> Obj
    C2 --> Obj
    C3 --> Obj
    Obj --> Opt["Apply dTCU/dQ equals 0 and dTCU/dQ1 equals 0"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison of With-Shortage vs No-Shortage EMQ

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Production Lot Size Model Family"] --> B["EMQ with Instantaneous Replenishment"]
    A --> C["EMQ with Finite Production Rate"]
    B --> D["Allowing Shortages? NO"]
    B --> E["Allowing Shortages? YES"]
    C --> F["Allowing Shortages? NO"]
    C --> G["Allowing Shortages? YES — THIS TOPIC"]
    G --> H["Q star = sqrt 2KD h plus s over h s"]
    G --> I["Q1 star = h over h plus s times Q star"]
    G --> J["TCU star = sqrt 2KDhs over h plus s"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the assumptions of the Manufacturing Model with Shortages. How does it differ from the basic EOQ model?**

**Model Answer:**
The manufacturing model with shortages assumes: (i) demand rate $D$ is constant and known, (ii) production rate $P$ is finite and exceeds $D$ ($P > D$), (iii) shortages are permitted and **backlogged** (not lost), (iv) replenishment is instantaneous, and (v) holding cost $h$ and shortage cost $s$ are linear.
It differs from the basic EOQ in two key ways: (a) replenishment is at a *finite rate* $P$ rather than instantaneous, and (b) shortages are *allowed* up to $Q_1$ units per cycle, with a penalty cost $s$ per unit short. These two features together give the $Q^{*}$ and $Q_1^{*}$ formulas with the $(h+s)$ and $s$ terms that are absent in classical EOQ.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is the optimal backorder quantity $Q_1^{*}$ less than the optimal lot size $Q^{*}$? What happens when $s \to \infty$?**

**Model Answer:**
$Q_1^{*} < Q^{*}$ because the firm never wants to backorder the entire lot — keeping the remaining $(Q - Q_1)$ as positive inventory protects against excessive shortage penalties. The ratio is $Q_1^{*}/Q^{*} = h/(h+s)$, which is always less than 1.
As $s \to \infty$, the shortage cost becomes prohibitive, $Q_1^{*} \to 0$, and the model **degenerates into the classical EMQ without shortages** ($Q^{*} \to \sqrt{2KD/h}$), with $I_{\max}^{*} = Q^{*}/2$. This is the limiting behaviour: when backordering is forbidden or infinitely costly, the firm produces only enough to meet demand with a small buffer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - **Don't forget the $(h+s)$ factor.** The most common error is writing $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2KD/h}$ (no-shortage formula) when shortages are allowed. *Always* use $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2KD(h+s)/(hs)}$.
> - **Confusion between $Q_1^{*}$ and $I_{\max}^{*}$.** They look symmetric but contain $h$ and $s$ in *different places*. Use $Q_1^{*} = hQ^{*}/(h+s)$ and $I_{\max}^{*} = sQ^{*}/(h+s)$; verify their sum equals $Q^{*}$.
> - **Forgetting units / per-year basis.** All costs must be expressed *annually*; if the question gives a weekly demand, convert to yearly using $D_{\text{year}} = 52 \times D_{\text{week}}$.
> - **Stopping at the formula.** KTU requires substitution of values and a final *numerical* answer in part (a) and (b). Always show step-by-step arithmetic.

### 📘 Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]**

> A firm produces a component at the rate $P = 4{,}000$ units/year. The demand is $D = 1{,}000$ units/year, setup cost $K = \text{₹}\,200$ per run, holding cost $h = \text{₹}\,4$ per unit per year, and shortage cost $s = \text{₹}\,8$ per unit per year.
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the optimal lot size $Q^{*}$, the maximum inventory $I_{\max}^{*}$, and the maximum backorder $B_{\max}^{*}$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Find the optimum cycle time, the production time per cycle, and the minimum total annual cost.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

Given: $D = 1{,}000$, $P = 4{,}000$, $K = 200$, $h = 4$, $s = 8$ (all annual).

**Step 1 — Compute $(h+s)$ and $hs$:**
$$h + s = 4 + 8 = 12, \qquad hs = 4 \times 8 = 32.$$

**Step 2 — Optimal lot size $Q^{*}$:**
$$Q^{*2} = \frac{2KD(h+s)}{hs} = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 1000 \times 12}{32} = \frac{4{,}800{,}000}{32} = 150{,}000.$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{150{,}000} \approx 387.30 \text{ units.}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum inventory $I_{\max}^{*}$:**
$$I_{\max}^{*2} = \frac{2KDs}{h(h+s)} = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 1000 \times 8}{4 \times 12} = \frac{3{,}200{,}000}{48} = 66{,}666.67.$$

$$I_{\max}^{*} = \sqrt{66{,}666.67} \approx 258.20 \text{ units.}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum backorder $B_{\max}^{*}$:**
$$B_{\max}^{*2} = \frac{2KDh}{s(h+s)} = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 1000 \times 4}{8 \times 12} = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{96} = 16{,}666.67.$$

$$B_{\max}^{*} = \sqrt{16{,}666.67} \approx 129.10 \text{ units.}$$

**Cross-check:** $I_{\max}^{*} + B_{\max}^{*} = 258.20 + 129.10 = 387.30 = Q^{*}.\;\checkmark$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 5 — Optimum cycle time $T^{*}$:**
$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{387.30}{1000} = 0.3873 \text{ year} \approx 20.14 \text{ weeks.}$$

**Step 6 — Production time $t_1^{*}$:**
$$t_1^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{P} = \frac{387.30}{4000} = 0.0968 \text{ year} \approx 5.03 \text{ weeks.}$$

**Step 7 — Minimum total annual cost:**
$$\text{TCU}^{*2} = \frac{2KDhs}{h+s} = \frac{2\times 200\times 1000 \times 4 \times 8}{12} = \frac{12{,}800{,}000}{12} = 1{,}066{,}666.67.$$

$$\text{TCU}^{*} = \sqrt{1{,}066{,}666.67} \approx \text{₹}\,1{,}032.80 \text{ per year.}$$

**Cost breakdown for verification:**
- Setup/yr: $\dfrac{1000}{387.30}\times 200 \approx \text{₹}\,516.40.$
- Holding/yr: $\dfrac{4 \times (258.20)^2}{2 \times 387.30} \approx \text{₹}\,344.27.$
- Shortage/yr: $\dfrac{8 \times (129.10)^2}{2 \times 387.30} \approx \text{₹}\,172.13.$
- Total $\approx \text{₹}\,1{,}032.80.\;\checkmark$

---

### 📗 Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Restated]**

> A manufacturing company has demand $D = 5{,}000$ units/year, production rate $P = 20{,}000$ units/year, setup cost $K = \text{₹}\,1{,}000$, holding cost $h = \text{₹}\,20$/unit/yr, and shortage cost $s = \text{₹}\,5$/unit/yr.
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Find the optimal production quantity $Q^{*}$, the optimal backorder level $Q_1^{*}$, and the maximum inventory.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the length of the production run, the cycle time, and the minimum total cost. Also comment on the percentage saving in total cost compared to the no-shortage EMQ.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Auxiliary quantities:**
$$h+s = 25,\qquad hs = 100,\qquad \frac{h+s}{hs} = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25.$$

**Step 2 — Optimal lot size $Q^{*}$:**
$$Q^{*2} = 2 \times 1000 \times 5000 \times 0.25 = 2{,}500{,}000.$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{2{,}500{,}000} = 1{,}581.14 \text{ units.}$$

**Step 3 — Optimal backorder $Q_1^{*}$:**
$$Q_1^{*} = \frac{h}{h+s}\,Q^{*} = \frac{20}{25}\times 1581.14 = 0.8 \times 1581.14 \approx 1{,}264.91 \text{ units.}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum inventory $I_{\max}^{*}$:**
$$I_{\max}^{*} = Q^{*} - Q_1^{*} = 1581.14 - 1264.91 \approx 316.23 \text{ units.}$$

*(Cross-check via the direct formula: $I_{\max}^{*2} = 2KDs/[h(h+s)] = 2\times 1000\times 5000\times 5/(20\times 25) = 100{,}000$, $I_{\max}^{*} = 316.23\;\checkmark$)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 5 — Production run length $t_1^{*}$:**
$$t_1^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{P} = \frac{1581.14}{20000} = 0.0791 \text{ yr } \approx 4.11 \text{ weeks.}$$

**Step 6 — Cycle time $T^{*}$:**
$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{1581.14}{5000} = 0.3162 \text{ yr } \approx 16.44 \text{ weeks.}$$

**Step 7 — Minimum total cost:**
$$\text{TCU}^{*2} = \frac{2KDhs}{h+s} = \frac{2 \times 1000 \times 5000 \times 20 \times 5}{25} = \frac{10^9}{25} = 4 \times 10^7.$$

$$\text{TCU}^{*} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^7} = \text{₹}\,6{,}324.56 \text{ per year.}$$

**Step 8 — Comparison with no-shortage EMQ:**

No-shortage TCU$_{\text{no-short}}^{*} = \sqrt{2KDh} = \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times 5000 \times 20} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^8} = \text{₹}\,14{,}142.14.$

Percentage saving:
$$\Delta\% = \frac{14{,}142.14 - 6{,}324.56}{14{,}142.14}\times 100\% = 55.28\%.$$

> **Engineering comment:** Allowing shortages reduces annual cost by approximately **55 %** in this case, because the shortage cost $s$ is small relative to the holding cost $h$. If the ratio $s/h$ were small, the firm would plan for substantial backlogging.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

- **Core variables:** $Q$ (lot size), $Q_1$ (backorder), $I_{\max} = Q - Q_1$ (max inventory).
- **Cycle structure:** $T = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = Q/D$, where $t_1 = Q/P$, $t_2 = (Q - Q_1)/D$, $t_3 = Q_1/D$.
- **TCU function:** $\text{TCU} = \dfrac{DK}{Q} + \dfrac{h(Q - Q_1)^2}{2Q} + \dfrac{s Q_1^2}{2Q}.$
- **Optimal backorder rule:** $Q_1^{*} = \dfrac{h}{h+s}\,Q^{*}$ — derived from the $Q_1$-FOC, *no* need to remember the closed form.
- **Optimal lot size:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2KD(h+s)}{hs}}$ — has the $\sqrt{(h+s)/s}$ multiplier over the classical EMQ.
- **Min cost:** $\text{TCU}^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2KDhs}{h+s}}$.
- **Limiting cases:**
  - $s \to \infty$: $Q_1^{*} \to 0$, model reduces to **no-shortage EMQ** ($Q^{*} = \sqrt{2KD/h}$).
  - $h \to \infty$: firm backorders almost everything ($Q_1^{*} \to Q^{*}$).
- **Production rate $P$** does **not** appear in the optimal $Q^{*}$, $Q_1^{*}$, or TCU$^{*}$. It only affects the **production-time** $t_1 = Q^{*}/P$.
- **Sanity checks for full marks:** (i) $Q^{*} = Q_1^{*} + I_{\max}^{*}$, (ii) setup cost = holding cost + shortage cost at the optimum (in the single-variable reduced form).
- **KTU marking weightage (typical):** Part A — 3 marks × 2 = 6 marks; Part B — 14 marks × 1 = 14 marks (internal choice). Part (a) and (b) of Part B carry 7 marks each, with 1 mark for formula and 6 for substitution + answer.
- **Common mistakes:** (a) using the no-shortage formula, (b) forgetting the $(h+s)$ factor, (c) mixing up $Q_1$ and $I_{\max}$, (d) mis-converting weekly to annual data, (e) not verifying the answer with the cost-component sum.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision mantra for KTU:**
> *"Square the Q, multiply by $hs$, divide by $(h+s)$, multiply by $2KD$ — square root gives $Q^{*}$. Multiply $Q^{*}$ by $h/(h+s)$ for $Q_1^{*}$. The rest is automatic."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPM and PERT - Time Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CPM and PERT - Time Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition: CPM and PERT Time Estimation

**Critical Path Method (CPM)** is a deterministic project management technique that uses a **single, fixed time estimate** for every activity in a project network. The critical path is the **longest-duration path** through the network, and it determines the minimum project completion time.

**Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)** is a probabilistic/stochastic project management technique that uses **three time estimates** per activity, modeled using the **Beta Probability Distribution**, to account for uncertainty in activity durations.

Both techniques are network-based scheduling tools used to plan, schedule, coordinate, and control complex engineering projects. The "time estimation" step is the foundation upon which all subsequent forward/backward pass calculations, critical path identification, and crashing decisions are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Time estimation is the *core input* to all CPM/PERT calculations. In CPM we use $t_e$ (single value), while in PERT we compute a *weighted expected time* $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ from three estimates.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Road Trip Planning" Intuition

Imagine you are planning a road trip from Kochi to Delhi with multiple stops (Coimbatore → Bengaluru → Hyderabad → Nagpur → Delhi).

- **CPM Analogy:** You have done this trip 10 times. You *know* Coimbatore takes 5 hours, Bengaluru takes 8 hours, etc. You have **single, confident numbers**. Your total travel time is simply the sum along the longest route.
- **PERT Analogy:** This is your **first road trip**, and your friends give you three estimates for each leg: *best-case* (no traffic, smooth road), *most-likely* (normal day), *worst-case* (monsoon + jam). You cannot pick just one — you blend them into a single *expected* time using the formula $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$.

The "critical path" is the chain of legs that, if delayed by even an hour, delays your **entire arrival in Delhi**. All other legs have *slack* — they can be late without affecting the final arrival.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Activity (A)** | A time-consuming task represented by an arrow in the network |
| **Event (Node)** | A milestone/point in time (start or end of an activity), represented by a circle |
| **Predecessor** | An activity that must finish before another can start |
| **Successor** | An activity that cannot start until the current one ends |
| **Dummy Activity** | A zero-duration logical connector used to maintain correct precedence |
| **EST (Earliest Start Time)** | The earliest time an activity can begin, considering all predecessors |
| **EFT (Earliest Finish Time)** | $EFT = EST + t_e$ |
| **LST (Latest Start Time)** | The latest time an activity can begin without delaying the project |
| **LFT (Latest Finish Time)** | $LFT = LST + t_e$ |
| **Critical Path** | Longest path; activities with **zero total float** |
| **Optimistic Time ($t_o$)** | Minimum possible duration if everything goes perfectly |
| **Most Likely Time ($t_m$)** | Modal estimate under normal conditions |
| **Pessimistic Time ($t_p$)** | Maximum possible duration under worst conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention (KTU):** All times are measured in **days** unless otherwise specified in the problem. Critical Path variance is computed in **days²** and standard deviation in **days**.

## 1.4 Difference Between CPM and PERT at a Glance

| Feature | CPM | PERT |
|---------|-----|------|
| Time estimates per activity | One (deterministic) | Three (probabilistic) |
| Distribution assumed | None | Beta |
| Focus | Time-Cost trade-off | Time-Probability analysis |
| Use case | Construction, manufacturing | R&D, research projects |
| Developed by | DuPont (1957) | U.S. Navy (1958) |
| Crashing possible | Yes | No (not standard) |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three PERT Time Estimates — Deconstructed

For every activity $i$, three estimates are collected from historical data or expert judgment:

1. **Optimistic Time ($t_{o,i}$):** Duration when *all* goes right. Probability of occurrence is very low (~1 in 100).
2. **Most Likely Time ($t_{m,i}$):** Duration under *normal/expected* conditions. This is the mode of the Beta distribution.
3. **Pessimistic Time ($t_{p,i}$):** Duration when *all* goes wrong (bad weather, breakdown, etc.). Probability is very low (~1 in 100).

### Why the Beta Distribution?

The Beta distribution is chosen because:

- It is bounded between two finite positive limits (a, b), perfectly modeling $t_o$ and $t_p$.
- It is **unimodal** (single peak), matching $t_m$.
- It is **flexible** in shape (skewed left, skewed right, or symmetric).
- The mean and variance of Beta have *simple closed-form* expressions in terms of $t_o, t_m, t_p$, which makes calculations tractable for hand-solved KTU problems.

## 2.2 The Two Foundational PERT Formulas

### (A) Expected Time of an Activity

$$t_{e,i} = \frac{t_{o,i} + 4 \cdot t_{m,i} + t_{p,i}}{6}$$

**Why the weight 4 on $t_m$?** In the Beta distribution, the relationship between the mean $\mu$, mode $M$, and endpoints $(a, b)$ is:

$$\mu = \frac{a + 4M + b}{6}$$

Since the optimistic estimate approximates $a$, the most-likely approximates the mode $M$, and the pessimistic approximates $b$, the weights 1, 4, 1 arise naturally from Beta's mathematical structure.

### (B) Variance of an Activity

$$\sigma_{i}^{2} = \left( \frac{t_{p,i} - t_{o,i}}{6} \right)^{2}$$

**Why the divisor 6?** For a Beta distribution, the standard deviation is approximately one-sixth of the range $(b - a)$. This is a heuristic approximation that is statistically valid under the assumption of moderate variability (range not extreme).

## 2.3 Forward Pass — Earliest Event Times

Working from the **start node** to the **end node**:

$$E_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} \left( E_i + t_{e,ij} \right)$$

where $E_j$ is the earliest occurrence time of event $j$ and $t_{e,ij}$ is the expected duration of activity $(i \to j)$.

For activity $A_{ij}$:
- $EST_{ij} = E_i$ (earliest event time of the start node)
- $EFT_{ij} = EST_{ij} + t_{e,ij} = E_j$ (by definition of $E_j$)

## 2.4 Backward Pass — Latest Event Times

Working from the **end node** back to the **start node**:

$$L_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} \left( L_j - t_{e,ij} \right)$$

For activity $A_{ij}$:
- $LFT_{ij} = L_j$ (latest event time of the end node)
- $LST_{ij} = LFT_{ij} - t_{e,ij}$

The project is **feasible in time** if and only if $E_{\text{start}} = L_{\text{start}} = 0$ and $E_{\text{end}} = L_{\text{end}} = T_E$ (the expected project duration).

## 2.5 Float (Slack) Calculations

### Total Float ($TF$)
The amount of time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the project completion date*:

$$TF_{ij} = LST_{ij} - EST_{ij} = LFT_{ij} - EFT_{ij} = L_j - E_j$$

### Free Float ($FF$)
The amount of time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the early start of any successor*:

$$FF_{ij} = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(A_{ij})} EST_{ik} - EFT_{ij} = \min E_k - E_j$$

### Independent Float ($IF$)
The amount of time an activity can be delayed *assuming all predecessors are at their latest times and all successors at their earliest times*:

$$IF_{ij} = FF_{ij} - TF_{\text{predecessor critical}} = \max\left(0,\, \min E_k - L_i - t_{e,ij}\right)$$

### Interfering Float
$$IF_{\text{total}} = TF_{ij} - FF_{ij}$$

## 2.6 Critical Path Identification

An activity is on the **Critical Path** if and only if **$TF_{ij} = 0$** (equivalently, $E_j = L_j$ for both its endpoints).

The Critical Path is the chain of zero-float activities from start to end, and its length equals the **expected project duration** $T_E$.

## 2.7 Probability of Meeting a Scheduled Date (PERT Exclusive)

Given a scheduled completion time $T_s$, the probability of finishing the project by $T_s$ is found by:

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}}$$

where:
- $T_E$ = expected project duration (sum of $t_e$ along critical path)
- $\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_{i}^{2}}$ (square root of *sum* of variances on critical path)
- $Z$ = standard normal variable

Then $P(T \le T_s) = \Phi(Z)$, looked up in the Standard Normal Table.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in 95% of KTU ESE problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Purpose | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | Expected time per activity | days |
| 2 | $\sigma^{2} = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^{2}$ | Variance per activity | days² |
| 3 | $E_j = \max(E_i + t_{e,ij})$ | Forward pass (EST at node) | days |
| 4 | $L_i = \min(L_j - t_{e,ij})$ | Backward pass (LFT at node) | days |
| 5 | $TF = LST - EST$ | Total float | days |
| 6 | $FF = \min E_{\text{succ}} - EFT$ | Free float | days |
| 7 | $IF = \max(0, \text{FF} - \text{TF pred})$ | Independent float | days |
| 8 | $T_E = \sum_{\text{CP}} t_e$ | Project expected duration | days |
| 9 | $\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{\sum_{\text{CP}} \sigma^{2}}$ | Critical path std deviation | days |
| 10 | $Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}}$ | Probability calculation | dimensionless |
| 11 | $P(T \le T_s) = \Phi(Z)$ | Probability of on-time finish | probability |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Projects:** CPM is used to schedule high-rise buildings, bridges, and highways; crashing analysis tells the engineer the cheapest way to accelerate delivery.
- **Aerospace & Defense R&D:** PERT is used because R&D activity durations are highly uncertain.
- **Software Engineering:** Agile-Scrum uses PERT-like estimation (optimistic, most-likely, pessimistic in the "PERT" sizing of story points).
- **Manufacturing:** PERT charts are used in supply chain rollouts to identify bottleneck stations.
- **Shutdown Maintenance:** Refinery turnarounds use CPM to schedule hundreds of interlinked maintenance activities in a 30-day window.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the PERT Expected Time Formula

### Starting Point — The Beta Distribution

The Beta probability density function is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} (x - a)^{\alpha - 1} (b - x)^{\beta - 1}, \quad a \le x \le b$$

where $\alpha, \beta > 1$ ensure a unimodal shape, and $B(\alpha, \beta)$ is the Beta function.

### The Mean (Expected Value) of Beta

The mean of a Beta distribution is:

$$\mu = a + \frac{\alpha (b - a)}{\alpha + \beta}$$

The mode (most likely value) is:

$$M = a + \frac{(\alpha - 1)(b - a)}{\alpha + \beta - 2}, \quad \alpha, \beta > 1$$

### Eliminating the Parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$

We can solve for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in terms of the mean $\mu$ and mode $M$:

$$\alpha = \frac{(\mu - a)(2M - a - b)}{(M - \mu)(b - a)}$$

$$\beta = \frac{(b - \mu)(2M - a - b)}{(M - \mu)(b - a)}$$

### The Clever Substituion

Now substitute $a = t_o$, $b = t_p$, $M = t_m$, and $\mu = t_e$ into the expressions for $\alpha$ and $\beta$, then solve for $t_e$ in terms of $t_o, t_m, t_p$. After algebraic manipulation:

$$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$

The 4 in the numerator is a **direct consequence of the Beta distribution's mode-mean relationship** — it is *not* an arbitrary choice. The derivation shows it comes from the algebraic elimination of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ when $M$ is at the center of $[a, b]$.

### Derivation of Variance

For a Beta distribution, the variance is:

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{\alpha \beta (b - a)^{2}}{(\alpha + \beta)^{2}(\alpha + \beta + 1)}$$

For the symmetric-ish case where $\alpha + \beta$ is large (typical of project estimates), this simplifies to the standard PERT approximation:

$$\sigma^{2} \approx \left( \frac{b - a}{6} \right)^{2} = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^{2}$$

The "$\div 6$" comes from the empirical observation that the standard deviation of activity durations is roughly one-sixth of their full range. This is the standard **PERT heuristic**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full Forward/Backward Pass

### Problem Setup

A small project has the following activities and expected durations (assume CPM, single time per activity for clarity of the pass mechanics):

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration $t_e$ (days) |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| A | — | 6 |
| B | — | 4 |
| C | A | 3 |
| D | A | 7 |
| E | B, C | 5 |
| F | D | 4 |
| G | E, F | 3 |

### Network (Conceptual Layout)
- Start → A, B (A and B start from node 1)
- A → C, D (Node 2)
- B → E (Node 3)
- C → E (Node 2 to Node 3)
- D → F (Node 2 to Node 4)
- E → G (Node 3 to Node 5)
- F → G (Node 4 to Node 5)
- G → End (Node 5 to Node 6)

### Step 1 — Forward Pass (Compute $E_i$)

Start at Node 1: $E_1 = 0$

**Node 2** (reached by A): $E_2 = E_1 + t_A = 0 + 6 = 6$

**Node 3** (reached by B and C; take max):
$$E_3 = \max(E_1 + t_B,\; E_2 + t_C) = \max(0 + 4,\; 6 + 3) = \max(4, 9) = 9$$

**Node 4** (reached by D):
$$E_4 = E_2 + t_D = 6 + 7 = 13$$

**Node 5** (reached by E and F; take max):
$$E_5 = \max(E_3 + t_E,\; E_4 + t_F) = \max(9 + 5,\; 13 + 4) = \max(14, 17) = 17$$

**Node 6** (reached by G):
$$E_6 = E_5 + t_G = 17 + 3 = 20$$

**Project Duration:** $T_E = 20$ days.

### Step 2 — Backward Pass (Compute $L_i$)

Start at End: $L_6 = E_6 = 20$

**Node 5:**
$$L_5 = L_6 - t_G = 20 - 3 = 17$$

**Node 4** (only F goes out):
$$L_4 = L_5 - t_F = 17 - 4 = 13$$

**Node 3** (only E goes out):
$$L_3 = L_5 - t_E = 17 - 5 = 12$$

**Node 2** (C goes to Node 3, D goes to Node 4; take min):
$$L_2 = \min(L_3 - t_C,\; L_4 - t_D) = \min(12 - 3,\; 13 - 7) = \min(9, 6) = 6$$

**Node 1:**
$$L_1 = \min(L_2 - t_A,\; L_3 - t_B) = \min(6 - 6,\; 12 - 4) = \min(0, 8) = 0$$

**Feasibility Check:** $L_1 = 0$ ✓ (matches $E_1 = 0$).

### Step 3 — Float and Critical Path

| Activity | $i \to j$ | $EST = E_i$ | $EFT$ | $LST$ | $LFT = L_j$ | $TF$ | Critical? |
|----------|-----------|-------------|--------|--------|--------------|------|-----------|
| A | 1→2 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | **0** | ✓ |
| B | 1→3 | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 8 | ✗ |
| C | 2→3 | 6 | 9 | 9 | 12 | 3 | ✗ |
| D | 2→4 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | **0** | ✓ |
| E | 3→5 | 9 | 14 | 12 | 17 | 3 | ✗ |
| F | 4→5 | 13 | 17 | 13 | 17 | **0** | ✓ |
| G | 5→6 | 17 | 20 | 17 | 20 | **0** | ✓ |

**Critical Path: A → D → F → G**, with total duration $6 + 7 + 4 + 3 = 20$ days. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example — PERT Three-Estimate Probability

### Problem
Consider a PERT network with the critical path consisting of activities having the following three-time estimates (in days):

| Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|
| P | 2 | 3 | 10 |
| Q | 1 | 4 | 7 |
| R | 3 | 5 | 13 |
| S | 2 | 2 | 8 |

**Find:** Probability of completing the project within 20 days.

### Step 1 — Expected Time for Each Activity

$$t_{e,P} = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,Q} = \frac{1 + 4(4) + 7}{6} = \frac{1 + 16 + 7}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,R} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,S} = \frac{2 + 4(2) + 8}{6} = \frac{2 + 8 + 8}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}$$

### Step 2 — Expected Project Duration

$$T_E = t_{e,P} + t_{e,Q} + t_{e,R} + t_{e,S} = 4 + 4 + 6 + 3 = 17 \text{ days}$$

### Step 3 — Variance of Each Activity

$$\sigma_P^{2} = \left(\frac{10 - 2}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{9} \approx 1.778$$

$$\sigma_Q^{2} = \left(\frac{7 - 1}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{6}{6}\right)^{2} = 1^{2} = 1$$

$$\sigma_R^{2} = \left(\frac{13 - 3}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{25}{9} \approx 2.778$$

$$\sigma_S^{2} = \left(\frac{8 - 2}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{6}{6}\right)^{2} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Critical Path Standard Deviation

Variances add along the critical path (independent activities):

$$\sigma_{CP}^{2} = \sigma_P^{2} + \sigma_Q^{2} + \sigma_R^{2} + \sigma_S^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{CP}^{2} = \frac{16}{9} + 1 + \frac{25}{9} + 1 = \frac{16 + 9 + 25 + 9}{9} = \frac{59}{9}$$

$$\sigma_{CP}^{2} \approx 6.556 \text{ days}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{6.556} \approx 2.561 \text{ days}$$

### Step 5 — Z-value and Probability

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}} = \frac{20 - 17}{2.561} = \frac{3}{2.561} \approx 1.17$$

From Standard Normal Table: $\Phi(1.17) \approx 0.8790$

**Result:** $P(T \le 20) \approx 0.879$ or **87.9%**

**Interpretation:** There is about an 88% chance the project will be completed within 20 days.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always show the Z-lookup step explicitly. Writing "from tables" without quoting the value loses 1 mark. State $\Phi(Z) = $ the value and what you are concluding.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CPM/PERT Computation

The following block diagram shows the *flow of data* through a typical CPM/PERT computational pipeline — from raw inputs to the final probability report.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Activity Data Input] --> A1[Activity List with Predecessors]
    A1 --> A2[Time Estimates: t_o, t_m, t_p or single t]
    A2 --> B1{Is PERT or CPM?}
    B1 -- CPM --> C1[Use single t as t_e]
    B1 -- PERT --> C2[Compute t_e and sigma squared per activity]
    C1 --> D1[Build Network AOA or AON]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Forward Pass: compute EST and EFT at each node]
    E1 --> F1[Identify Project Duration T_E]
    F1 --> G1[Backward Pass: compute LST and LFT]
    G1 --> H1[Compute Total Float for each activity]
    H1 --> I1[Critical Path: activities with TF equals zero]
    I1 --> J1{Is PERT analysis needed?}
    J1 -- No --> K1[Output: Schedule, Floats, CP]
    J1 -- Yes --> L1[Sum sigma squared along CP]
    L1 --> M1[Compute sigma equals sqrt of variance sum]
    M1 --> N1[Compute Z for given T_s]
    N1 --> O1[Look up Phi of Z in Normal Table]
    O1 --> P1[Output: Probability of on-time completion]
```

**Block Descriptions:**

- **Block 1 (Inputs):** Activity list, precedence table, time estimates.
- **Block 2 (Time Conversion):** Converts PERT three-estimates into $t_e, \sigma^2$ per activity.
- **Block 3 (Network Build):** Constructs the AOA (Activity on Arrow) or AON (Activity on Node) network with dummies if needed.
- **Block 4 (Forward Pass):** Computes earliest times.
- **Block 5 (Backward Pass):** Computes latest times.
- **Block 6 (Critical Path):** Filters zero-float activities.
- **Block 7 (PERT Probability):** Adds critical path variance, computes Z, and looks up probability.

## 4.2 Network Topology Diagram for Worked Example (Section 3.2)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1((1\nE=0 L=0)) -- A:6 --> node2((2\nE=6 L=6))
    node1 -- B:4 --> node3((3\nE=9 L=12))
    node2 -- C:3 --> node3
    node2 -- D:7 --> node4((4\nE=13 L=13))
    node3 -- E:5 --> node5((5\nE=17 L=17))
    node4 -- F:4 --> node5
    node5 -- G:3 --> node6((6\nE=20 L=20))
    style node1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node6 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node2 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style node4 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style node5 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
```

**Critical Path Highlighted (in red):** 1 → 2 → 4 → 5 → 6, traced by activities A → D → F → G with combined duration $6 + 7 + 4 + 3 = 20$ days.

**Non-Critical Activities (with float):**
- B has total float 8 days.
- C has total float 3 days.
- E has total float 3 days.

## 4.3 PERT Beta Distribution Visualization (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[t_o Optimistic Lower Bound] --> R[Beta Distribution PDF]
    Y[t_m Most Likely Mode Peak] --> R
    Z[t_p Pessimistic Upper Bound] --> R
    R --> M1[Mean t_e equals weighted average]
    R --> M2[Variance sigma squared]
    M1 --> OUT[Project Schedule]
    M2 --> P[Probability of Completion by T_s]
```

This block diagram illustrates the information flow: the three estimates $t_o, t_m, t_p$ define the Beta distribution, from which the expected time and variance are extracted, which in turn drive the schedule and probability computations.
