# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q:** Define the following terms in a PERT network: (i) Optimistic time, (ii) Most likely time, (iii) Pessimistic time. State the formula for expected time and variance of an activity.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Optimistic time ($t_o$):** The minimum possible duration of an activity if *everything* goes perfectly — no delays, no failures, no bad luck. It is the lower bound of the Beta distribution.

(ii) **Most likely time ($t_m$):** The duration of an activity under *normal/expected* working conditions. It corresponds to the mode (peak) of the Beta distribution.

(iii) **Pessimistic time ($t_p$):** The maximum possible duration if *everything* goes wrong — bad weather, breakdowns, severe delays. It is the upper bound of the Beta distribution.

**Expected time formula:**
$$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$

**Variance formula:**
$$\sigma^{2} = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^{2}$$

*(3 marks — 1 each for definitions + 0.5 each for the two formulas is the standard split.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is a critical path? How is it identified in a network diagram?

**Model Answer:**

A **critical path** is the *longest path* through a project network — the chain of activities that determines the *minimum* project completion time. Any delay along this path delays the entire project.

**Identification Procedure:**
1. Perform the **forward pass** to find the earliest event times $E_i$ at every node.
2. Perform the **backward pass** to find the latest event times $L_i$ at every node.
3. Compute the **total float** $TF = L_j - E_j$ for each activity $A_{ij}$.
4. Activities with $TF = 0$ lie on the critical path.

Equivalently, the critical path consists of activities for which $EST = LST$ and $EFT = LFT$ (no scheduling flexibility).

The length of the critical path equals the **expected project duration** $T_E$.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B Module questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. The question has two sub-parts typically of 7 marks each. Below we provide **Option A** and **Option B** as independent alternatives.

---

### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q:** A small construction project consists of 7 activities with the following precedence relationships and time estimates (in days):

| Activity | Predecessors | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ |
|----------|--------------|-------|-------|-------|
| A | — | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| B | — | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| C | A | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| D | A | 2 | 3 | 10 |
| E | B, C | 4 | 5 | 12 |
| F | D | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| G | E, F | 2 | 4 | 6 |

**(a)** Construct the network, compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity, and determine the expected project duration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify the critical path, find total and free float for each activity, and calculate the probability of completing the project in **22 days**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Compute $t_e$ for each activity.**

$$t_{e,A} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,B} = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,C} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,D} = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,E} = \frac{4 + 4(5) + 12}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,F} = \frac{1 + 4(3) + 5}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,G} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\sigma^2$ for each activity.**

$$\sigma_A^{2} = \left(\frac{6-2}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{9}$$

$$\sigma_B^{2} = \left(\frac{3-1}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{9}$$

$$\sigma_C^{2} = \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{9}$$

$$\sigma_D^{2} = \left(\frac{10-2}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{9}$$

$$\sigma_E^{2} = \left(\frac{12-4}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{9}$$

$$\sigma_F^{2} = \left(\frac{5-1}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{9}$$

$$\sigma_G^{2} = \left(\frac{6-2}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{9}$$

**Step 3: Network Construction and Forward/Backward Pass.**

- Node 1 (Start): $E_1 = 0, L_1 = 0$
- A goes 1→2, B goes 1→3
- C goes 2→3, D goes 2→4
- E goes 3→5 (from B and C), F goes 4→5 (from D)
- G goes 5→6 (from E and F)

**Forward Pass:**
$$E_2 = 0 + 4 = 4$$
$$E_3 = \max(E_1 + t_B,\; E_2 + t_C) = \max(0 + 2,\; 4 + 5) = 9$$
$$E_4 = E_2 + t_D = 4 + 4 = 8$$
$$E_5 = \max(E_3 + t_E,\; E_4 + t_F) = \max(9 + 6,\; 8 + 3) = \max(15, 11) = 15$$
$$E_6 = E_5 + t_G = 15 + 4 = 19$$

**Expected Project Duration:** $T_E = 19$ days.

**Backward Pass:**
$$L_6 = 19$$
$$L_5 = 19 - 4 = 15$$
$$L_4 = 15 - 3 = 12$$
$$L_3 = 15 - 6 = 9$$
$$L_2 = \min(L_3 - t_C,\; L_4 - t_D) = \min(9 - 5,\; 12 - 4) = \min(4, 8) = 4$$
$$L_1 = \min(L_2 - t_A,\; L_3 - t_B) = \min(4 - 4,\; 9 - 2) = \min(0, 7) = 0 \;\; ✓$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Float Table:**

| Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF | FF | Critical? |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|----|-----------|
| A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | 0 | ✓ |
| B | 0 | 2 | 7 | 9 | 7 | 7 | ✗ |
| C | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | **0** | 0 | ✓ |
| D | 4 | 8 | 8 | 12 | 4 | 0 | ✗ |
| E | 9 | 15 | 9 | 15 | **0** | 0 | ✓ |
| F | 8 | 11 | 12 | 15 | 4 | 4 | ✗ |
| G | 15 | 19 | 15 | 19 | **0** | 0 | ✓ |

**Critical Path: A → C → E → G**, total duration $4 + 5 + 6 + 4 = 19$ days ✓.

**Critical Path Variance:**
$$\sigma_{CP}^{2} = \sigma_A^{2} + \sigma_C^{2} + \sigma_E^{2} + \sigma_G^{2} = \frac{4}{9} + \frac{4}{9} + \frac{16}{9} + \frac{4}{9} = \frac{28}{9} \approx 3.111$$

$$\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{3.111} \approx 1.764 \text{ days}$$

**Probability of Completion in 22 Days:**

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}} = \frac{22 - 19}{1.764} = \frac{3}{1.764} \approx 1.70$$

From Standard Normal Table: $\Phi(1.70) \approx 0.9554$

**Answer:** $P(T \le 22) \approx 0.955$ or **95.5%**

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A project has the following activities with CPM (single-time) durations:

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1-2 | — | 8 |
| 1-3 | — | 6 |
| 2-4 | 1-2 | 4 |
| 3-4 | 1-3 | 3 |
| 3-5 | 1-3 | 7 |
| 4-5 | 2-4, 3-4 | 2 |
| 4-6 | 2-4, 3-4 | 5 |
| 5-6 | 3-5, 4-5 | 4 |

**(a)** Draw the network and perform the forward and backward pass. Compute the earliest and latest event times at each node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the critical path, total float, free float, and independent float for each activity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Network Construction.**
Nodes 1 to 6. Edges as per the activity list.

**Step 2: Forward Pass (compute $E_i$).**

$E_1 = 0$

$E_2 = E_1 + t_{1,2} = 0 + 8 = 8$

$E_3 = E_1 + t_{1,3} = 0 + 6 = 6$

$E_4 = \max(E_2 + t_{2,4},\; E_3 + t_{3,4}) = \max(8 + 4,\; 6 + 3) = \max(12, 9) = 12$

$E_5 = \max(E_3 + t_{3,5},\; E_4 + t_{4,5}) = \max(6 + 7,\; 12 + 2) = \max(13, 14) = 14$

$E_6 = \max(E_4 + t_{4,6},\; E_5 + t_{5,6}) = \max(12 + 5,\; 14 + 4) = \max(17, 18) = 18$

**Step 3: Backward Pass (compute $L_i$).**

$L_6 = E_6 = 18$

$L_5 = L_6 - t_{5,6} = 18 - 4 = 14$

$L_4 = \min(L_6 - t_{4,6},\; L_5 - t_{4,5}) = \min(18 - 5,\; 14 - 2) = \min(13, 12) = 12$

$L_3 = \min(L_5 - t_{3,5},\; L_4 - t_{3,4}) = \min(14 - 7,\; 12 - 3) = \min(7, 9) = 7$

$L_2 = L_4 - t_{2,4} = 12 - 4 = 8$

$L_1 = \min(L_2 - t_{1,2},\; L_3 - t_{1,3}) = \min(8 - 8,\; 7 - 6) = \min(0, 1) = 0$ ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Float Table:**

| Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF | FF | IF | Critical? |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|----|-----|-----------|
| 1-2 | 0 | 8 | 0 | 8 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 1-3 | 0 | 6 | 1 | 7 | 1 | 0 | 0 | ✗ |
| 2-4 | 8 | 12 | 8 | 12 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 3-4 | 6 | 9 | 9 | 12 | 3 | 3 | 2 | ✗ |
| 3-5 | 6 | 13 | 7 | 14 | 1 | 1 | 0 | ✗ |
| 4-5 | 12 | 14 | 12 | 14 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 4-6 | 12 | 17 | 13 | 18 | 1 | 1 | 0 | ✗ |
| 5-6 | 14 | 18 | 14 | 18 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✓ |

**Sample Free Float Calculation (Activity 1-3):**
Successor is event 4 (via 3-4) and event 5 (via 3-5).
$$FF_{1,3} = \min(E_4, E_5) - EFT_{1,3} = \min(12, 14) - 6 = 12 - 6 = 6$$

Wait — let me recompute. The free float is $\min E_{\text{successor node}} - EFT_{1,3}$. Successor nodes of 1-3 are 4 and 5.

$$FF_{1,3} = \min(E_4, E_5) - E_3 - t_{1,3} = \min(12, 14) - 6 = 12 - 6 = 6$$

Hmm, that gives 6 not 0. Let me reconcile: the **earliest start** of activity 3-4 is $E_3 = 6$, and $EFT_{1,3} = 6$, so the slack is 0. The convention is $FF = E_{\text{succ}} - E_i - t_{1,3}$ where $E_{\text{succ}}$ is the earliest event time of the *immediate* successor node reached via that activity.

For activity 1-3 → node 3, but 1-3 ENDS at node 3. So $FF_{1,3}$ is the minimum of the earliest start of *activities leaving node 3*, minus $EFT_{1,3}$:
- Activity 3-4 starts at $E_3 = 6$ → contributes $6 - 6 = 0$
- Activity 3-5 starts at $E_3 = 6$ → contributes $6 - 6 = 0$

So $FF_{1,3} = 0$ (as in the table). ✓

**Critical Path: 1-2 → 2-4 → 4-5 → 5-6**, total duration $8 + 4 + 2 + 4 = 18$ days. ✓

**Project Duration:** $T_E = 18$ days.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing EST with LST:** EST is from the forward pass, LST from the backward pass. Mixing them up reverses the sign of float.
> 2. **Forgetting to take MAX in forward pass, MIN in backward pass:** Many students add all predecessor EFTs — wrong! Take the MAX for forward, MIN for backward.
> 3. **Variance on non-critical activities:** Students sometimes include non-critical path variances. Only the *critical path* variances are summed for $\sigma_{CP}$.
> 4. **Z-table lookup direction:** For $P(T \le T_s)$ with $T_s > T_E$, $Z$ is positive. For $T_s < T_E$, $Z$ is negative. Use the *left-tail* value $\Phi(Z)$.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert "weeks" to "days" (or vice versa):** KTU problems often give times in different units. Read carefully and be consistent.
> 6. **Not showing units:** $t_e$ in days, $\sigma^2$ in days², $\sigma$ in days. Examiners deduct marks for missing units in tables.
> 7. **Independent Float formula:** A common mistake is computing $IF$ without the $\max(0, \cdot)$ safeguard. IF cannot be negative — if the formula gives a negative number, the answer is 0.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **CPM** uses **one** time estimate per activity; **PERT** uses **three**.
- [x] PERT expected time: $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ — always weighted 1:4:1.
- [x] PERT variance: $\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2$ — uses the range, not the mode.
- [x] The "1, 4, 1" weights come from the **Beta distribution's mean–mode relationship**, not an arbitrary choice.
- [x] Forward pass: $E_j = \max(E_i + t_{e,ij})$ — start to end.
- [x] Backward pass: $L_i = \min(L_j - t_{e,ij})$ — end to start.
- [x] Float = slack = $LST - EST = LFT - EFT$.
- [x] **Critical Path** = chain of activities with $TF = 0$ = longest path = determines $T_E$.

### Float Hierarchy
- [x] **Total Float (TF):** Delay without delaying project end. Computed from $L - E$.
- [x] **Free Float (FF):** Delay without delaying any successor's early start. Computed from $\min E_{\text{succ}} - EFT$.
- [x] **Independent Float (IF):** Delay assuming predecessors are at *latest* and successors at *earliest*. Computed as $FF - TF_{\text{pred}}$.
- [x] Always $IF \le FF \le TF$ for any given activity.
- [x] Negative $IF$ is set to 0.

### Probability Calculation
- [x] Sum variances **only on the critical path** to get $\sigma_{CP}^2$.
- [x] $\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{\sigma_{CP}^2}$.
- [x] $Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}}$.
- [x] $P(T \le T_s) = \Phi(Z)$ — read from left-tail of normal table.
- [x] $T_s > T_E \Rightarrow Z > 0 \Rightarrow P > 0.5$.
- [x] $T_s < T_E \Rightarrow Z < 0 \Rightarrow P < 0.5$.

### Common Network Conventions
- [x] **AOA** (Activity on Arrow): arrows = activities, nodes = events. Dummy arrows used for logic.
- [x] **AON** (Activity on Node): boxes = activities, arrows = dependencies. No dummies needed.
- [x] KTU prefers AOA for problems with dummies; AON for clean precedence lists.
- [x] Dummy activity has zero duration and zero cost; it only encodes logic.
- [x] **No two activities can share the same start and end nodes** in AOA without a dummy.

### Exam-Day Memory Aids
- [x] If a path has more than one critical value, **sum** the variances (they are independent).
- [x] If asked "probability project finishes in X days," it is *always* $\le$ (cumulative), not $\ge$.
- [x] $E_1 = L_1 = 0$ and $E_{\text{end}} = L_{\text{end}}$ are mandatory **consistency checks**.
- [x] KTU gives a **Standard Normal Table** in the question paper — learn to use it, not memorize values.
- [x] Final answer should include: project duration, critical path, list of floats, probability (if PERT).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Path, Basic Concepts of Crashing. Replacement Problems: Replacement of Assets That Deteriorate with Time, Replacement of Assets That Fail Completely
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT417 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Critical Path, Basic Concepts of Crashing. Replacement Problems: Replacement of Assets That Deteriorate with Time, Replacement of Assets That Fail Completely

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Critical Path Method (CPM) — The Backbone of Project Scheduling

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** is a deterministic project management technique that identifies the longest sequence of dependent activities in a project network. The path with the **maximum total duration** is the **Critical Path (CP)**, and activities on this path are termed **Critical Activities**. Any delay in a critical activity directly delays the entire project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Critical Path always has **Slack = 0**. Project completion time equals the length of the Critical Path.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine planning a wedding. You can book the venue, send invitations, and order the cake in parallel. But the actual wedding ceremony can only happen *after* all three are done. The *chain of activities* that takes the longest time dictates when the wedding starts. That chain is your **critical path** — every minute of delay here delays the wedding itself.

**Key Metrics in CPM:**

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Earliest Start Time | $E_j$ | Earliest an activity $j$ can begin |
| Earliest Finish Time | $E_j + t_{ij}$ | Earliest an activity $j$ can complete |
| Latest Start Time | $L_j$ | Latest an activity $j$ can begin without delay |
| Latest Finish Time | $L_j$ | Latest an activity $j$ can finish without delay |
| Total Float / Slack | $S_{ij}$ | $L_j - E_j - t_{ij}$ |
| Free Float | $F_{ij}$ | $E_k - E_j - t_{ij}$ |

---

### 1.2 Project Crashing — Speeding Up at a Price

**Formal Definition:**
> **Crashing** is the deliberate compression of a project schedule by allocating additional resources (overtime labor, extra machines, etc.) to critical activities to reduce their duration. The **Cost Slope** determines how much extra money must be paid per unit time saved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of riding an express train vs. a regular train. To reach your destination faster (crash the journey), you pay more for tickets (cost slope). The fastest train that still fits your budget is the **optimal crashed duration**.

**Cost Slope Formula:**
The standard engineering metric used by KTU examiners:

$$
\text{Cost Slope} = \frac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}
$$

where $C_c$ = Crash cost, $C_n$ = Normal cost, $T_n$ = Normal time, $T_c$ = Crash time. Units: **Currency per unit time**.

---

### 1.3 Replacement Problems — Engineering Asset Management

**Formal Definition:**
> **Replacement Problems** deal with the decision of *when* to replace existing equipment/machinery with new ones, balancing the competing costs of **maintenance (rising with age)** versus **replacement (a one-time capital cost)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Distinct KTU Categories:**
> 1. **Replacement of Assets That Deteriorate with Time** — Gradual decline (e.g., a vehicle whose mileage increases, tire wear).
> 2. **Replacement of Assets That Fail Completely (Suddenly)** — Sudden failure (e.g., light bulbs, electronic components).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Owning a car is like having a deteriorating asset. As the car ages, repair bills climb. The smart owner asks: *"At what age does selling the old car and buying a new one cost less per year than continuing to repair?"* That breakeven age is the **optimal replacement age**.

For complete-failure assets (like a fluorescent tube), the dilemma is: replace only the failed tube individually, or replace the whole set in bulk at scheduled intervals to avoid the chaos of frequent individual failures.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Critical Path: Forward & Backward Pass Logic

The CPM is solved in two systematic passes through the network:

**Forward Pass (Calculates $E_j$ — Earliest Event Times):**
- $E_1 = 0$ (Start node is always zero).
- For every other event $j$:
$$
E_j = \max_{i \in P_j}\{E_i + t_{ij}\}
$$
- The project duration is $E_n$ where $n$ is the terminal event.

**Backward Pass (Calculates $L_j$ — Latest Event Times):**
- $L_n = E_n$ (Project must finish at $E_n$).
- For every other event $i$:
$$
L_i = \min_{j \in S_i}\{L_j - t_{ij}\}
$$

**Critical Path Identification:**
- An activity $(i,j)$ is **critical** if and only if:
$$
E_i + t_{ij} = E_j \quad \text{AND} \quad L_j - t_{ij} = L_i
$$
- Equivalently, $L_j - E_j = 0$ and $L_i - E_i = 0$.

**Total Float of Activity $(i,j)$:**
$$
TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}
$$

**Free Float of Activity $(i,j)$:**
$$
FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}
$$

**Why This Matters (Engineering Utility):**
In construction, aerospace, and software deployment, CPM helps managers allocate buffers, schedule subcontractors, and forecast delivery dates with mathematical certainty — a cornerstone of PRINCE2 and PMBOK methodologies.

---

### 2.2 Crashing: The Decision Framework

**Step-by-Step Logic for Minimum-Cost Crashing:**

1. Compute the **normal project duration** and **normal cost**.
2. Identify the **critical path**.
3. Calculate **cost slopes** for all critical activities:
$$
\text{Slope} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta T} = \frac{C_{\text{crash}} - C_{\text{normal}}}{T_{\text{normal}} - T_{\text{crash}}}
$$
4. Among critical activities, crash the one with the **lowest cost slope** (cheapest time-saver).
5. Re-evaluate the network — new critical paths may emerge.
6. Continue until either:
   - The desired duration is reached, OR
   - Further crashing is **economically infeasible** (indirect cost savings < cost slope), OR
   - All activities are fully crashed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** When two paths become critical simultaneously, you MUST crash one activity on EACH path. Crashing only one will not reduce project duration.

**Indirect / Penalty Cost Consideration:**
The **optimal crash duration** is where:
$$
\text{Direct Cost Slope} = \text{Indirect Cost Slope}
$$
Beyond this point, crashing costs more than the penalty for delay.

---

### 2.3 Replacement: Assets That Deteriorate with Time

**Formal Model:**
A machine has a purchase price $C$ (constant) and a maintenance cost $M_n$ in year $n$ of its life, which **increases monotonically** with age. The goal is to find the replacement age $n^*$ that **minimizes the average cost per year**:

$$
\text{Average Annual Cost, } AC(n) = \frac{C + \sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k}{n}
$$

**Optimal Replacement Rule (KTU Standard):**
Replace the machine when:

$$
AC(n) \leq AC(n+1) \quad \text{AND} \quad AC(n-1) \geq AC(n)
$$

That is, choose $n$ such that:

$$
\frac{C + \sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k}{n} \leq \frac{C + \sum_{k=1}^{n+1} M_{n+1}}{n+1}
$$

Rearranging gives the **classical KTU condition**:

$$
M_{n+1} \leq \frac{C + \sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k}{n} + C
$$

A simpler practical check:

$$
M_{n+1} \leq \text{Average cost up to year } n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Exams:** Replace when the maintenance cost of the next year **exceeds or equals** the average cost of owning through year $n$.

---

### 2.4 Replacement: Assets That Fail Completely

**Two Strategies:**

**Strategy A — Individual Replacement (Run-to-Failure):**
Replace the item only when it fails. Each failure incurs:
- Cost of new item
- Cost of **downtime** (often dominant)
- Possible collateral damage

Expected cost per period = Probability of failure × Replacement cost

**Strategy B — Group Replacement:**
Replace all items in a batch at **regular intervals of $T$ periods**, regardless of individual condition. This minimizes downtime (replacement done during planned shutdowns) but may waste some items that are still functional.

**Decision Rule (KTU Formula):**
- Let $P$ = cost of individual replacement (item + labor per failure)
- Let $Q$ = cost of group replacement (item only, no per-failure labor)
- Find the interval $T$ that **minimizes total expected cost per period**:
$$
E(T) = \frac{Q}{T} + \frac{P}{T} \sum_{n=1}^{T-1} p_n \cdot n
$$
where $p_n$ = probability of failure at age $n$.

Often KTU problems give a failure-frequency table, and the optimal $T$ is found by direct comparison.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Earliest Event Time | $E_j = \max\{E_i + t_{ij}\}$ | Days / Hours |
| Latest Event Time | $L_i = \min\{L_j - t_{ij}\}$ | Days / Hours |
| Total Float | $TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$ | Days / Hours |
| Free Float | $FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$ | Days / Hours |
| Cost Slope | $\text{Slope} = (C_c - C_n)/(T_n - T_c)$ | ₹/day, $/hr |
| Avg. Annual Cost (Deterioration) | $AC(n) = (C + \sum M_k)/n$ | Currency/year |
| Optimal Replacement Rule | $M_{n+1} \leq AC(n) + C$ | Comparison rule |
| Group Replacement Cost | $E(T) = Q/T + (P/T) \sum n \cdot p_n$ | Currency/period |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Critical Path Calculation

**Problem:** A small project has activities with the following relationships:

| Activity | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Predecessor | — | — | A | A | B | C, D | E, F |
| Duration (days) | 4 | 6 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 4 | 5 |

**Step 1: Construct the Network Logic (Nodes 1 to 7).**

We assign events:
- Event 1 = Start
- A: 1 → 2 (duration 4)
- B: 1 → 3 (duration 6)
- C: 2 → 4 (duration 5)
- D: 2 → 5 (duration 3)
- E: 3 → 6 (duration 7)
- F: 4, 5 → 6 (duration 4)
- G: 6 → 7 (duration 5)

**Step 2: Forward Pass (Computing $E_j$):**

$E_1 = 0$ (Start event has zero time).

For event 2 (predecessor A from event 1):
$$
E_2 = E_1 + t_{12} = 0 + 4 = 4
$$

For event 3 (predecessor B from event 1):
$$
E_3 = E_1 + t_{13} = 0 + 6 = 6
$$

For event 4 (predecessor C from event 2):
$$
E_4 = E_2 + t_{24} = 4 + 5 = 9
$$

For event 5 (predecessor D from event 2):
$$
E_5 = E_2 + t_{25} = 4 + 3 = 7
$$

For event 6 (predecessors E from 3, and F from 4, 5):
$$
E_6 = \max\{E_3 + t_{36},\; E_4 + t_{46},\; E_5 + t_{56}\}
$$
$$
E_6 = \max\{6 + 7,\; 9 + 4,\; 7 + 4\} = \max\{13,\; 13,\; 11\} = 13
$$

For event 7 (predecessor G from 6):
$$
E_7 = E_6 + t_{67} = 13 + 5 = 18
$$

**Project Duration = 18 days.**

**Step 3: Backward Pass (Computing $L_j$):**

$L_7 = E_7 = 18$ (Final event must equal project duration).

For event 6 (successor G):
$$
L_6 = L_7 - t_{67} = 18 - 5 = 13
$$

For event 5 (successor F):
$$
L_5 = L_6 - t_{56} = 13 - 4 = 9
$$

For event 4 (successor F):
$$
L_4 = L_6 - t_{46} = 13 - 4 = 9
$$

For event 3 (successor E):
$$
L_3 = L_6 - t_{36} = 13 - 7 = 6
$$

For event 2 (successors C, D):
$$
L_2 = \min\{L_4 - t_{24},\; L_5 - t_{25}\} = \min\{9 - 5,\; 9 - 3\} = \min\{4,\; 6\} = 4
$$

For event 1 (successors A, B):
$$
L_1 = \min\{L_2 - t_{12},\; L_3 - t_{13}\} = \min\{4 - 4,\; 6 - 6\} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Identify Critical Activities (TF = 0):**

| Activity | $E_i + t_{ij}$ | $E_j$ | $L_j - t_{ij}$ | $L_i$ | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (1→2) | 0 + 4 = 4 | 4 | 4 − 4 = 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| B (1→3) | 0 + 6 = 6 | 6 | 6 − 6 = 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| C (2→4) | 4 + 5 = 9 | 9 | 9 − 5 = 4 | 4 | ❌ No (TF = 0... wait) |

Recheck: For activity C, $L_4 - t_{24} = 9 - 5 = 4$, and $L_2 = 4$. So $L_i = 4$, and $E_i = 4$. Condition for critical: $E_j - E_i = t_{ij}$. Here $9 - 4 = 5$ ✓. And $L_j - L_i = t_{ij}$. Here $9 - 4 = 5$ ✓. **C is also critical!**

Let me re-evaluate the table systematically:

| Activity | $i \to j$ | $t_{ij}$ | $E_i$ | $E_j$ | $L_i$ | $L_j$ | TF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1→2 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | ✅ |
| B | 1→3 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | ✅ |
| C | 2→4 | 5 | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | 0 | ✅ |
| D | 2→5 | 3 | 4 | 7 | 4 | 9 | 2 | ❌ |
| E | 3→6 | 7 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | 0 | ✅ |
| F | 4→6 (and 5→6) | 4 | 9/7 | 13 | 9/9 | 13 | 0/2 | ✅ via 4→6 |
| G | 6→7 | 5 | 13 | 18 | 13 | 18 | 0 | ✅ |

**Critical Paths:**
- **Path 1:** A → C → F → G (1→2→4→6→7) = 4+5+4+5 = **18 days** ✓
- **Path 2:** B → E → G (1→3→6→7) = 6+7+5 = **18 days** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two parallel critical paths exist.** This is a classic KTU scenario — any further crashing must address *both* paths.

---

### 3.2 Crashing — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given Data (Extension of above example):**

| Activity | Normal Time | Crash Time | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | 2 | 1,000 | 1,800 |
| B | 6 | 4 | 1,500 | 2,500 |
| C | 5 | 3 | 800 | 1,600 |
| E | 7 | 5 | 2,000 | 3,600 |
| G | 5 | 3 | 1,200 | 2,000 |

Indirect cost = ₹100/day.

**Step 1: Cost Slope Calculation.**

$$
\text{Slope}_A = \frac{1800 - 1000}{4 - 2} = \frac{800}{2} = ₹400/\text{day}
$$

$$
\text{Slope}_B = \frac{2500 - 1500}{6 - 4} = \frac{1000}{2} = ₹500/\text{day}
$$

$$
\text{Slope}_C = \frac{1600 - 800}{5 - 3} = \frac{800}{2} = ₹400/\text{day}
$$

$$
\text{Slope}_E = \frac{3600 - 2000}{7 - 5} = \frac{1600}{2} = ₹800/\text{day}
$$

$$
\text{Slope}_G = \frac{2000 - 1200}{5 - 3} = \frac{800}{2} = ₹400/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2: First Crash Iteration.**

Both critical paths must be reduced. The cheapest option: pick the lowest slope activity on each path.
- Path 1 (A-C-F-G): cheapest is A, C, or G (all ₹400).
- Path 2 (B-E-G): cheapest is G (₹400) — but G is shared!

Since G is on BOTH paths, crashing G by 1 day reduces both paths simultaneously at cost = ₹400 (single crash).

**Crash G by 1 day:** New duration = 17 days. Cost = ₹400.

**Step 3: Re-evaluate.**

After crashing G, G is now 4 days. Both paths still 17 days. Now to crash further, we need to hit both paths differently. Crash one activity on each path:
- Path 1: cheapest = A or C (₹400)
- Path 2: cheapest = B (₹500) — E is ₹800

**Option:** Crash A (Path 1) and B (Path 2) simultaneously = ₹400 + ₹500 = **₹900/day**.

Indirect saving = ₹100/day. Net additional cost = ₹900 − ₹100 = **₹800/day**. Economically infeasible *if we only consider direct vs. indirect*. But many KTU problems ignore indirect cost and ask for pure minimum-cost crash.

**Step 4: Continue crashing until the project is fully crashed.**

This iterative process is the heart of KTU crashing problems.

---

### 3.3 Replacement — Deterioration with Time (Full Derivation)

**Problem:** A machine costs ₹10,000 new. Maintenance costs (in ₹) for years 1 to 6 are:

| Year $n$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M_n$ (₹) | 1,000 | 1,200 | 1,400 | 1,800 | 2,300 | 2,900 |

**Step 1: Compute Average Annual Cost for each candidate $n$.**

For $n=1$:
$$
AC(1) = \frac{10000 + 1000}{1} = ₹11,000
$$

For $n=2$:
$$
AC(2) = \frac{10000 + 1000 + 1200}{2} = \frac{12200}{2} = ₹6,100
$$

For $n=3$:
$$
AC(3) = \frac{10000 + 1000 + 1200 + 1400}{3} = \frac{13600}{3} = ₹4,533.33
$$

For $n=4$:
$$
AC(4) = \frac{10000 + 1000 + 1200 + 1400 + 1800}{4} = \frac{15400}{4} = ₹3,850
$$

For $n=5$:
$$
AC(5) = \frac{10000 + 1000 + 1200 + 1400 + 1800 + 2300}{5} = \frac{17700}{5} = ₹3,540
$$

For $n=6$:
$$
AC(6) = \frac{10000 + 1000 + 1200 + 1400 + 1800 + 2300 + 2900}{6} = \frac{20600}{6} = ₹3,433.33
$$

**Step 2: Verify with the KTU Optimality Rule.**

Check whether $M_{n+1} \leq AC(n)$:

| $n$ | $AC(n)$ | $M_{n+1}$ | $M_{n+1} \leq AC(n)$? | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 11,000 | 1,200 | ✅ Yes | Continue to year 2 |
| 2 | 6,100 | 1,400 | ✅ Yes | Continue to year 3 |
| 3 | 4,533.33 | 1,800 | ✅ Yes | Continue to year 4 |
| 4 | 3,850 | 2,300 | ✅ Yes | Continue to year 5 |
| 5 | 3,540 | 2,900 | ✅ Yes | Continue to year 6 |

**Conclusion:** Since $AC$ is still decreasing, replace the machine **after 6 years** (or whenever next year's maintenance exceeds $AC(n)$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This monotonic-decrease pattern is typical of KTU problems. The optimal replacement age is where the curve bottoms out — sometimes the answer is "the data given does not warrant replacement within the planning horizon."

---

### 3.4 Replacement — Complete Failure (Group vs. Individual)

**Problem:** A factory has 1,000 light bulbs. Failure pattern observed:

| Week $n$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Failures $f_n$ | 5 | 15 | 35 | 70 | 110 | 175 | 240 |
| Survival at start | 1000 | 995 | 980 | 945 | 875 | 765 | 590 |

Cost of individual replacement: ₹100 (bulb ₹40 + labor ₹60).
Cost of group replacement: ₹40 per bulb (no per-bulb labor).

**Step 1: Cost of Pure Individual Replacement (No Group Plan).**

Total bulbs replaced per cycle of 7 weeks = 5+15+35+70+110+175+240 = **650 bulbs** (cumulative).

Wait, this is for the *original* 1,000. By week 7, only 590 remain. So 410 have failed over 7 weeks. Cost = 410 × ₹100 = **₹41,000** for 7 weeks.

Rate = ₹41,000 / 7 ≈ **₹5,857/week**.

**Step 2: Cost of Group Replacement Every 7 Weeks.**

Replace all 1,000 bulbs at week 7 (or end of cycle): cost = 1,000 × ₹40 = ₹40,000. Plus failures during 7 weeks: 410 × ₹100 = ₹41,000.

Total = ₹81,000 over 7 weeks ≈ **₹11,571/week**. Worse!

But the trade-off depends on the schedule. KTU problems typically ask for the *optimal* interval $T$.

**Step 3: Group Replacement Every 3 Weeks (Try $T=3$):**

Replace 1,000 at week 3, 6, 9, ... Failures over 3 weeks: 5+15+35 = 55 bulbs (cost ₹100 each) + 1000 × ₹40 group cost.

Per cycle: 55 × 100 + 1000 × 40 = 5,500 + 40,000 = **₹45,500 per 3 weeks** ≈ **₹15,167/week**. Worse.

This is illustrative — the *optimal* $T$ in such problems is found by direct comparison or the formula in Section 2.4.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CPM Network — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow of the Critical Path Method** — how activities, predecessors, and successors interact to determine the critical chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Event E1 equals 0] --> A1[Activity A: 1 to 2]
    A0 --> A2[Activity B: 1 to 3]
    A1 --> A3[Event 2 Calculated]
    A1 --> A4[Activity C: 2 to 4]
    A1 --> A5[Activity D: 2 to 5]
    A2 --> A6[Event 3 Calculated]
    A2 --> A7[Activity E: 3 to 6]
    A3 --> A8[Event 4 Calculated]
    A4 --> A9[Event 5 Calculated]
    A5 --> A9
    A6 --> A10[Event 6 Calculated]
    A7 --> A10
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Activity G: 6 to 7]
    A11 --> A12[End Event E7 equals 18]
    A12 --> A13[Forward Pass Complete]
    A13 --> A14[Backward Pass L values]
    A14 --> A15[Compute Total Float TF]
    A15 --> A16{TF equals 0?}
    A16 -->|Yes| A17[Mark as Critical]
    A16 -->|No| A18[Activity has Slack]
    A17 --> A19[Identify Critical Path]
    A18 --> A19
```

---

### 4.2 Project Crashing — Decision Flow Architecture

This block diagram maps the **iterative decision logic** of minimum-cost crashing as used in KTU board problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Construct Network and Compute Normal Duration] --> B1[Identify Critical Path]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Cost Slope for all Critical Activities]
    B2 --> B3[Select Activity with Minimum Cost Slope]
    B3 --> B4[Crash Selected Activity by 1 Unit]
    B4 --> B5[Re-evaluate Network for New Critical Paths]
    B5 --> B6{Multiple Critical Paths Emerged?}
    B6 -->|Yes| B7[Crash One Activity on EACH Path]
    B6 -->|No| B3
    B7 --> B5
    B5 --> B8{Target Duration Reached?}
    B8 -->|No| B2
    B8 -->|Yes| B9[Compute Total Crashed Cost]
    B9 --> B10[Compare with Indirect Cost Savings]
    B10 --> B11[Determine Optimal Crash Duration]
```

---

### 4.3 Replacement Strategy Flow — Deterioration with Time

This flowchart visualizes the **decision rule for deteriorating assets** using the KTU average-cost method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Acquire Machine at Cost C] --> C1[Use for Year 1]
    C1 --> C2[Pay Maintenance M1]
    C2 --> C3[Compute AC of n equals 1]
    C3 --> C4{M_next less than or equal to AC of n?}
    C4 -->|Yes| C5[Continue to Year n plus 1]
    C4 -->|No| C6[Replace Machine at Year n]
    C5 --> C7[Increment n by 1]
    C7 --> C4
    C6 --> C8[Compute Average Cost of New Cycle]
    C8 --> C0
```

---

### 4.4 Replacement Strategy Flow — Complete Failure

This diagram differentiates the **two competing strategies** for assets that fail suddenly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Asset Population of N units] --> D1{Choose Strategy}
    D1 -->|A| D2[Individual Replacement]
    D1 -->|B| D3[Group Replacement at Interval T]
    D2 --> D4[Run until Failure]
    D4 --> D5[Replace only Failed Unit]
    D5 --> D6[Cost equals Item plus Labor]
    D3 --> D7[Replace ALL units at time T]
    D7 --> D8[Cost equals Item only bulk]
    D5 --> D9[Compare Total Expected Cost per Period]
    D8 --> D9
    D9 --> D10[Select Strategy with Minimum Expected Cost]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Critical Path" in a project network. List any two characteristics of critical activities.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A **Critical Path** is the longest path in a project network, connecting the start and end events, whose total duration equals the project completion time. It represents the sequence of activities that determines the minimum time required to complete the project.

**Characteristics (1 Mark — any two):**
1. Critical activities have **zero total float** ($TF = 0$).
2. Any delay in a critical activity causes an **equal delay in project completion**.
3. They are the activities where the **earliest and latest event times are equal** ($E_i = L_i$ and $E_j = L_j$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between "Individual Replacement" and "Group Replacement" strategies for items that fail completely.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Individual Replacement | Group Replacement |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Replace only when a unit fails | Replace all units at fixed intervals |
| Cost per replacement | High (item + labor cost) | Low (item cost only, bulk operation) |
| Best suited for | Items with low failure rate OR high individual cost | Items with predictable, frequent failures |

**Key Trade-off (1 Mark):** Group replacement avoids repeated individual labor charges and emergency downtime, but may waste still-functional items. Individual replacement minimizes wastage but incurs higher labor and downtime costs.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]

**The following data relates to a small construction project:**

| Activity | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Predecessors | — | A | A | B | B | C, D | C | E, F, G |
| Normal Time (days) | 6 | 8 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 4 |
| Normal Cost (₹) | 800 | 1,200 | 600 | 500 | 700 | 900 | 1,100 | 600 |
| Crash Time (days) | 4 | 6 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Crash Cost (₹) | 1,400 | 1,800 | 1,000 | 800 | 1,100 | 1,500 | 1,700 | 1,000 |

The indirect cost is ₹150 per day. The contractor has been penalized ₹500 per day of delay beyond 18 days.

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Draw the network, find the critical path, and project duration. Compute total cost under normal conditions.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Crash the project to its optimal duration and find the corresponding minimum total cost.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Network Construction.**

Event list:
- 1 → 2: A (6)
- 1 → 3: B (8) — wait, B has predecessor A. So 2 → 3: B
- 2 → 4: C (4)
- 2 → 5: D (3)
- 3 → 6: E (5)
- 4, 5 → 6: F (6)
- 4 → 7: G (7)
- 6, 7 → 8: H (4)

**Step 2: Forward Pass.**

$E_1 = 0$
$E_2 = 0 + 6 = 6$
$E_3 = 6 + 8 = 14$
$E_4 = 6 + 4 = 10$
$E_5 = 6 + 3 = 9$
$E_6 = \max\{E_3 + 5,\; E_4 + 6,\; E_5 + 6\} = \max\{19,\; 16,\; 15\} = 19$
$E_7 = E_4 + 7 = 10 + 7 = 17$
$E_8 = \max\{E_6 + 4,\; E_7 + 4\} = \max\{23,\; 21\} = 23$

**Project Duration = 23 days.**

**Step 3: Backward Pass.**

$L_8 = 23$
$L_7 = 23 - 4 = 19$
$L_6 = 23 - 4 = 19$
$L_5 = 19 - 6 = 13$
$L_4 = \min\{19 - 6,\; 19 - 7\} = \min\{13,\; 12\} = 12$
$L_3 = 19 - 5 = 14$
$L_2 = \min\{14 - 8,\; 12 - 4,\; 12 - 3\} = \min\{6,\; 8,\; 9\} = 6$
$L_1 = 6 - 6 = 0$

**Step 4: Total Float and Critical Path.**

| Activity | $i \to j$ | $t$ | $E_i, L_i$ | $E_j, L_j$ | TF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1→2 | 6 | 0, 0 | 6, 6 | 0 | ✅ |
| B | 2→3 | 8 | 6, 6 | 14, 14 | 0 | ✅ |
| C | 2→4 | 4 | 6, 6 | 10, 12 | 2 | ❌ |
| D | 2→5 | 3 | 6, 6 | 9, 13 | 4 | ❌ |
| E | 3→6 | 5 | 14, 14 | 19, 19 | 0 | ✅ |
| F | 4,5→6 | 6 | 10, 9 | 19, 19 | 3, 4 | ❌ |
| G | 4→7 | 7 | 10, 10 | 17, 19 | 2 | ❌ |
| H | 6,7→8 | 4 | 19, 17 | 23, 23 | 0, 2 | ✅ |

**Critical Path: A → B → E → H (1→2→3→6→8) = 6+8+5+4 = 23 days ✓**

**Total Cost (Normal):**
- Direct Cost = 800+1200+600+500+700+900+1100+600 = **₹6,400**
- Indirect Cost = 23 × 150 = **₹3,450**
- Penalty = (23 − 18) × 500 = **₹2,500**
- **Total Normal Cost = ₹12,350**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): Crashing**

**Cost Slopes:**

| Activity | $t_n - t_c$ | $C_c - C_n$ | Slope (₹/day) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 600 | 300 |
| B | 2 | 600 | 300 |
| C | 2 | 400 | 200 |
| D | 1 | 300 | 300 |
| E | 2 | 400 | 200 |
| F | 2 | 600 | 300 |
| G | 2 | 600 | 300 |
| H | 1 | 400 | 400 |

**Iteration 1:** Critical path is A-B-E-H. Cheapest slope = E (₹200). Crash E by 1 day (allowed max 2). New duration: 22 days. Cost: +₹200.

After E crash: check if new critical path emerges. E was on path A-B-E-H. After crash, all paths must be re-evaluated.

**Iteration 2:** Crash E by another day → 21 days. Cost: +₹200 (total ₹400).

Now E is fully crashed. Critical path still A-B-E-H. Cheapest among A, B, H = A or B (₹300). Crash A by 1 day → 20 days. Cost: +₹300 (total ₹700).

**Iteration 3:** Now check critical paths again. Crash B by 1 day simultaneously with A would be smart, but let's be systematic.

After crashing A by 1 day: A = 5, project = 20 days. Path A-B-E-H: 5+8+5+4 = 22... wait, this doesn't reduce by 1. Let me recheck.

Actually, if we crash A by 1, only the path A-B-E-H is affected. New duration = 20 days. ✓

**Iteration 4:** Crash B by 1 day → 19 days. Cost: +₹300 (total ₹1,000).

**Iteration 5:** Target 18 days. Crash B by another day → 18 days. Cost: +₹300 (total ₹1,300).

Now all critical activities (A, B, E, H) — A has been crashed by 1 (can crash 1 more), B fully crashed, E fully crashed, H not crashed.

Path duration = 5+6+5+4 = 20. We're stuck at 20 with the current approach.

To go below 20, we need to either crash A again or H. Let's crash A by 1 more day → 19 days. Cost: +₹300 (total ₹1,600).

To reach 18: H needs crashing. But H is on path A-B-E-H. If A=4, B=6, E=5, H=4, total = 19. Crash H by 1 → 18 days. Cost: +₹400 (total ₹2,000).

**Final Crashed Project: 18 days, Direct Cost Increase = ₹2,000**

**Total Crashed Cost:**
- Direct = 6,400 + 2,000 = ₹8,400
- Indirect = 18 × 150 = ₹2,700
- Penalty = 0
- **Total = ₹11,100**

**Optimal Decision:** Crashed cost (₹11,100) < Normal cost (₹12,350). **Crash the project to 18 days.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**A machine shop uses a particular lathe whose purchase price is ₹15,000. The maintenance costs for years 1 through 7 are as follows:**

| Year $n$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Maintenance $M_n$ (₹) | 1,500 | 1,800 | 2,200 | 2,800 | 3,500 | 4,400 | 5,500 |

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Determine the optimal age at which the machine should be replaced.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A fleet of 200 bulbs is used in a workshop. The failure pattern is given below. The cost of individual replacement is ₹50 per bulb (bulb cost ₹20 + labor ₹30). Group replacement (replacing all bulbs together) costs only ₹20 per bulb. Determine the optimal replacement strategy.

| Week $n$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cumulative failures (out of 200) | 20 | 50 | 90 | 130 | 160 | 180 | 195 |

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Compute Average Annual Cost:

$AC(n) = \frac{15000 + \sum M_k}{n}$

| $n$ | $\sum M_k$ | Total Cost | $AC(n)$ (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,500 | 16,500 | 16,500.00 |
| 2 | 3,300 | 18,300 | 9,150.00 |
| 3 | 5,500 | 20,500 | 6,833.33 |
| 4 | 8,300 | 23,300 | 5,825.00 |
| 5 | 11,800 | 26,800 | 5,360.00 |
| 6 | 16,200 | 31,200 | 5,200.00 |
| 7 | 21,700 | 36,700 | 5,242.86 |

**Apply Rule:** $M_{n+1} \leq AC(n)$

| $n$ | $AC(n)$ | $M_{n+1}$ | $M_{n+1} \leq AC(n)$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 16,500 | 1,800 | ✅ |
| 2 | 9,150 | 2,200 | ✅ |
| 3 | 6,833.33 | 2,800 | ✅ |
| 4 | 5,825 | 3,500 | ✅ |
| 5 | 5,360 | 4,400 | ✅ |
| 6 | 5,200 | 5,500 | ❌ (5500 > 5200) |

**Optimal Replacement Age: 6 years.**

At $n=6$, $AC(6) = ₹5,200$ — the minimum. After this, $AC$ increases.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Strategy 1: Pure Individual Replacement (7-week cycle).**

Failures per week (incremental):
- Week 1: 20
- Week 2: 30
- Week 3: 40
- Week 4: 40
- Week 5: 30
- Week 6: 20
- Week 7: 15
- Total failures: 195 over 7 weeks

Cost = 195 × ₹50 = ₹9,750 per 7 weeks
Rate = ₹9,750 / 7 ≈ **₹1,392.86/week**

**Strategy 2: Group Replacement at $T=7$ weeks.**

- 195 individual failures: 195 × ₹50 = ₹9,750
- 200 bulbs replaced as group at week 7: 200 × ₹20 = ₹4,000
- Total: ₹13,750 per 7 weeks
- Rate = ₹13,750 / 7 ≈ **₹1,964.29/week**

**Strategy 3: Group Replacement at $T=4$ weeks.**

- Failures in 4 weeks: 130
- Individual cost: 130 × ₹50 = ₹6,500
- Group cost: 200 × ₹20 = ₹4,000
- Total: ₹10,500 per 4 weeks
- Rate = ₹10,500 / 4 = **₹2,625/week** — worse.

**Strategy 4: Group Replacement at $T=2$ weeks.**

- Failures: 50
- Individual: 50 × ₹50 = ₹2,500
- Group: 200 × ₹20 = ₹4,000
- Total: ₹6,500 per 2 weeks
- Rate = ₹6,500 / 2 = **₹3,250/week** — worst.

**Conclusion:** **Pure Individual Replacement** at cost ₹1,392.86/week is the optimal strategy in this case. The high individual labor cost is offset by avoiding the bulk replacement of still-functional bulbs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to re-evaluate critical paths after each crash.** Always recompute $E_j$ values after every iteration (2 marks lost per occurrence).
> 2. **Confusing Free Float with Total Float.** Free Float = $E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$; Total Float = $L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$ (1 mark lost).
> 3. **In replacement problems, using $M_{n+1} \geq AC(n)$ instead of $\leq$.** The condition is **$M_{n+1} \leq AC(n)$ to CONTINUE**, otherwise REPLACE (2 marks lost — very common).
> 4. **Failing to handle multiple critical paths in crashing.** Crashing one activity on only one of two critical paths leaves the project duration unchanged — the activity is wasted (1–2 marks lost).
> 5. **Forgetting to add the purchase price $C$ in the numerator of $AC(n)$.** Maintenance cost alone is insufficient; total cost includes capital recovery.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CPM Critical Path:** Longest path; activities with $TF = 0$; any delay = project delay.
- **Forward Pass:** $E_j = \max\{E_i + t_{ij}\}$; start at $E_1 = 0$.
- **Backward Pass:** $L_i = \min\{L_j - t_{ij}\}$; end at $L_n = E_n$.
- **Total Float:** $TF = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$. Free Float: $FF = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$.
- **Cost Slope:** $\text{Slope} = (C_c - C_n)/(T_n - T_c)$. Units: ₹/day.
- **Crashing Rule:** Pick lowest cost slope on the critical path; if multiple critical paths, hit each.
- **Optimal Crash:** Stop when direct cost slope = indirect cost slope (savings) — or when project can't be crashed further.
- **Deterioration Replacement:** Compute $AC(n) = (C + \sum M_k)/n$. Replace when $M_{n+1} > AC(n)$ — the first $n$ where average cost rises.
- **Complete Failure Replacement:** Compare per-period cost of **individual** vs. **group** strategy. Choose minimum.
- **Common KTU Traps:** Slack ≠ 0 activity can't be crashed to reduce project time; group replacement wastes functional items; forgetting to recompute E and L after each crash iteration.

---
